words.text: 1. Who created the world?
a. In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
2. How did God create the world?
a. By his Word and Spirit, God created the world and everything in it in six days.
3. What did God make on the first day?
a. God said, “Let there be light,” and there was light.
4. What did God create on the second day?
a. God created the firmament.
5. What did God create on the third day?
a. God created the sea, the dry land, and all the plants and trees.
6. What did God create on the fourth day?
a. God created the sun to rule the day, the moon to rule the night, and all the stars of heaven.
7. What did God create on the fifth day?
a. God created all the fish of the sea and all the birds.
8. What did God create on the sixth day?
a. God created all the beasts of the field and every kind of animal to walk on the earth.
9. What did God say after he had created all these things?
a. God said that it all was very good.
10. Why did God create all things?
a. God created all things for Jesus Christ and his elect church.
Memory verse: “God saw every thing that he had made, and behold, it was very good.”— Genesis 1:31
Genesis 1; Colossians 1:16–18; Hebrews 11:3
Lesson 1: The Creation of the World
1. How did God create Adam?
a. God formed Adam out of the dust of the ground and breathed into him the breath of life.
2. What did Adam do with the animals, and what did he realize when he did that?
a. Adam named the pairs of animals, and he saw that he was alone.
3. Whom did God create for Adam?
a. God made Adam sleep, and from his rib God created Eve to be his wife.
4. What was special about the creation of Adam and Eve?
a. God created Adam and Eve perfect and in God’s image and likeness.
5. Where did Adam and Eve live?
a. Adam and Eve lived in the garden of Eden that God had planted for them.
6. What did Adam do in the garden?
a. Adam kept the garden and walked with God in the cool of the day.
7. What two trees did God plant in the middle of the garden?
a. God planted the tree of life and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
8. What did the tree of life teach Adam?
a. That tree taught him that God was Adam’s life.
9. What did the tree of the knowledge of good and evil teach Adam?
a. That tree taught Adam that it is good to love and serve his creator.
10. What did God tell Adam about eating of those trees?
a. God said that Adam could eat of the tree of life, but he would surely die the day that he ate of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
Memory verse: “The LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul.”—Genesis 2:7
Genesis 2
Lesson 2: The Creation of Adam and Eve
1. Did Adam and Eve remain perfect?
a. No, Adam and Eve disobeyed God in the garden.
2. How did Adam and Eve disobey God?
a. They ate of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
3. Who tempted Eve to eat of that tree?
a. Satan, who came into the garden in a serpent.
4. What did Satan say to Eve?
a. Satan lied to Eve and said that she would not die if she ate of that tree.
5. What did Satan promise?
a. Satan promised Adam and Eve that they would be as God.
6. Did Eve listen to Satan?
a. Yes, Eve ate of the tree, and she gave fruit to Adam to eat also.
7. What happened to Adam and Eve after they ate from the tree?
a. Adam and Eve were afraid of God and tried to cover themselves with fig leaves.
8. What did God say to Satan after he had tempted Eve?
a. God promised that the seed of the woman would come to crush Satan’s head.
9. Who is the seed of the woman?
a. The seed of the woman is our savior, Jesus Christ.
10. Why did the fall of Adam happen?
a. The fall happened for the sake of the coming of Jesus Christ.
Memory verse: “I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.”—Genesis 3:15
Genesis 3
Lesson 3: The Fall of Man into Sin
1. Who were the two sons of Adam and Eve?
a. God gave Cain and Abel to Adam and Eve.
2. Who was Abel?
a. Abel was righteous and of the seed of the woman.
3. Who was Cain?
a. Cain was wicked and of the seed of the serpent.
4. How did Abel show that he was righteous?
a. By faith Abel offered a lamb as a sacrifice to God.
5. How did Cain show that he was wicked?
a. Cain showed his wickedness when he offered the fruits of the ground as a sacrifice to God.
6. What did Cain do to Abel?
a. Cain killed his brother Abel in the field.
7. Why did Cain kill Abel?
a. Cain killed Abel because the seed of the serpent always hates the seed of the woman.
8. What did God do to Cain?
a. God put a mark on Cain and told him that he had to wander in the earth.
9. Did God give Adam and Eve another son?
a. Yes, God gave Seth to Adam and Eve.
10. What does the birth of Seth teach?
a. Seth’s birth teaches that the seed of the woman always has the victory over the seed of the serpent.
Memory verse: “By faith Abel offered unto God a more excellent sacrifice than Cain.”— Hebrews 11:4
Genesis 4:1–16, 25; Hebrews 11:4; 1 John 3:11–15
Lesson 4: Cain and Abel
1. What was the world like after Cain and Abel?
a. The wickedness of man was great in the world.
2. What made that wickedness become great so fast?
a. The sons of God married the daughters of men.
3. Give examples of this great wickedness.
a. Lamech and his three sons: Jabal, Jubal, and Tubalcain.
4. What did Lamech do that was so evil?
a. Lamech killed two men and boasted about that to his two wives.
5. What was Jabal known for?
a. Jabal was the father of those who raise cattle and live in tents.
6. What did Jubal invent?
a. Jubal invented music and musical instruments.
7. What did Tubalcain do?
a. Tubalcain taught men to work in iron and brass.
8. Who was God’s prophet against that great wickedness in the world?
a. God’s prophet was Enoch, who walked and talked with God.
9. What did Enoch preach?
a. Enoch preached that God would come in judgment on the ungodly, wicked world.
10. What did the wicked, ungodly people do to Enoch?
a. They searched for Enoch to kill him, but God translated Enoch alive into heaven.
Memory verse: “By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him.”—Hebrews 11:5
Genesis 4:16–24; Genesis 5; Genesis 6:1–8; Hebrews 11:5–6
Lesson 5: The Growth of Wickedness
1. What did God say about the wicked world during the time of Noah?
a. God said that the whole world was full of sin and that he would destroy the world with a flood.
2. What did God promise to Noah?
a. God promised to save Noah and his family and to keep some of every creature alive.
3. Why did God save Noah?
a. God saved Noah because he found grace in the eyes of the Lord.
4. Who were Noah’s sons?
a. Noah’s sons were Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
5. What did God command Noah to do?
a. God commanded Noah to build an ark.
6. What else did Noah do?
a. Noah was a preacher of righteousness against the wicked world.
7. When did the flood happen?
a. The flood happened when Noah was six hundred years old.
8. How long did the flood last?
a. The rain lasted forty days and forty nights, and Noah was in the ark for a year and ten days.
9. What happened in the flood?
a. All men and all animals perished, except those in the ark.
10. What did Noah do after he left the ark?
a. Noah sacrificed in thanksgiving to God.
Memory verse: “Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD.”—Genesis 6:8
Genesis 6:9–22; Genesis 7–8; Hebrews 11:7; 2 Peter 2:5
Lesson 6: Noah
1. What did God promise to Noah after the flood?
a. God promised that he would never again destroy the whole world with water.
2. What else did God tell Noah?
a. God said that he made a covenant with Noah and the whole creation.
3. What did that covenant mean?
a. That covenant meant that God would make the whole creation perfect in Christ.
4. What sign did God give of his covenant?
a. God put a rainbow in the cloud as a token of his covenant.
5. Does the sign of the rainbow mean that God will never again destroy the earth?
a. No, God will destroy the world with fire when Christ comes again and makes all things new.
6. What event made clear that the flood did not destroy sin?
a. Noah planted grapes, made wine, drank the wine, and became drunk.
7. What did Ham do to his drunken father?
a. Ham mocked his faither in his sin.
8. What did Shem and Japheth do?
a. They covered their father with a blanket.
9. What did Noah do later?
a. Noah cursed Canaan, the son of Ham, and blessed Shem and Japheth.
10. What did the blessings of Shem and Japheth mean?
a. The blessings meant that Christ would come from the seed of Shem, and later Japheth’s children would be gathered in Christ.
Memory verse: “I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth.”—Genesis 9:13
Genesis 9; Romans 8:19–22
Lesson 7: God’s Promise After the Flood
1. What was true of all men after the flood?
a. All men spoke the same language.
2. After the flood what did God tell men to do?
a. God told men to be fruitful and multiply and spread over the whole earth.
3. Did men listen to God?
a. No, they tried to stay together by building the city of Babel with a tower to reach heaven.
4. Who was the leader of that wickedness?
a. An antichrist named Nimrod.
5. Who was Nimrod?
a. Nimrod was a mighty hunter and a great boaster in himself.
6. Why was Nimrod and his kingdom bad for God’s church?
a. Nimrod and the people of his kingdom hated God and his people.
7. What did God do?
a. God visited Babel and changed the one language into many languages.
8. Why did God change the one language into many different languages?
a. God did that to spread men throughout the earth and to keep them from making one kingdom.
9. Will the wicked men of this world ever be united again?
a. Yes, at the end of time during the kingdom of the final antichrist.
10. What will God do to the kingdom of the final antichrist?
a. God will destroy it by the coming of Christ and bringing his eternal kingdom.
Memory verse: “Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth.”—Genesis 11:9
Genesis 11; Revelation 13:1–9
Lesson 8: Babel
1. Who was Job?
a. Job was a very rich man from the land of Uz, who was righteous, feared God, and turned away from evil.
2. How many children did Job have?
a. Job had seven sons and three daughters.
3. What did Job do for his children?
a. Job prayed for them that God would forgive all their sins.
4. Who attacked Job?
a. Satan went to heaven and told God the lie that Job only served God because he had made Job rich.
5. What did God allow Satan to do?
a. God gave Satan permission to take all Job’s possessions, but Satan could not kill Job.
6. What did Satan do?
a. Satan killed all Job’s children, stole all his animals, killed his servants, and made Job very sick.
7. What did Job’s wife say?
a. She said that Job should curse God and die.
8. Did Job’s friends comfort him?
a. No, the friends said that Job was a sinner and that God does not bless sinners.
9. What did Job confess in his misery?
a. Job said, “I know that my Redeemer lives.”
10. Why did God through Satan do those things to Job?
a. To show that God controls evil for the good of his people.
Memory verse: “The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.”—Job 1:21
Job 1
Lesson 9: Job
1. Who was Abram?
a. Abram was the son of Terah.
2. Where did Abram live?
a. Abram lived in Ur of the Chaldees.
3. What did God command Abram to do?
a. God told Abram to leave his home and family and go to Canaan.
4. What did God promise Abram?
a. God promised to make Abram a great nation and to bless him.
5. What else did God promise?
a. God promised to give Canaan to Abram’s seed.
6. Of what was Canaan a type, or a picture?
a. Canaan pictured heaven.
7. Who went with Abram to Canaan?
a. Abram’s wife Sarai and Lot, his nephew, went with Abram.
8. How did Abram live in Canaan?
a. Abram lived in tents as a pilgrim and a stranger.
9. What did it mean that Abram was a pilgrim and a stranger?
a. It meant that Abram lived in the hope of a heavenly city, and the earthly was not his home.
10. What did Abram do when he came into Canaan?
a. Abram built an altar and worshiped God.
Memory verse: “Now they desire a better country, that is, an heavenly: wherefore God is not ashamed to be called their God: for he hath prepared for them a city.”—Hebrews 11:16
Genesis 11:27–12:9; Hebrews 11:8–16
Lesson 10: The Call of Abraham
1. What did Abram do when there was a famine in Canaan?
a. Abram went to live for a time in Egypt.
2. What did Abram tell Pharaoh about Sarai, Abram’s wife?
a. Abram lied and said that Sarai was his sister.
3. What did Pharaoh do?
a. Pharaoh took Sarai into his house and gave Abram great riches.
4. What did God do to Pharaoh and his household because he had taken Sarai?
a. God plagued Pharaoh and his household.
5. What was true of Abram and Lot after they returned from Egypt?
a. They were both very rich in cattle, and there was not enough land for all their cattle to feed together.
6. What happened between the herdsmen of Abram and Lot because there was not enough land for all the cattle?
a. There was strife between the herdsmen.
7. To solve the strife, what did Abram suggest to Lot?
a. Abram suggested that he and Lot separate.
8. What did Abram tell Lot before they separated?
a. Abram said that Lot could choose where he wanted to go, and Abram would go in the other direction.
9. Where did Lot choose to go?
a. Lot saw that the land near wicked Sodom was very good, and he pitched his tent toward Sodom.
10. How did God comfort Abram?
a. God promised to give the whole land to Abram’s seed.
Memory verse: “All the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever.”— Genesis 13:15
Genesis 13
Lesson 11: Strife Between Abram and Lot
1. What happened in Canaan after Abram and Lot separated?
a. Kings from the East attacked the king of Sodom and his allies.
2. What happened in that battle?
a. The kings from the East defeated the king of Sodom and his allies and captured all their goods.
3. Who else was taken captive?
a. Lot and all his goods were taken captive.
4. What did Abram do when he heard that Lot was taken?
a. Abram armed 318 of his servants and chased the kings from the East.
5. Did Abram defeat those kings?
a. Yes, Abram attacked the kings at night and rescued Lot, all the people of Sodom, and their goods.
6. Who met Abram after he returned from defeating those kings?
a. Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest of the most high God met Abram.
7. What did Melchizedek do for Abram?
a. Melchizedek gave bread and wine to Abram and blessed him.
8. What did Abram give to Melchizedek?
a. Abram gave tithes of all his possessions to Melchizedek?
9. Why did Abram give Melchizedek tithes?
a. Abram gave tithes to show that he honored Melchizedek as God’s king- priest.
10. Of whom was Melchizedek a type, or picture?
a. Melchizedek was a type of Jesus Christ, our king-priest.
Memory verse: “The LORD hath sworn, and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek.”—Psalm 110:4
Genesis 14; Hebrews 6:20–7:10
Lesson 12: The Slaughter of the Kings
1. What did God promise to Abram about his seed?
a. God promised that Abram’s seed would be as many as the stars of heaven.
2. Did Abram have any children at that time?
a. No, but God had promised to give a son to Abram.
3. Did Abram believe God?
a. Yes, Abram believed God, and God counted Abram’s faith for righteousness.
4. What does faith for righteousness mean?
a. It means that God justifies the ungodly.
5. What does it mean that God justifies the ungodly?
a. It means that we are sinners and that God forgives all our sins for the sake of Jesus Christ.
6. What else does justify the ungodly mean?
a. It means that God gives us, the ungodly, eternal life.
7. What did God did tell Abram to do at that time?
a. Abram had to divide a cow, goat, and ram and take two birds and put them all in a row.
8. What else did God tell Abram that night?
a. God told Abram that his children would be slaves in Egypt and that God would deliver them from Egypt and give the land of Canaan to them.
9. What did God show Abram that same night?
a. God showed Abram a vision of a smoking furnace and a burning lamp that passed through the row of divided animals.
10. What did that vision mean?
a. The vision meant that God alone would fulfill his covenant promise and would give Abram’s seed the land of Canaan.
Memory verse: “[Abram] believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness.”— Genesis 15:6
Genesis 15; Romans 4:1–5
Lesson 13: Vision of the Smoking Furnace
1. How did Abram and Sarai try to bring the promised seed themselves?
a. Sarai gave her handmaid Hagar to Abram for a wife.
2. Did Abram and Hagar have a son?
a. Yes, and Abram called his name Ishmael.
3. What did Hagar think of Sarai after the birth of Ishmael?
a. Hagar despised Sarai.
4. What did Sarai do to Hagar?
a. Sarai treated Hagar roughly until she ran away.
5. What did God tell Hagar?
a. God told Hagar that he would multiply her seed.
6. What did God tell Abram?
a. God told Abram that he would have a son with Sarai and be a father of many nations.
7. What did God do to Abram and Sarai at that time?
a. God changed their names to Abraham and Sarah.
8. What sign of his covenant did God give to Abraham?
a. God gave the sign of circumcision to Abraham.
9. What is God’s covenant?
a. God’s covenant is God’s friendship with his elect people in Christ.
10. Of what was circumcision a sign?
a. Circumcision was a sign of the righteousness of faith.

Memory verse: “I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee in their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee.”—Genesis 17:7
Genesis 16–17
Lesson 14: Abraham and Ishmael
1. Who came to visit Abraham?
a. The angel of the Lord with two other angels came to visit.
2. Why did those angels come to Abraham?
a. The angels came to tell Abraham that Sarah surely would have a son.
3. What did Sarah do when she heard that?
a. Sarah laughed in her heart.
4. What else did the angels tell Abraham?
a. The angels told Abraham that God would destroy the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah.
5. What did Abraham ask God?
a. Abraham asked, “Will God destroy the righteous with the wicked?”
6. Who was “the righteous” that Abraham was thinking of?
a. Abraham was thinking of his nephew Lot.
7. Why was Abraham thinking of his nephew Lot?
a. Abraham knew that Lot had moved into the wicked city of Sodom.
8. Was Lot happy in Sodom?
a. No, Lot vexed his righteous soul.
9. Did God deliver Lot from Sodom?
a. Yes, two angels led Lot, his wife, and two daughters out of the city.
10. How did God destroy Sodom and Gomorrah?
a. God rained down fire and brimstone on the cities.
11. What happened to Lot’s wife?
a. She looked back at the cities and was turned into a pillar of salt.
Memory verse: “His wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.”— Genesis 19:26
Genesis 18–19
Lesson 15: The Destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah
1. How old were Abraham and Sarah when Isaac was born?
a. Abraham was one hundred years old, and Sarah was ninety years old.
2. What does Sarah’s old age show about the birth of Isaac?
a. It showed that the birth of Isaac was a wonder of God’s grace.
3. What did Abraham do to Isaac after he was born?
a. Abraham circumcised Isaac as a sign of the covenant of grace.
4. What did Sarah see Ishmael doing to Isaac?
a. Sarah saw Ishmael persecuting Isaac.
5. What did Sarah tell Abraham to do about that?
a. Sarah told Abraham to send Hagar and Ishmael away.
6. Why did Sarah say that?
a. Sarah said that because Ishmael could not inherit the promise of God with Isaac.
7. What did God tell Abraham about Ishmael?
a. God said that he would make Ishmael a great nation.
8. What happened to Ishmael after he and his mother were sent away?
a. Ishmael was ready to die from thirst.
9. How did God provide for Hagar and Ishmael in the wilderness?
a. God gave a well where Hagar could get water.
10. What did Ishmael do when he grew up?
a. Ishmael became an archer who lived in the wilderness.
Memory verse: “Through faith also Sara herself received strength to conceive seed, and was delivered of a child when she was past age, because she judged him faithful who had promised.”—Hebrews 11:11
Genesis 20–21; Hebrews 11:11–12
Lesson 16: Isaac is Born
1. What great trial did God give to Abraham?
a. God commanded Abraham to sacrifice Isaac.
2. Where was Abraham to sacrifice Isaac?
a. Abraham was to take Isaac to Mount Moriah.
3. Besides Isaac, what did Abraham take for the sacrifice?
a. Abraham took wood, fire, and a knife.
4. As Abraham and Isaac were walking up the mountain, what did Isaac ask his father?
a. Isaac asked, “Where is the lamb for a burnt offering?”
5. What did Abraham do to Isaac?
Abraham bound him, laid him on the altar, and raised his knife to kill
Isaac.
6. What did God say to Abraham?
a. God said, “Abraham, Abraham, do not lay your hand on the child.”
7. What did God provide as a sacrifice in the place of Isaac?
a. God provided a ram that was caught by his horns in a thicket.
8. What did God promise to Abraham?
a. God promised to bless Abraham and to make his seed like the stars of heaven.
9. What did Abraham’s obedience show?
a. Abraham’s obedience showed that he loved God and believed that God was able to raise Isaac from the dead.
10. Who is the true Lamb that was sacrificed in our place.
a. The true Lamb is our Lord Jesus Christ, whom God raised from the dead.
Memory verse: “By faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered up Isaac: and he that had received the promises offered up his only begotten son.”—Hebrews 11:17
Genesis 22; Hebrews 11:17–19
Lesson 17: Abraham Called to Sacrifice Isaac
1. How old was Sarah when she died?
a. Sarah was 127 years old.
2. Where did Abraham bury Sarah?
a. Abraham bought a cave and a field in Canaan.
3. What did that burial place show?
a. The burial place showed that Abraham and Sarah lived in hope of the resurrection.
4. What did Abraham do for Isaac?
a. Abraham sent his servant to find a wife for Isaac.
5. What did Abraham make his servant promise?
a. Abraham made his servant promise that he would not a take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites.
6. Where did the servant go to find a wife?
a. The servant traveled to Abraham’s family in Haran.
7. How did the servant find a wife?
a. He prayed and asked God to show kindness and reveal to him the woman that God had chosen.
8. What sign would show the servant the woman whom God had chosen?
a. The sign was the woman who offered to water the servant’s camels.
9. Whom had God chosen as a wife for Isaac?
a. God had chosen Rebekah.
10. What did Isaac think of Rebekah?
a. Isaac loved Rebekah, and he was comforted after his mother’s death.
Memory verse: “[Rebekah] made haste and let down her pitcher from her shoulder, and said, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also.”—Genesis 24:46
Genesis 23–24
Lesson 18: A Wife for Isaac
1. What children did God give to Isaac and Rebekah?
a. God gave them twin sons, Esau and Jacob.
2. What did Esau and Jacob do in Rebekah’s womb?
a. Esau and Jacob fought together in the womb.
3. Why did the twins fight together?
a. The twins fought because one baby was of the seed of the woman, and the other was of the seed of the serpent.
4. Did God love both babies?
a. No, God loved Jacob and hated Esau.
5. How did Esau show that he despised his birthright?
a. Esau sold the birthright to Jacob for a bowl of pottage.
6. How else did Esau show that he was wicked?
a. Esau married two Canaanite women.
7. What sin did Isaac commit?
a. Isaac was determined to bless Esau.
8. What did Isaac tell Esau to do?
a. Isaac told Esau to hunt a deer and cook venison for his father before he blessed Esau.
9. What did Rebekah do?
a. Rebekah dressed Jacob as Esau to trick Isaac into blessing Jacob.
10. Did Isaac bless Jacob?
a. Yes, Isaac gave the birthright to Jacob.
11. What did Esau do?
a. Esau hated Jacob and sought to kill him.
Memory verse: “As it is written, Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated.”—Romans 9:13
Genesis 25:19–34; Genesis 26:34–27:46; Romans 9:10–13
Lesson 19: Jacob and Esau
1. Why did Jacob flee from Canaan?
a. Jacob fled because Esau wanted to kill Jacob.
2. Where did Jacob go?
a. Jacob went to his Uncle Laban, Rebekah’s brother.
3. What did Isaac command Jacob before he left Canaan?
a. Isaac said, “Do not take a wife from the Canaanites.”
4. Where did Jacob stop at night to rest?
a. Jacob stopped at Bethel and used stones for his pillow.
5. What did Jacob see at Bethel?
a. Jacob saw a vision of a ladder between earth and heaven.
6. What did Jacob see on the ladder?
a. Jacob saw the angels of God going up and down on the ladder.
7. Of what was the ladder in the vision a type, or picture?
a. The ladder was a type of our Lord Jesus Christ.
8. What did God say to Jacob in the vision?
a. God said that he was Jacob’s God who surely would bless Jacob.
9. What did God promise Jacob?
a. God promised to protect Jacob and to bring him again to the land of Canaan.
10. What did Jacob do?
a. Jacob built a pillar of stone and promised to worship God in Bethel when he returned to Canaan.
Memory verse: “Behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land.”—Genesis 28:15
Genesis 27:41–46; Genesis 28
Lesson 20: Jacob Flees to Haran
1. Whom did Jacob meet when he came to his Uncle Laban in Haran?
a. Jacob met Rachel when she came to water her father’s flocks.
2. Did Jacob love Rachel?
a. Yes, and so he could marry Rachel, Jacob promised to serve Laban seven years.
3. What did Laban do on Jacob’s wedding night?
a. Laban tricked Jacob and gave Leah to him instead of Rachel.
4. What excuse did Laban give to Jacob for tricking him?
a. Laban said that in his country the younger daughter could not marry before the older daughter.
5. What did Laban tell Jacob?
a. Laban said that Jacob could serve Laban seven more years for Rachel.
6. Did Jacob love Leah?
a. No, Jacob loved Rachel.
7. What did God do for Leah because she was hated?
a. God gave many children to Leah.
8. What did Rachel do when she had no children?
a. Rachel gave her slave Bilhah to Jacob for a wife.
9. What did Leah do after she stopped having children?
a. Leah gave her slave Zilpah to Jacob for a wife.
10. Did Rachel have any children?
a. Yes, later God gave Joseph to Rachel.
Memory verse: “When the LORD saw that Leah was hated, he opened her womb: but Rachel was barren.”—Genesis 29:31
Genesis 29–30:24
Lesson 21: Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel
1. What did Laban ask Jacob when he wanted to leave Haran?
a. Laban asked Jacob to watch Laban’s flocks for wages.
2. What were those wages?
a. As wages Jacob could have all the speckled, spotted, and brown sheep and goats.
3. Did Laban treat Jacob well?
a. No, Laban changed Jacob’s wages ten times.
4. Was God with Jacob through his trials in Haran?
a. Yes, God caused Jacob, who had received the blessing, to prosper in his family, cattle, and servants.
5. What did Laban’s sons think about Jacob?
a. They said that Jacob stole everything that was their father’s.
6. What did God tell Jacob about this time?
a. God said, “Return to Canaan, and I will be with you.”
7. How did Jacob leave Haran?
a. Jacob left secretly for fear of Laban.
8. Did Laban let Jacob leave?
a. No, Laban and an army chased Jacob.
9. What did God tell Laban in a dream?
a. God told Laban that he could not speak good or bad to Jacob.
10. What was Laban’s excuse for chasing Jacob?
a. Laban said that someone had stolen his idols.
11. What did Jacob tell Laban when he found no idols among Jacob’s things?
a. Jacob told Laban that he had mistreated him and that God had rebuked Laban in his dream.
Memory verse: “The LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another.”—Genesis 31:49
Lesson 22: Jacob Leaves Haran
1. Who met Jacob as he was returning to Canaan?
a. An army of angels met Jacob.
2. What news did Jacob hear?
a. Jacob heard that Esau and four hundred men were coming to meet Jacob.
3. What was Jacob’s reaction to that news?
a. Jacob was greatly afraid.
4. What did Jacob pray to God?
a. Jacob confessed his unworthiness of God’s mercies, and he asked God to deliver him from his brother Esau.
5. What did Jacob do with his family?
a. Jacob divided his family into two groups.
6. Why did Jacob divide his family?
a. Jacob divided his family so that the second group could escape if Esau attacked the first group.
7. What did Jacob send to Esau?
a. Jacob sent a large present of animals to Esau.
8. What did Jacob do that night at Peniel?
a. Jacob wrestled all night with a man.
9. What did that man do to Jacob?
a. When the man saw that he was not winning, he touched Jacob’s thigh and made it go out of joint.
10. What did Jacob say to that man?
a. Jacob said, “I will not let you go except you bless me.”
11. Who was that man?
a. The man was our Lord Jesus Christ.
Memory verse: “He had power over the angel, and prevailed: he wept, and made supplication unto him.”—Hosea 12:4
Genesis 32; Hosea 12:3–4
Lesson 23: Jacob Wrestles with God
1. Who was born after Jacob came into Canaan?
a. Benjamin was born to Rachel, but she died while he was being born.
2. Which one of his children did Jacob love more?
a. Jacob loved Joseph.
3. How did Jacob show that he loved Joseph?
a. Jacob made a coat of many colors for Joseph.
4. What did Joseph’s brothers think of him?
a. The brothers hated Joseph and could not speak well to him.
5. What dream did Joseph see?
a. Joseph saw his brothers’ sheaves bow to his sheaf.
6. What did Joseph’s brothers do when he told them the dream?
a. His brothers hated Joseph even more.
7. What other dream did Joseph see?
a. Joseph saw the sun, moon, and eleven stars bow to him.
8. What did Joseph’s brothers plan to do with Joseph?
a. His brothers planned to kill Joseph and throw him into a pit.
9. What did Rueben do?
a. Reuben tried to save Joseph and bring him back to Jacob.
10. What did the brothers do while Reuben was away?
a. The brothers sold Joseph as a slave into Egypt.
11. What did Joseph’s brothers do to cover up their wicked deed?
a. They dipped Joseph’s coat in blood and brought the coat to Jacob so that he would think that a wild animal had killed Joseph.
12. What happened to Joseph in Egypt?
a. Joseph was bought by Potiphar to be his slave.
Memory verse: “He sent a man before them, even Joseph, who was sold for a servant”—Psalm 105:17
Genesis 35:16–20; Genesis 37; Genesis 39:1–6
Lesson 24: Joseph Is Sold into Egypt
1. Did Joseph remain in Potiphar’s house?
a. No, Potiphar’s wife lied about Joseph, and he was put in prison.
2. Whom did Joseph meet in prison?
a. Joseph met Pharaoh’s butler and baker.
3. Why were the butler and baker in prison?
a. The butler and baker had angered Pharaoh.
4. What did Joseph do for the butler and the baker?
a. Joseph told them the meanings of their dreams.
5. What happened to the butler and the baker?
a. Pharaoh restored the butler to his job and killed the baker, just as Joseph had said.
6. Did the butler tell Pharaoh about Joseph and that he was in prison?
a. No, the butler forgot about Joseph.
7. When did the butler remember Joseph?
a. The butler remembered Joseph when Pharaoh had two dreams and no one could tell the meaning.
8. What did Joseph say was the meaning of Pharaoh’s dreams.
a. Joseph said that there would be seven years of plenty and then seven years of famine.
9. What did Pharaoh do to Joseph?
a. Pharaoh made Joseph ruler over all Egypt.
10. What did Joseph do as ruler of all Egypt?
a. Joseph prepared for the seven years of famine by storing corn from the years of plenty.
Memory verse: “Pharaoh said unto Joseph, See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt.”— Genesis 41:41
Genesis 39:7–41
Lesson 25: Joseph Becomes the Ruler of Egypt
1. Why did Joseph’s brothers come to Egypt?
a. Joseph’s brothers came to buy corn because of the famine.
2. When the brothers came to Joseph, did they recognize him?
a. No, but Joseph knew his brothers and called them spies.
3. What else did Joseph say to his brothers?
a. Joseph said that they had to bring their youngest brother Benjamin to Joseph when they came again to buy corn.
4. What did Joseph do to his brother Simeon?
a. Joseph put Simeon in prison.
5. What did Joseph do before his brothers left Egypt?
a. Joseph put his brothers’ money in their corn sacks.
6. Did the brothers return for more corn?
a. Yes, and they took Benjamin with them.
7. What did Joseph do for his brothers when they returned?
a. Joseph made them a feast at his house and gave them presents.
8. What did Joseph tell his steward?
a. Joseph told his steward to put Joseph’s silver cup in Benjamin’s sack of corn.
9. Did Joseph reveal himself to his brothers?
a. Yes, and his brothers were all very glad.
10. How did Joseph explain the evil that his brothers had done to him?
a. Joseph said that God planned that evil to keep his church alive.
Memory verse: “As for you, ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good.”—Genesis 50:20
Genesis 42–45:1–15; Genesis 50:20
Lesson 26: Joseph Meets His Brothers
1. What did Joseph do for his family after making himself known to his brothers?
a. Joseph took his whole family into Egypt and gave them the land of Goshen.
2. What did Jacob do when he was about to die?
a. Jacob blessed Joseph’s sons, Ephraim and Manasseh.
3. What displeased Joseph about Jacob’s blessings?
a. Jacob put the younger son, Ephraim, before Manasseh.
4. What else did Jacob do?
a. Jacob blessed all his sons before he died.
5. What was important about Jacob blessing all his sons?
a. Jacob gave his sons the blessing of the God of Abraham and Isaac.
6. What was the meaning of that blessing?
a. The blessing meant that God would fulfill his promise by the wonder of grace.
7. What did Jacob tell Joseph?
a. Jacob said, “Do not bury me in Egypt but bury me in the field that Abraham bought in Canaan.”
8. How long did Joseph live?
a. Joseph lived to be 110 years old.
9. What did Joseph tell the children of Israel before he died.
a. Joseph said that God would surely bring them out of Egypt and into Canaan, the land of promise.
10. What did Joseph tell the children of Israel to do when God delivered them from Egypt?
a. Joseph told them to take his bones with them.
Memory verse: “By faith Jacob, when he was a dying, blessed both the sons of Joseph; and worshipped, leaning upon the top of his staff.”—Hebrews 11:21
Genesis 45:9–28; Genesis 46:1–7, 26–34; Genesis 47–50
Lesson 27: Israel in Egypt
Old Testament History
Grades 1–4
Creation to Israel in Egypt
1. What happened to the Israelites after Joseph died?
a. A Pharaoh who did not know Joseph enslaved the Israelites.
2. Why did Pharaoh enslave the Israelites?
a. The seed of the serpent always hates the seed of the woman.
3. What did Pharaoh do to the Israelites?
a. Pharaoh set taskmasters over them to afflict them with heavy burdens.
4. What happened to the Israelites under that affliction?
a. The more the Egyptians afflicted them, the more the Israelites grew strong and multiplied.
5. Was Pharaoh pleased when Israel multiplied and became strong?
a. No, Pharaoh was angry, and he tried to destroy Israel with hard and cruel bondage.
6. What did Pharaoh command the Hebrew midwives to do?
a. Pharaoh commanded the midwives to save the newborn girls, but to kill the newborn boys.
7. Did the midwives obey Pharaoh?
a. No, the midwives obeyed God rather than men.
8. What did God do for the midwives?
a. God gave children of their own to the midwives.
9. What did Pharaoh command all the people of Israel?
a. He commanded them to throw their newborn sons into the river of Egypt.
10. Did God see the suffering of his people in Egypt?
a. Yes, and God was determined to destroy Pharaoh and the land of Egypt.
11. What does Israel’s bondage in Egypt teach us?
a. We learn that we are enslaved to sin until God delivers us by his grace.
Memory verse: “The LORD said, I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters.”—Exodus 3:7
Exodus 1; Acts 7:17–19
Lesson 1: Israel Enslaved by the Egyptians
1. Whom did God raise up to deliver his people Israel from the bondage of Egypt?
a. God raised up Moses of the tribe of Levi.
2. Did Moses’ parents obey Pharaoh’s command to throw baby boys into the river?
a. No, Moses’ parents were not afraid of the king’s command.
3. What did Moses’ mother do when she could no longer hide Moses?
a. She put Moses in an ark and placed it in the reeds of the river.
4. Who watched to see what would happen to baby Moses?
a. Moses’ sister Miriam watched by the river.
5. What did Miriam do when Pharaoh’s daughter found Moses?
a. Miriam asked Pharaoh’s daughter if she wanted a nurse to care for the baby.
6. To whom did Miriam bring Moses?
a. Miriam brought Moses to his mother, who raised him until he was a young boy.
7. What did Moses’ mother do when he was a young boy?
a. She brought Moses to the house of Pharaoh’s daughter.
8. When Moses became a man, did he stay with Pharaoh’s daughter?
a. No, Moses refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter.
9. Why did Moses decide to leave Egypt?
a. Moses had killed an Egyptian who was beating a Hebrew, and Pharaoh wanted to kill Moses.
10. Of whom was Moses a type?
a. Moses was a type of our mediator, Jesus Christ.
Memory verse: “By faith Moses, when he was come to years, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter.”—Hebrews 11:24
Exodus 2; Acts 7:20–28; Hebrews 11:23
Lesson 2: Moses Is Born
1. Where did Moses go after he left Egypt?
a. Moses went to dwell in Midian with Jethro.
2. What did Moses do in Midian?
a. For forty years Moses worked for Jethro as a shepherd and married Zipporah, his daughter.
3. What did Moses see one day on Mount Sinai?
a. Moses saw a burning bush that was not consumed.
4. Who called to Moses from the bush?
a. The angel of the Lord, who is God.
5. What did God tell Moses?
a. God said that he would deliver his people from Egypt by signs and wonders through Moses.
6. What did God tell Moses when he asked what God’s name is?
a. God said that his name is I AM THAT I AM.
7. What does that name of God mean?
a. I AM THAT I AM means that God is faithful to his covenant promise because he does not change.
8. What signs did God give to Moses to show that God had sent Moses to deliver God’s people?
a. Moses threw his rod on the ground, and the rod became a snake; and Moses put his hand in his robe, and his hand became leprous.
9. What other sign did God give to Moses?
a. When Moses poured water on the ground, the water became blood.
10. Whom did God give to Moses to speak for him?
a. God gave Aaron, Moses’ brother, to him.
11. Why did God want to kill Moses on his way back to Egypt from Midian?
a. God wanted to kill Moses because he had not circumcised his son Gershom.
Memory verse: “God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you.”—Exodus 3:14
Lesson 3: Moses in Midian
1. What did Moses tell Pharaoh?
a. Moses said, “Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go.”
2. What did Pharaoh say?
a. Pharaoh said, “Who is the LORD that I should obey his voice to let Israel go?”
3. What did Pharaoh do to the Israelites?
a. Pharaoh refused to give them straw, but they had to make the same number of bricks.
4. What did Pharaoh do to the leaders of Israel?
a. Pharaoh told his taskmasters to beat Israel’s leaders.
5. What did God say he would do to Pharaoh?
a. God said that he would harden Pharaoh’s heart, so that he would not let the people go.
6. What did God promise Israel?
a. God promised to deliver Israel by signs and great wonders.
7. What sign did Moses and Aaron do before Pharaoh?
a. Aaron cast his rod down, and his rod became a serpent.
8. What happened when the Egyptian magicians did the same?
a. Aaron’s serpent ate up all the magicians’ serpents.
9. What other sign did Moses and Aaron do?
a. Aaron stretched his rod over the waters of Egypt, the waters became blood, and all the fish died.
10. What happened after Pharaoh’s magicians also turned water to blood?
a. God hardened Pharaoh’s heart.
Memory verse: “The scripture saith unto Pharaoh, Even for this same purpose have I raised thee up, that I might shew my power in thee.”—Romans 9:17
Exodus 4:29–7:25
Lesson 4: Moses Confronts Pharaoh
1. What plague did God bring on Egypt after he had turned all the water to blood?
a. God brought frogs into all the land of Egypt.
2. Were the magicians of Egypt able to bring frogs too?
a. Yes, and the magicians made the plague of frogs worse.
3. After Moses prayed to God and he removed the frogs from the land, what did Pharaoh do?
a. Pharaoh hardened his heart and would not let Israel go.
4. After God through Moses brought lice on the land of Egypt, could the magicians bring lice too?
a. No, and the magicians told Pharaoh that the plague of lice was the finger of God on the Egyptians.
5. What was different about the plague of flies?
a. God separated the Israelites and the Egyptians, so that no flies were among the Israelites in Goshen.
6. What plague did God send on all the Egyptians’ cattle?
a. God sent a very grievous disease on the cattle, so that they died.
7. When Moses threw ashes from a furnace of Egypt into the air, what did the ashes become?
a. The ashes became boils over all the men and beasts of Egypt.
8. What was the next plague about which Moses warned Pharaoh?
a. Moses warned that God would send a terrible thunderstorm with hail to break the trees, destroy the crops, and kill whatever was in the fields.
9. During the storm when Pharaoh said that he would let Israel go, did Pharaoh mean what he said?
a. No, Pharaoh lied again to Moses, for God had hardened Pharaoh’s heart in order that God might show his power.
10. When Pharaoh drove Moses and Aaron from his presence, what did Moses do?
a. Moses stretched out his rod and brought locusts that filled the houses of the Egyptians and ate everything left in the land.
11. When Pharaoh hardened his heart yet again after the locusts were gone, what plague did God bring on the Egyptians?
a. God brought a thick darkness that could be felt, but there was light in the houses of the Israelites.
Memory verse: “[God] sent Moses his servant; and Aaron whom he had chosen. They shewed his signs among them, and wonders in the land of Ham.”—Psalm 105:26–27
Exodus 8:1–10:29
Lesson 5: God Plagues Egypt
1. What feast did God give to Israel in Egypt?
a. God gave the feast of passover to Israel.
2. How were the Israelites to celebrate the passover?
a. God commanded them to eat a spotless lamb and to sprinkle the lamb’s blood on their doorposts and lintels.
3. What else did God require in the Israelites’ celebration of the passover?
a. They had to eat the lamb with unleavened bread.
4. Of what was the passover a type?
a. The passover was a type of the deliverance of God’s church by the cross of Jesus Christ.
5. Why did God command Israel to celebrate the passover in Egypt?
a. Because the angel of the Lord would pass through the land to kill all the firstborn of Egypt.
6. How did the passover save the firstborn of the Israelites from being killed?
a. Israel’s firstborn were saved from death when the angel saw the blood on the Israelites’ doorposts.
7. What happened at midnight the day that Israel celebrated the passover?
a. The angel of the Lord entered all the houses and barns of the Egyptians to kill the firstborn of man and beast.
8. What did Pharaoh do?
a. Pharaoh told Moses and Aaron to take the Israelites with their flocks and herds and go and serve their God.
9. What did the Israelites do as they left Egypt?
a. The Israelites spoiled the Egyptians of their gold, silver, clothing, and precious things.
10. What did Moses take with him?
a. Moses took the bones of Joseph, who had promised that God would deliver his people from Egypt.
Memory verse: “Through faith [Moses] kept the passover, and the sprinkling of blood, lest he that destroyed the firstborn should touch them.”—Hebrews 11:28
Exodus 11–12; Hebrews 11:28
Lesson 6: The Death of the Egyptians’ Firstborn
1. How did God guide the Israelites when he took them out of Egypt?
a. God led them by a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night.
2. Who was with Israel in that pillar of cloud and fire?
a. The angel of the Lord was with Israel.
3. Where did God lead the Israelites?
a. God led them to the shore of the Red Sea.
4. What did Pharaoh do when he saw that Israel was trapped by the sea and the wilderness?
a. Pharaoh hardened his heart and chased Israel with his army.
5. What did the Israelites do when they saw Pharaoh’s horses and chariots?
a. They rebelled and accused Moses of trying to kill them.
6. What did Moses tell the Israelites?
a. Moses told them to stand still because God would fight for them.
7. What did the angel of the Lord do?
a. The angel in the pillar of cloud stood between Israel and Egypt and was light to the Israelites and darkness to the Egyptians.
8. What did Moses do?
a. Moses stretched out his hand, and God divided the waters of the Red Sea, and Israel passed through the sea on dry ground.
9. What happened when Pharaoh tried to pursue Israel into the sea?
a. God overthrew Pharaoh and his host in the Red Sea.
10. What does Israel’s deliverance from the bondage of Egypt and through the Red Sea teach us?
a. We learn that we are delivered from our sins through Christ, who is our Red Sea.
Memory Verse: “By faith they passed through the Red sea as by dry land: which the Egyptians assaying to do were drowned.”—Hebrews 11:29
Exodus 14–15:21; Psalm 77:11–20; Psalm 106:7–12; 1 Corinthians 10:1–2; Hebrews 11:29
Lesson 7: Israel Delivered Through the Red Sea
1. What happened three days after Israel had passed through the Red Sea?
a. Israel found that the waters of Marah were bitter and undrinkable.
2. What did the Israelites do?
a. The Israelites murmured against Moses, saying, “What shall we drink?”
3. After God showed Moses a tree, what did Moses do?
a. Moses cast the tree into the waters, and God made them sweet.
4. What did God show by this?
a. God showed that it was his purpose in grace to save and heal his people Israel.
5. What did Israel find at Elim?
a. Israel found a refreshing oasis of seventy palms and water in the desert.
6. What happened when Israel was in the wilderness of Sin?
a. Israel lusted for all the meat and the bread of Egypt.
7. What did those who lusted after the food of Egypt tell Moses?
a. They accused Moses of bringing them into the wilderness to kill them with hunger.
8. What did God send to Israel?
a. God rained manna from heaven and sent quails that covered the camp.
9. What was manna?
a. Manna was bread from heaven and Israel’s perfect food.
10. How did some in Israel sin with the manna?
a. They gathered more manna than they needed for one day, and some tried to gather manna on the Sabbath.
11. Of what was manna a type, or picture?
a. Manna was a type of the body of our Lord Jesus Christ, in which alone there is life.
Memory verse: “[Jesus said,] I am the living bread which came down from heaven: if any man eat of this bread, he shall live for ever.”—John 6:51
Exodus 15:23–Exodus 16
Lesson 8: Marah, Elim, and Manna
1. What was especially grievous about the Israelites’ behavior at Rephidim?
a. The Israelites fought with God and tempted him, saying, “Is the LORD among us or not?”
2. Why was Israel’s temptation of God so grievous?
a. Israel’s temptation of God was unbelief in the gospel, in which God showed all his mighty works.
3. What explains that unbelief?
a. God was not well pleased with many in Israel.
4. What did God tell Moses to do?
a. God told Moses to strike the rock, and out of it would come water.
5. Why did God give water to Israel?
a. God gave water to Israel for the sake of his elect remnant in Israel.
6. Who came out to fight against Israel at Rephidim?
a. The Amalekites attacked the Israelites when they were faint and weary.
7. Whom did Amalek picture?
a. Amalek pictured the hostile world powers that do not fear God and are always enemies of God’s church.
8. How was Amalek defeated?
a. Moses held up the rod of God’s power against Amalek because the battle was the Lord’s.
9. Of whom was Moses a type in Israel’s battle with Amalek?
a. Moses was a type of our Lord Jesus Christ, who will break the nations with a rod of iron.
10. What did God say about Amalek after that battle?
a. God said that he would have war with Amalek forever.
Memory verse: “It came to pass, when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed: and when he let down his hand, Amalek prevailed.”—Exodus 17:11
Exodus 17; Numbers 24:20; Deuteronomy 25:17–19; Psalm 95; Hebrews 3–4
Lesson 9: Massah and Rephidim
1. Where did God bring the Israelites after they left Egypt?
a. God brought the Israelites to Mount Sinai.
2. What did God give to Israel at Mount Sinai?
a. God gave the law to Israel.
3. Why did God give the law to the Israelites?
a. God gave the law to teach that they could not be righteous by their works and that righteousness and salvation are in Christ alone.
4. What did God require Israel to build at Sinai?
a. Israel had to build the tabernacle of God.
5. What did Moses put in the holy of holies?
a. Moses put the ark of God’s covenant in the holy of holies.
6. Who is the true tabernacle of God?
a. The true tabernacle is Jesus Christ and his elect church.
7. What did the tabernacle show Israel?
a. The tabernacle showed that God dwelt with his people graciously through sacrifices.
8. What did those sacrifices teach the Israelites?
a. The sacrifices taught them that God forgave all their sins and took the people into his covenant fellowship for Christ’s sake.
9. What office did God give at that time?
a. God gave the office of the priesthood to the tribe of Levi.
10. What were the priests to do for the people?
a. The priests were to makes sacrifices for the people, teach them the law, and pray for them.
Memory verse: “I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage. Thou shalt have no other gods before me.”—Exodus 20:2–3
Exodus 19–20; and various passages from Exodus 25–27 regarding the ark, tabernacle, and burnt offerings; Galatians 3; Hebrews 12:18–29
Lesson 10: Israel Receives the Law
1. What sin did the Israelites commit at Mount Sinai?
a. While Moses was on Mount Sinai, the people told Aaron to make them gods.
2. What did Aaron say about the golden calf that he had made for Israel?
a. Aaron said that the calf was Israel’s god that had brought Israel out of Egypt.
3. How else did the Israelites sin at Sinai?
a. They ate and drank and rose up to play while they worshiped the golden calf.
4. As Moses was coming down the mountain, what did he do when he saw the Israelites worshiping the golden calf?
a. Moses smashed the two tables of the ten commandments that were in his hands.
5. What did Moses do with the golden calf?
a. Moses burned the calf, ground it to powder, spread it on the water, and made the people drink that water.
6. How did God avenge the glory of his holy name?
a. The sons of Levi went throughout the camp and killed three thousand men, and God plagued the people.
7. What did Moses pray for the people?
a. Moses prayed God to forgive their sins; and if God would not, then to blot out Moses from God’s book instead of God’s people.
8. Of whom was Moses a type with his prayer?
a. Moses was a type of Jesus Christ, our mediator, whom God blotted out and forgave our sins.
9. What did the sins of making and worshiping the golden calf show?
a. The sins showed that the Israelites could not keep the law and that they daily increased their debt of sin.
10. What else did those sins show the Israelites?
a. The sins showed that their salvation from the law and sin could come only through the promised savior, Jesus Christ.
Memory verse: “Wherefore the law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith.”—Galatians 3:24
Exodus 32–33
Lesson 11: Israel Sins at Sinai
1. What became plain as Israel traveled from Sinai toward Canaan?
a. It became plain that Israel was a mixed multitude of Israelites and Egyptians and a rebellious and disobedient people.
2. What did Israel’s mixed multitude say about the manna?
a. The mixed multitude despised the manna.
3. What did that mean?
a. Despising the manna meant despising God, who was among Israel.
4. For what did the mixed multitude lust?
a. The mixed multitude lusted for all the good food of Egypt and wanted meat to eat.
5. Where did this mixed multitude want to go?
a. The mixed multitude wanted to return to Egypt.
6. What did God say to Moses?
a. God said that he would give to Israel more meat than the people could eat.
7. What did God give to Israel?
a. God sent a wind that brought quails that covered the ground around the camp of Israel.
8. What did God do as the Israelites were eating the quails?
a. As soon as they began to eat, God smote the people with a great plague and many died.
9. What did God give to Moses at that time?
a. God gave to Moses seventy elders on whom God put his Spirit so that they could help Moses.
10. What did Moses’ need for elders make plain?
a. That need showed that Moses could not lead Israel alone and that he was not Christ, who has the Spirit without measure.
Memory verse: “[They] lusted exceedingly in the wilderness, and tempted God in the desert.”— Psalm 106:14
Numbers 10:11–12, 33–36; Numbers 11–12; 1 Corinthians 10
Lesson 12: Israel Travels Toward Canaan
1. What did God command Moses to do when Israel came near Canaan?
a. God told Moses to send spies into Canaan.
2. How many spies did Moses send?
a. Moses sent twelve men, one man from each tribe.
3. What did the spies report?
a. The spies showed the beautiful fruit of the land and said that it flowed with milk and honey, but there were giants and many strong cities in Canaan.
4. What happened when the spies said that?
a. The Israelites were angry and accused Moses of trying to kill them with the swords of their enemies.
5. What did the Israelites want to do?
a. They wanted to make a new leader who would bring them back to Egypt.
6. Did any of the spies bring a good report?
a. Yes, Joshua and Caleb brought a good report.
7. What did Joshua and Caleb report?
a. Joshua and Cabel reported that the land was very good and that by the grace of God, Israel would possess Canaan.
8. What did the people say about Joshua and Caleb after that report?
a. The people said that Joshua and Caleb should be killed with stones.
9. How did God punish unbelieving Israel?
a. God said that Israel could not enter Canaan but must wander in the wilderness for forty years.
10. What did God say would happen while Israel wandered in the wilderness for forty years?
a. God said that the unbelieving generation of Israelites would die, and God would give Canaan to their children.
Memory verse: “So we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief.”—Hebrews 3:19
Numbers 13–14; Deuteronomy 1:22–46
Lesson 13: Israel at the Door of Canaan
1. What terrible event happened after Israel was turned back into the wilderness?
a. Korah, Dathan, and Abiram rebelled with 250 princes of the congregation.
2. Why did those men rebel?
a. The men envied Moses and Aaron and wanted to be priests themselves.
3. What was the heart of the sin of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram?
a. They despised Christ as the only way of salvation.
4. How did God answer that rebellion?
a. God told all those who had rebelled to come to the tabernacle with their censors, and God would show them whom he had chosen to be his priest.
5. What happened at the tabernacle?
a. God consumed with fire the 250 princes who had offered incense.
6. In what other way did God deal with their rebellion?
a. The earth opened and swallowed up alive the men, the tents, and all the goods of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.
7. Did the Israelites repent at that sign of God’s displeasure?
a. No, in the morning they accused Moses and Aaron of killing the people of God.
8. What did God do to that rebellious people?
a. God sent a plague that killed many Israelites until Aaron stood with his censor between the living and the dead.
9. What sign did God give to Israel at that time to show that he had chosen Aaron as high priest?
a. God gave the sign of Aaron’s rod that budded, while the eleven rods of the other tribes remained dead.
10. What did that sign of rebuke from God mean?
a. The sign meant that they were dead in their unbelief and that there is life only in God’s anointed Son, Jesus Christ.
11. Did the people repent at that sign?
a. The people did not repent, but they cried in unbelief that they could not come near the tabernacle of God.
Memory verse: “They envied Moses also in the camp, and Aaron the saint of the LORD. The earth opened and swallowed up Dathan and covered the company of Abiram.”—Psalm 106:16–
Numbers 16–17; Numbers 26:9–11; Psalm 106:16–18
Lesson 14: The Rebellion of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram
1. What happened at Meribah in Kadesh?
a. Israel fought with God because there was no water.
2. What did the Israelites say?
a. They accused Moses and Aaron of bringing them out of Egypt to kill them and their children with thirst.
3. What did God tell Moses to do?
a. God told Moses to take his rod and speak to the rock in the presence of the people, and God would give water to them.
4. What did Moses do?
a. Moses said, “Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?” and he hit the rock twice.
5. What did God mean when he said that Moses did not sanctify God before Israel when Moses hit the rock?
a. God meant that Moses in his anger did not show that God is the holy one of his elect Israel and the God who justifies the ungodly.
6. What sin did the Israelites commit as they traveled toward Canaan?
a. They spoke against God and Moses, saying that they had no food and water and they hated the manna.
7. What did God send against Israel?
a. God sent a plague of poisonous serpents, and many in Israel died.
8. What did the fiery serpents picture?
a. The serpents pictured the corruption of sin, the curse, and the wrath of God.
9. What did Moses do for the Israelites?
a. Moses lifted up a brazen serpent on a pole, and all who looked on the serpent were healed.
10. What did the brazen serpent picture?
a. The brazen serpent pictured Christ as he was lifted up on the cross and was made sin and a curse for his people.
Memory verse: “As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up: That whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have eternal life.”—John 3:14–15
Lesson 15: A Wicked and Rebellious Generation
1. As Israel traveled toward Canaan, what did Moses ask the king of Edom?
a. Moses sent messengers to ask if Israel could pass peacefully through the land of Edom.
2. How did Edom answer Moses?
a. Edom said that Israel could not go through its land, and Edom came against Israel with a great army.
3. Why did Edom do that?
a. As reprobates from Esau’s seed, they hated the Israelites and their God.
4. What did God tell Israel about the lands of Moab and Ammon?
a. God said that Israel could not take those lands because God had given them to Lot’s children, the Moabites and the Ammonites.
5. What did Sihon, king of the Amorites, do when Moses asked to pass through his land?
a. a. a. Sihon fought against Israel, but Israel destroyed Sihon and took his land.
6. Who attacked Israel after the battle with Sihon?
7. The giant Og, king of Bashan, attacked Israel.
8. What did Israel do to King Og?
9. Israel destroyed Og, his armies, and all his people until there were none left.
How did God fight for Israel against Sihon and Og?
a. God sent swarms of hornets against Sihon and Og.
What did the tribes of Rueben, Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh ask of
Moses?
a. Those tribes asked that the lands on the east side of the Jordan River be given to them for their inheritance.
10. What did the men of those tribes promise?
a. They promised to fight with the other tribes in the conquest of Canaan.

Memory verse: “Who smote great nations, and slew mighty kings; Sihon king of the Amorites, and Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan: and gave their land for an heritage, an heritage unto Israel his people.”—Psalm 135:10–12
Numbers 20:14–21; Numbers 21:10–35; Numbers 32; Deuteronomy 2–3; Joshua 1; Joshua 21:5-12; Joshua 22
Lesson 16: Edom’s Cruelty and the Destruction of Og and Sihon
1. What did Balak, king of Moab, do when he saw what God had done to the Amorites?
a. Balak hired the false prophet Balaam to curse Israel?
2. What did God tell Balaam?
a. God told Balaam that he could not curse the Israelites because they were God’s blessed people.
3. Why did Balaam go to curse them anyway?
a. Balaam went because he loved the thought of getting rich.
4. How did God rebuke the wickedness of the false prophet Balaam?
a. Balaam’s donkey spoke to him.
5. What did the angel of the Lord tell Balaam on his way to Balak?
a. The angel said that he had stood in the path to kill Balaam and that if the donkey had not stopped, the angel would have killed Balaam.
6. Was Balaam able to curse Israel?
a. No, Balaam tried three times to curse Israel, and each time God made Balaam bless Israel.
7. Why was Balaam unable to curse Israel?
a. Balaam could not curse Israel because God loved his elect Israel and had forgiven all her sins.
8. After Balaam was unable to curse Israel, what did he tell Balak?
a. Balaam prophesied to Balak that a Star would come out of Jacob and smite Moab.
9. Who is that Star?
a. That Star is our Lord Jesus Christ.
10. What happened to Balaam after that?
a. When the Israelites later warred against the Midianites, they found and killed Balaam.
Memory verse: “[God] hath not beheld iniquity in Jacob, neither hath he seen perverseness in Israel: the LORD his God is with him, and the shout of a king is among them.”—Numbers 23:21
Numbers 22–24; Numbers 31:8; Deuteronomy 23:4–5; Joshua 13:8; Joshua 24:9–10; 2 Peter 2:15–16
Lesson 17: The False Prophet Balaam
1. After the false prophet Balaam failed to curse Israel, what did he tell Balak?
a. Balaam told Balak to use the beautiful women of Moab and Midian to trap the men of Israel to worship the idol Baalpeor.
2. Why did Balaam give that advice?
a. Balaam thought that if the Israelites were unfaithful to God, then God would not bless them.
3. What was the meaning of Israel’s sin at Baalpeor?
a. Israel’s sin was idolatry and the doctrine that to sin is good.
4. Did all Israel go after the idol Baalpeor?
a. No, an elect remnant remained faithful to God.
5. What did the elect remnant do?
a. The remnant wept before the tabernacle in sorrow for Israel’s sin.
6. What did Moses tell the judges of Israel to do to the leaders of the sin?
a. Moses told the judges to hang them up before the Lord as a curse.
7. How did Salu, a prince of Simeon, and Cozbi, a princess of Midian, show a terrible hardness against God’s judgment?
a. They lay together in a tent in front of the Israelites who were weeping at the tabernacle.
8. What did God do to Israel?
a. The plague of God killed twenty-three thousand Israelites.
9. How was the plague stopped by Phinehas, the son of Eleazer the priest?
a. Phinehas killed Salu and Cozbi with his spear in their tent.
10. What did God instruct Moses to do to avenge Israel?
a. God said that the Israelites had to kill all the kings and men of Midian.
Memory verse: “Then stood up Phinehas, and executed judgment: and so the plague was stayed.”—Psalm 106:30
Numbers 25; Numbers 31; Deuteronomy 4:3–4; Psalm 106:28–31; Hosea 9:10; Micah 6:5; 1 Corinthians 10:8; Revelation 2:14
Lesson 18: Israel Sins at Baalpeor
1. What did Joshua do before Israel entered Canaan?
a. Joshua sent two men to spy out the city of Jericho.
2. Who hid the spies from the soldiers of Jericho’s king?
a. The harlot Rahab hid the spies.
3. Where did Rahab hide the spies?
a. Rahab hid the spies in the flax on her roof.
4. What did Rahab tell the king’s soldiers?
a. Rahab said that the spies left the city, and the soldiers should chase them.
5. What did Rahab tell Joshua’s two spies?
a. Rahab said that the people in Jericho heard how God had dried up the Red Sea for the Israelites and destroyed Og and Sihon.
6. What did Rahab ask the spies?
a. Rahab asked the spies to save alive her and her parents, brothers, and sisters.
7. What did the spies say to Rahab?
a. The spies said that they would deal kindly and truly with her.
8. How did the spies escape?
a. Rahab let down the spies by a cord through a window in her house on the wall of Jericho.
9. What sign did the spies give to Rahab so that they would know where she and her family were?
a. She had to hang a scarlet cord from her window.
10. Who married Rahab after she joined the nation of Israel?
a. Rahab married Salmon, a man of Judah and an ancestor of King David.
Memory verse: “By faith the harlot Rahab perished not with them that believed not, when she had received the spies with peace.”—Hebrews 11:31
Joshua 2; Matthew 1:5; Hebrews 11:31; James 2:25
Lesson 19: Rahab and Two Spies
1. How did Israel cross the Jordan River into Canaan?
a. God made the waters of the Jordan River stop flowing, so Israel walked on dry ground.
2. What means did God use to stop the river from flowing?
a. The priests carried the ark, and when their feet stepped into the river, the waters stopped flowing.
3. What did God tell twelve men of Israel to do?
a. Each man had to take a stone from the middle of the riverbed.
4. What did Joshua do with the twelve stones?
a. Joshua made a monument for a sign to Israel.
5. How was the monument a sign to Israel?
a. The monument taught that the fulfillment of God’s promise to establish his elect Israel in Canaan was a wonder of grace.
6. What do those twelve stones mean for us now?
a. The stones mean that the fulfillment of God’s promise to us and our children is a wonder of grace alone and not our work.
7. What did Joshua do when Israel entered Canaan?
a. Joshua circumcised all the males.
8. Why was that circumcision necessary?
a. Circumcision was necessary because their unbelieving parents in the wilderness had disobeyed God’s command to circumcise their sons.
9. Whom did Joshua meet as he spied out Jericho?
a. Joshua met the captain of God’s armies.
10. Who was that captain?
a. The captain was the angel of Jehovah, our Lord Jesus Christ.
Memory verse: “O give thanks unto the LORD, for he is good: for his mercy endureth for ever.”— Psalm 107:1
Joshua 3–5
Lesson 20: Israel Crosses the Jordan River
1. What city in Canaan did Israel destroy first?
a. Israel destroyed first the mighty city of Jericho.
2. What did God command Joshua about how to defeat Jericho?
a. Israel had to march around Jericho’s walls once every day for six days.
3. How did Israel march?
a. Some soldiers marched in front of seven priests with trumpets, followed by the ark of the covenant, and some soldiers followed the ark.
4. What did Joshua tell the people?
a. Joshua said that the people could not talk or shout until he had commanded them.
5. What did the priests and soldiers do on the seventh day?
a. They marched around Jericho’s walls seven times.
6. What did Joshua tell the people to do after the seventh time around the walls?
a. Joshua commanded the people to shout when the priests blew the trumpets, because God had given the city to Israel.
7. What did God command Israel to do to Jericho and its inhabitants?
a. God commanded Israel to destroy completely every living thing with swords and to burn the city.
8. What did God command Israel to do with the gold, silver, iron, and brass from Jericho?
a. God commanded Israel to put the gold, silver, iron, and brass in God’s treasury.
9. Was Rahab saved?
a. Yes, the two men who had spied Jericho saved alive Rahab and her family.
10. What did Joshua say about anyone who rebuilt the city of Jericho?
a. Joshua said that anyone who rebuilt Jericho would be cursed.
Memory verse: “By faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were compassed about seven days.”—Hebrews 11:30
Joshua 6; Hebrews 11:30
Lesson 21: Jericho Defeated
1. What town did Israel attack after Jericho?
a. Joshua commanded three thousand men to attack the little town of Ai.
2. What happened in the battle against Ai?
a. Israel fled before the men of Ai, and thirty-six Israelites were killed.
3. How did God explain Israel’s defeat to Joshua?
a. God said that Israel had broken his covenant by taking some of the forbidden things from Jericho.
4. What was so serious about that?
a. The one who had taken those forbidden things brought a curse on the nation of Israel.
5. Whom did God expose as the sinner who had taken the forbidden things?
a. God showed by lot that the sinner was Achan of the tribe of Judah.
6. What did God command Israel to do with Achan?
a. God said that Achan and his family, his animals, and all his possessions were to be stoned, burned, and a pile of stones heaped over them.
7. What did that pile of stones over Achan, his family, and animals picture?
a. The pile of stones pictured the curse of God against all who transgress his law.
8. Why did God say in Hosea that he would make the valley where Achan had been stoned “a door of hope”?
a. God said that because he would send Jesus Christ to take our curse on him at the cross.
9. Did Israel defeat Ai after being defeated the first time?
a. Yes, Joshua stretched his spear out against the city until Israel killed all the people of Ai.
10. What did Joshua’s reading of the blessings and cursing of the law to Israel at Mount Ebal and Mount Gerazim teach the people?
a. The reading of the law taught Israel that they had gained Canaan not by their works but through the righteousness of faith alone.
Memory verse: “Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree.”—Galatians 3:13
Joshua 7–8; 1 Chronicles 2:7; Hosea 2:14–15; Galatians 3:13
Lesson 22: Ai and Achan
1. Who were the Gibeonites?
a. The Gibeonites lived in one of the mighty cities of Canaan.
2. What did the Gibeonites do to Israel?
a. They tricked Israel into promising to be friends.
3. How did the Gibeonites trick Israel?
a. The Gibeonites pretended that they had come to Israel from a far country.
4. How did the Gibeonites explain why they came to seek friendship with Israel?
a. The Gibeonites said that they heard what God had done to Pharaoh and Egypt and to Sihon and Og.
5. How did the Gibeonites convince Israel that they were from a far country?
a. The Gibeonites showed their old clothes, shoes, wine bottles, and moldy bread to Israel.
6. What did Joshua do in response to the Gibeonites’ request for friendship?
a. Joshua and all the princes made peace and a covenant with the Gibeonites to let them live.
7. What did the Israelites do when they found out that they had been fooled because the Gibeonites lived in Canaan?
a. The Israelites murmured against their princes who had made peace with the Gibeonites.
8. What did Joshua do to the Gibeonites?
a. Joshua made them slaves who had to cut wood and draw water for the tabernacle.
9. Why could Israel not kill the Gibeonites?
a. Israel could not kill them because the princes had sworn to the Gibeonites in the name of God to let them live.
10. Why did the Gibeonites trick Israel?
a. The Gibeonites heard what Israel had done to Jericho and Ai and were afraid.
Memory verse: “Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man. Through God we shall do valiantly: for he it is that shall tread down our enemies.”—Psalm 60:11–12
Joshua 9
Lesson 23: The Deception of the Gibeonites
1. What did the five Canaanite kings do when they knew that the Gibeonites had made peace with Israel?
a. The Canaanite kings made war against the Gibeonites.
2. What did the Gibeonites do?
a. They sent messengers to Joshua, asking Israel to come quickly and save them.
3. How did Israel defeat the alliance of the five Canaanite kings?
a. God defeated the kings and slew many Canaanites.
4. How did God kill the Canaanites?
a. God killed many with the sword of Israel, but he killed many more with hailstones from heaven.
5. What did Joshua do as that day was ending, but there was still much more fighting to do?
a. Joshua told the sun and the moon to stand still.
6. Did God hear Joshua?
a. Yes, the sun and moon stood still for a whole day.
7. Of whom was Joshua a type?
a. Joshua was a type of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom God gives all authority in heaven and on earth.
8. What happened to the five Canaanite kings?
a. The kings hid in a cave, but Joshua captured them.
9. What did Joshua tell the captains of Israel’s army to do to the five kings?
a. Joshua told the captains to put their feet on the king’s necks as a sign that God would defeat all Israel’s enemies.
10. What did Israel do to the cities and to those who had come to fight Israel?
a. Israel utterly destroyed their cities, people, and kings as Israel had destroyed Jericho and Ai.
Memory verse: “I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he doth judge and make war.”—Revelation 19:11
Joshua 10; Isaiah 28:21; Habakkuk 3:11–13; Revelation 19:11–21
Lesson 24: The Sun Stands Still
1. What did the Canaanites do after the destruction of the five kings?
a. In order to destroy Israel, the Canaanites and their kings gathered together under Jabin, king of Hazor.
2. Was that a mighty army?
a. Yes, the army was a great host with horses and chariots.
3. Why did the Canaanites come against Israel?
a. God had hardened the hearts of the Canaanites to come against Israel so that God might destroy the Canaanites.
4. What did God tell Joshua about that mighty army?
a. God said, “Fear not, I will deliver them up slain before Israel.”
5. What did God command the Israelites to do to the Canaanites’ horses and chariots?
a. Israel had to hamstring the Canaanites’ horses and burn all their chariots.
6. Why were the Israelites commanded to do that?
a. They had to learn that God was their strength, not horses and chariots.
7. What did Joshua and Israel do to those Canaanite kings and their cities?
a. After fighting the Canaanites for a long time, Joshua and Israel destroyed the kings and their people and took their cattle for spoil.
8. How many kings in all did Joshua destroy?
a. Joshua defeated thirty-one kings of the Canaanites.
9. Whom else did Israel destroy in those wars?
a. Israel destroyed the Anakims, the giants of Canaan, and their cities.
10. What explains Israel’s victory over those mighty kings?
a. God had favor on Israel and fought for her.
Memory verse: “Some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God.”—Psalm 20:7
Joshua 11–12; Psalm 20:7; Psalm 44:1–8; Psalm 135:10–12; Psalm 136:17–26
Lesson 25: The Destruction of the Canaanites
1. What did Joshua do after destroying the Canaanites?
a. Joshua divided the land of Canaan among the tribes, as God had commanded Moses.
2. How was the land of Canaan divided?
a. Joshua divided the land by lot.
3. What did that lot mean?
a. The lot meant that God chose each tribe’s inheritance and graciously gave it to each tribe.
4. What was the significance of that inheritance?
a. An inheritance signified each tribe’s part in the covenant of God and was a picture of our eternal inheritance in heaven.
5. What else was the meaning of Joshua’s giving the land of Canaan to Israel?
a. Joshua gave the Israelites rest, as God had promised.
6. Of what was that rest a type?
a. The rest of Canaan was a type of the spiritual rest of our perfect salvation given to us in Christ.
7. What did God give to the tribe of Levi for its inheritance?
a. God gave to the tribe of Levi several cities and the offerings made in the tabernacle.
8. What did Joshua tell Israel about her inheritance?
a. Joshua said that Israel did not receive her inheritance by her sword, but God fought for Israel.
9. What explains that God gave the land of Canaan to Israel?
a. God was faithful to his covenant promise that he had given to Abraham.
10. About what did Joshua warn the Israelites?
a. Joshua warned them that they must not forsake the Lord their God.
Memory verse: “There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass.”—Joshua 21:45
Joshua 13–21
Lesson 26: The Division of Canaan
1. What did Joshua tell Gad, Reuben, and the half tribe of Manasseh after he had divided the land of Canaan?
a. Joshua told those tribes that they could return to their inheritances that Moses had given to them on the other side of the Jordan River.
2. What did those tribes do when they returned to their lands?
a. Those tribes built a very large altar by the Jordan River.
3. What did the other tribes of Israel do when they heard about that altar?
a. The other tribes gathered at the tabernacle to make war on the three tribes.
4. What did the other tribes of Israel do before going to war?
a. They sent men to investigate what the altar meant.
5. Of what did the other tribes accuse Gad, Reuben, and Manasseh because of their altar?
a. The other tribes said that the three tribes had rebelled against God by building an altar besides the one in the tabernacle.
6. Why was it so serious to have another altar?
a. Another altar was a rejection of Christ as the only way of salvation.
7. How did the men of Gad, Reuben, and the half tribe of Manasseh answer the other tribes of Israel?
a. Gad, Reuben, and Manasseh said that they had built an altar not to sacrifice on but for a witness.
8. Of what was that altar a witness?
a. The altar was a witness that Jehovah is God alone.
9. Why did Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh make that altar?
a. They made the altar as a sign that they and their seed had a part in the promise of God, even though they were on the other side of the Jordan River.
10. Did the other tribes of Israel accept that explanation?
a. Yes, and they stopped their plan to make war on Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh.
Memory verse: “The children of Reuben and the children of Gad called the altar Ed: for it shall be a witness between us that the LORD is God.”—Joshua 22:34
Joshua 22
Lesson 27: The Altar of Witness
Old Testament History
Grades 1–4
Israel Enslaved to The Altar of Witness
1. What did the prophet Malachi make clear about Judah at the end of the old dispensation?
a. Malachi said that Judah had corrupted herself and God’s promise could not come through Judah’s repentance and obedience.
2. What did God tell his elect remnant through Malachi about God’s faithfulness to his promise?
a. God said, “I am the LORD, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.”
3. For whom did Malachi teach the people to look?
a. Malachi taught the people to look for the messenger whom God would send to prepare the way before Jesus.
4. What was the church like during the time between the Old Testament and the New Testament?
a. During that time the people departed from the truth, forgot the house of David, and longed for an earthly kingdom.
5. What two sects arose during the time between the two testaments?
a. The two sects were the Pharisees, who taught the people to trust in their works, and the Sadducees, who taught the people to be worldly.
6. Who was the messenger whom God promised to send?
a. The promised messenger was John the Baptist, who would come in the spirit and power of Elijah.
7. Who were John’s parents?
a. John’s father was the priest Zacharias, and his mother was Elisabeth.
8. How was John’s birth announced?
