a. It showed that those who hate the gospel will always lie against the gospel and those who preach it.
8. When Claudius Lysius let Paul speak to the mob, what did he say?
a. Paul told the mob that he had been raised as a Jew and that he showed his zeal by persecuting the church.
9. Whom did Paul say had appeared to him?
a. Paul said that Christ had appeared to him and sent Paul to the Gentiles.
10. What did the enemies in the mob do when they heard that?
a. They shouted, “Away with this fellow, for he should not live.”
11. Why did those enemies hate Paul’s word so much?
a. They hated Paul’s word because it condemned them as those who rejected Christ as the only way of salvation.
Memory verse: “Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.”—John 14:6
Acts 21:15–Acts 22
Lesson 20: Paul’s Arrest in Jerusalem
1. What did Claudius Lysius do with Paul in order to understand why the Jews hated him so much?
a. Claudius told his soldiers to bind Paul and to examine him by scourging.
2. What did Paul ask the centurion who oversaw the soldiers?
a. Paul asked, “Is it lawful to scourge a man who is Roman and uncondemned?”
3. What did Claudius Lysius do with Paul the next morning?
a. Claudius gathered the Sanhedrin and set Paul in the midst of the chief rulers.
4. When Paul said that he lived in a good conscience before God, what did the high priest command?
a. The high priest commanded that Paul be hit on the mouth.
5. Why did Paul call the high priest a hypocrite?
a. Paul called the high priest a hypocrite because he judged Paul by the law of Moses and commanded that Paul be hit contrary to the law.
6. How did Paul respond after being told that he had called the high priest a hypocrite?
a. Paul said he did not know the man was the high priest, and that it is written in the law that one must not speak evil of the ruler of God’s people.
7. What did Paul’s words show?
a. Paul showed that he understood what the law said and that he honored the law.
8. What did Paul do when he saw that one part of the Sanhedrin was Pharisees and the other part Sadducees?
a. Paul cried out, “I am a Pharisee; and for the hope of the resurrection, I am called into question.”
9. What was the result of Paul’s words in the Sanhedrin?
a. A fight broke out between the Pharisees and Sadducees because the Sadducees denied the resurrection.
10. Of what was Claudius Lysius afraid when the fight broke out?
a. Claudius feared that Paul would be torn to pieces, so he rescued Paul.
11. How did Jesus Christ encourage Paul that night?
a. Jesus came and stood by Paul and said that he should not fear because he would testify of Christ also in Rome.

Memory verse: “Remember that Jesus Christ of the seed of David was raised from the dead according to my gospel: wherein I suffer trouble, as an evil doer, even unto bonds; but the word of God is not bound.”—2 Timothy 2:8–9
Lesson 21: Paul Before the Sanhedrin
1. How did the Jews respond the next day when they found out about Paul’s escape?
a. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7. 8. More than forty men bound themselves with a curse, saying that they would not eat or drink until they had killed Paul.
What did those men tell the Sanhedrin to request of the chief captain, Claudius
Lysius, concerning Paul?
a. The men told the Sanhedrin to ask that Paul be brought to the Sanhedrin while they lay in wait to kill him.
What did those actions of the men and the Sanhedrin show?
a. Those enemies of the truth showed that they were completely under the power of the lie and Satan.
Who uncovered the plot to kill Paul?
a. Paul’s sister’s son came and told Paul.
What did Paul tell the centurion who stood by?
a. Paul said, “Bring the young man to Claudius Lysius.”
What did Claudius Lysius do when the young man revealed the plot to kill Paul?
a. Claudius ordered two centurions to get ready two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen to take Paul to Felix the governor at night.
What did Claudius Lysius write in a letter to the governor?
a. Claudius puffed up himself and said that all along he had known that Paul was a Roman, so he rescued Paul from the Jews.
What else did Claudius write to the governor concerning the charges against
Paul?
a. Claudius wrote that Paul had been charged with questions about the Jews’ law, but he found nothing that made Paul worthy of prison or death.
9. After Felix read the letter, what did he tell Paul?
a. Felix said that he would hear Paul’s case when his accusers came.
10. What did that show concerning Christ’s sovereignty?
a. It showed that Christ is sovereign over all things so that Paul would go to Rome and Christ would bring the curse of his enemies on their own heads.
Memory verse: “The LORD thy God will put all these curses upon thine enemies, and on them that hate thee, which persecuted thee.”—Deuteronomy 30:7
Lesson 22: Paul Goes to Caesarea
1. Who came to accuse Paul before Felix the governor?
a. The high priest Ananias with the elders and a wicked lawyer Tertullus came to accuse Paul.
2. How did Tertullus slander Paul to Felix?
a. Tertullus accused Paul of being a rebel who stirred up the Jews and profaned the temple.
3. What did Tertullus say about the mob that tried to kill Paul and the chief captain, Claudius Lysius?
a. Tertullus said that the Jews were only going to judge Paul according to the Jews’ law, but the chief captain came with violence and took Paul away.
4. Did the Jews agree with the lies that Tertullus told?
a. Yes, the Jews said that Tertullus had told the truth.
5. How did Paul answer his false accusers?
a. Paul answered that he had been in Jerusalem only twelve days, had not disputed with any man, and the accusers could prove nothing.
6. What did Paul admit?
a. Paul admitted that his enemies called his preaching of Christ heresy, but he believed all that is written in the law and the prophets.
7. What hope did Paul confess concerning the resurrection?
a. Paul hoped in the resurrection of the just and the unjust, and he wanted the Jews to confess the same.
8. What did Paul point out to Felix the governor about his enemies from Asia?
a. Paul said that his enemies from Asia who had started the mob were not even present to accuse him.
9. What did Felix do when he heard that?
a. Felix knew about Christians, so he did not make a judgment but said he would do so later.
10. What did Felix and his Jewish wife Drusilla do later?
a. Felix sent for Paul, and Felix and his wife debated with Paul about righteousness, temperance, and the judgment to come.
11. Why did Felix not release Paul?
a. Felix hoped to receive money from Paul for his release, and Felix wanted to do the Jews a favor.
Memory verse: “Sanctify the Lord God in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear.”—1 Peter 3:15
Acts 24
Lesson 23: Paul Before Governor Felix
1. When Porcius Festus became governor after Felix, what did the Jews ask of Festus?
a. The Jews asked that Paul be brought to Jerusalem because they hoped to
1. kill him there.
3. What did Festus answer the Jews?
a. Festus said that the Jews had to come to Caesarea to accuse Paul.
4. What did the Jews say against Paul?
a. The Jews accused Paul of wickedness against the law, the temple, and Caesar, but they could prove nothing.
5. What did Paul say when Festus asked if Paul was willing to go to Jerusalem?
a. Paul said that he appealed to Caesar’s judgment in Rome.
6. Who came to help Festus write to Caesar about why Paul was being sent to
Rome?
a. King Herod Agrippa came with great pomp along with his sister Bernice.
How did Paul explain to King Agrippa the reason for the Jews accusations against him?
a. Paul said that he was being accused for the hope of God’s promise to Israel of the resurrection of the dead.
7. What else did Paul explain to Agrippa?
a. Paul explained that he had persecuted the Christians, but Christ appeared to him and converted him.
8. What was Paul’s explanation of the work Christ had sent Paul to do?
a. Paul said that Christ sent him to the Jews and the Gentiles to turn them from darkness to light and to give the forgiveness of sins and God’s covenant fellowship to them.
9. What did Paul say about his preaching?
a. Paul said that he preached God’s promise through Moses and the prophets that Christ should suffer, die, and arise for the salvation of Jews and Gentiles.
10. What did Agrippa say to Paul?
a. Agrippa said to Paul, “You almost persuaded me to be a Christian.”
11. What did Agrippa’s response show about faith?
a. It showed that not many mighty and not many noble are called and that faith is not in man’s power but is a gift to God’s elect.
Memory verse: “God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise; and God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things that are mighty.”—1 Corinthians 1:27
Lesson 24: Paul Before King Agrippa
1. What was the judgment of Agrippa and all who heard Paul’s defense?
a. They judged that Paul had done nothing worthy of death, but he had to go to Rome because he had appealed to Caesar.
2. Who guarded Paul on the ship that took him to Rome?
a. Paul was guarded by Julius the centurion, who treated Paul well and even allowed him to see his friends when they stopped at Sidon.
3. What was Paul’s warning to the captain of the ship when the ship stopped at
Crete?
a. Paul warned that on the voyage cargo would be lost, and the ship and the passengers would suffer much damage.
4. What happened when the centurion and the captain did not listen to Paul?
a. The ship was caught in a terrible storm for fourteen days.
5. When the angel of God appeared to Paul, how did Paul encourage the captain of the ship?
a. Paul said that the angel of God had appeared to him and said that Paul would appear before Caesar and that all who were on the ship were safe with Paul.
6. What happened when the ship was about to be dashed on the rocks?
a. While pretending to set anchors, the sailors let out a boat to flee the ship.
7. What did Paul tell the centurion?
a. Paul told the centurion that to save their lives the sailors had to stay with the ship and the centurion ordered his soldiers to cut the sailor’s boat away from the ship.
8. How did the ship come to land?
a. The men threw the whole cargo into the sea, drove the ship into the surf, and everyone swam for their lives.
9. What were the soldiers going to do with all the prisoners?
a. The soldiers were going to kill the prisoners to keep them from escaping.
10. Who stopped the soldiers from killing the prisoners?
a. The centurion stopped the solders because he wanted to save Paul alive.
11. What did all that history show?
a. That history showed that the world is saved alive only for the church’s sake.

Memory verse: “Let no man glory in men. For all things are yours; whether Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or the world, or life, or death, or things present, or things to come; all are yours; and ye are Christ’s; and Christ is God’s.”—1 Corinthians 3:21–23
Lesson 25: Paul’s Voyage to Rome
1. Where had the ship that Paul was on been shipwrecked?
a. The ship was wrecked off the island of Melita.
2. What did the tribes of that island do for the survivors?
a. The tribespeople were kind to the survivors and made a fire for them because it was raining and cold.
3. What happened as Paul was putting a bundle of sticks on the fire?
a. A viper came out of the sticks and bit Paul on the hand.
4. What did the tribespeople say when they saw that happen?
a. They said that Paul was a murderer who had escaped the sea but who met his fate on land.
5. What happened after Paul did not swell up and die from the snake bite?
a. The tribespeople said that Paul was a god.
6. Whom did Paul heal on the island?
a. Paul healed the father of Publius, chief of the island, whose father had a fever and a bloody flux.
7. What happened when the inhabitants of the island heard of that miracle?
a. Many others with diseases came to Paul and were healed.
8. What did those miracles show?
a. The miracles showed that Christ confirmed Paul’s preaching to the tribes that Christ alone is the way of salvation.
9. Why did Christ bring Paul to Melita?
a. Christ had his elect also on the islands of the sea, and he came through Paul to save them.
10. How did the people of Melita show their thankfulness?
a. The people honored Paul and his companions Luke and Aristarchus and loaded them with everything they needed.
11. How long was Paul on Melita?
a. Paul was there about three months, and then he left on a ship called Castor and Pollux that had wintered at Melita.

Memory verse: “It shall come to pass in that day, that the Lord shall set his hand again the second time to recover the remnant of his people, which shall be left, from Assyria, and from Egypt, and from Pathros, and from Cush, and from Elam, and from Shinar, and from Hamath, and from the islands of the sea.—Isaiah 11:11
Lesson 26: Paul at Melita
1. Who came to meet Paul after he landed near Rome?
a. Members of the church in Rome traveled to meet Paul and greatly encouraged him.
2. What privilege did the captain of the guard grant to Paul?
a. The captain allowed Paul to dwell in a house with a soldier to guard him.
3. Whom did Paul call to himself?
a. Paul called the leaders of the Jews to explain to them why he was in Rome.
4. What explanation did Paul give to the Jewish leaders?
a. Paul said that he had done nothing against the Jewish people or customs, but he was still delivered as a prisoner to the Romans.
5. What did Paul say about the governors who had examined him?
a. Paul said that the governors would have let him go, but the Jews were against it, so he was forced to appeal to Caesar.
6. What did Paul tell the Jewish leaders about the reason he was bound?
a. Paul said, “For the hope of Israel, I am bound with this chain.”
7. What did that mean?
a. It meant that Paul did not seek to overthrow the law, but he taught that the law had been fulfilled in Jesus Christ.
8. What did the Jewish leaders answer Paul?
a. The leaders said that they had heard nothing about Paul’s case, but they heard that a sect of Nazarenes was being spoken against everywhere.
9. What did Paul preach to the Jews in Rome?
a. Paul preached that in Jesus Christ the kingdom of God had come, and Paul proved that from Moses and the prophets.
10. What did Paul tell the Jewish leaders when they could not agree about the things Paul had said?
a. Paul said that Isaiah had prophesied of the Jewish leaders, saying, “Their hearts are fat, their ears are dull, and they have closed their eyes.”
11. What did Paul mean when he told the Jewish leaders about Isaiah’s prophecy?
a. Paul meant that God does not desire their salvation, but he will send the gospel to others and save those whom he has chosen.
12. What else did Paul tell the Jewish leaders.
a. Paul said, “Salvation is sent to the Gentiles, and they will hear it.”

Memory verse: “Make the heart of this people fat, and make their ears heavy, and shut their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and convert, and be healed.”—Isaiah 6:10
Lesson 27: Paul and the Jews in Rome
New Testament History
The Word into All the World
Grades 1–4
1. Did Israel remain faithful to God in Canaan, the land of promise?
a. Israel did not remain faithful and time and again turned away from God to serve idols.
2. How did Israel’s turning away from God happen?
a. There arose in Israel a generation that did not know the Lord or his mighty works.
3. What contributed to Israel’s failure to remain faithful to God?
a. Israel did not completely drive out the Canaanites, and they became a snare to Israel.
4. What was God’s purpose in Israel’s refusal to drive out all the heathen nations from Canaan?
a. God’s purpose was to prove the Israelites and to show what was in their hearts.
5. What became evident about Israel?
a. It became evident that by nature Israel was a sinful and unfaithful people who could not perfectly keep the law of God.
6. How did God deal with his sinful people?
a. God was most gracious: He chastised them with heathen nations, but he always raised up a judge to deliver them.
7. Who came to rebuke Israel for her failures?
a. The angel of Jehovah came to Bochim to tell Israel that she had not obeyed God by completely driving out the Canaanites.
8. What fruit did the angel’s rebuke work in Israel?
a. The people wept for their sins and made sacrifices to God in thankfulness for his mercy.
9. What else did the angel of Jehovah say to Israel at Bochim?
a. The angel said that he had sworn to Israel’s fathers that he would never break his covenant.
10. What does this history illustrate?
a. The history illustrates that the fulfillment of God’s promise is unconditional and is the work of his faithfulness and grace alone.
Memory verse: “Nevertheless the LORD raised up judges, which delivered them out of the hand of those that spoiled them.”—Judges 2:16
Judges 1–2; Judges 17–19; Psalm 106:34–48
Lesson 1: Israel’s Failure to Keep the Law
1. What did Israel do after the death of Joshua?
a. Israel’s sons and daughters married Canaanites and worshiped the idols of the Canaanites.
2. Whom did God use to chastise his people?
a. God sent the king of Mesopotamia, who ruled Israel for eight years.
3. Whom did God raise up to deliver Israel?
a. God raised up Judge Othniel, who judged Israel for forty years.
4. Did Israel continue to serve God after Othniel’s death?
a. No, Israel did evil in God’s sight.
5. Whom did God send against Israel?
a. God sent Eglon, king of Moab, who came with Ammon and Amalek and ruled Israel for eighteen years.
6. Whom did God raise up to save Israel?
a. God sent Ehud, the left-handed judge from the tribe of Benjamin.
7. What did Ehud say to King Eglon?
a. Ehud said that he had a secret message from God to Eglon.
8. What was Ehud’s message from God?
a. Ehud thrust a dagger through Eglon’s fat belly.
9. What did Ehud and Israel do to the Moabites?
a. They trapped the Moabites by the Jordan River and killed ten thousand Moabites.
10. How long did Israel have rest after Judge Ehud?
a. Israel had rest for eighty years.
Memory verse: “Sing aloud unto God our strength: make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob.”—Psalm 81:1
Judges 3
Lesson 2: Othniel and Ehud
1. Did Israel remain faithful to God after the death of Ehud?
a. No, and God sold Israel into the hand of Jabin king of Canaan, who terribly oppressed Israel for twenty years.
2. Who was the captain of King Jabin’s armies?
a. The captain was Sisera, who had with him nine hundred iron chariots.
3. Whom did God raise up to deliver Israel?
a. Through the prophetess Deborah, God called Barak of the tribe of Naphtali.
4. What did Barak say to Deborah?
a. Barak said to Deborah, “If you will go with me, I will go; but if not, then I will not go.”
5. What did Deborah say to Barak?
a. Deborah said that she would go with him, but God would not give the victory to Barak but to a woman.
6. What two tribes did Barak lead into battle?
a. Barak took ten thousand men from Zebulon and Naphtali.
7. How did God war against Sisera?
a. The angel of Jehovah caused a thunderstorm to flood the river where Sisera’s army was camped.
8. What happened to Sisera?
a. Sisera fled from the battle on foot and came to the tent of Jael.
9. What did Jael do when Sisera came to her tent?
a. Jael gave Sisera a drink of milk, and when he fell asleep on the tent floor, she pounded a tent stake through his temples and cut off his head.
10. What did the angel of Jehovah say to the Israelites who had refused to come to the battle against Sisera?
a. The angel cursed them because they would not fight against the Lord’s enemies.
Memory verse: “So let all thine enemies perish, O LORD: but let them that love him be as the sun when he goeth forth in his might.”—Judges 5:31
Judges 4–5; Psalm 83:9–10
Lesson 3: Deborah and Barak
1. Who oppressed Israel after the time of Deborah and Barak?
a. The Midianites with the Amalekites came up like a plague of locusts on Israel.
2. How did the Midianites and the Amalekites oppress Israel?
a. They came at harvest time every year and ate up all the food in the land.
3. What did God have a prophet tell the children of Israel?
a. The prophet told Israel that God had delivered them from Egypt and given them Canaan, but Israel disobeyed God and turned to idols.
4. Whom did God choose to deliver Israel from the Midianites?
a. God chose Gideon of the tribe of Manasseh.
5. How did the angel of Jehovah encourage Gideon?
a. The angel said that he would go with Gideon and that Gideon would destroy the Midianites.
6. What sign did the angel give to Gideon that God was with him?
a. With his staff the angel touched Gideon’s offering and consumed it with fire.
7. What did the sign of consuming Gideon’s offering mean?
a. The sign meant that Jehovah had forgiven Gideon and God’s people all their sins and was at peace with them.
8. What did Gideon do that night?
a. Gideon destroyed his father’s altar of Baal and made an altar to God.
9. What sign did Gideon ask of God that he would destroy Midian?
a. Gideon asked for the sign of dew upon a fleece.
10. What did that sign of the dew upon the fleece mean?
a. The sign meant that Israel’s oppression and her victory over Midian came from God’s sovereign love of his people.
Memory verse: “He remembered for them his covenant, and repented according to the multitude of his mercies.”—Psalm 106:45
Judges 6
Lesson 4: Gideon Called
1. How many men did God give to Gideon to fight the Midianites?
a. God gave three hundred men to Gideon.
2. Why did God give so few men to Gideon?
a. God gave few men so that Israel would know that God, not man, gave Israel her deliverance.
3. What weapons did God give to Gideon’s army?
a. God gave the army trumpets and torches inside pitchers.
4. How were the men to fight the Midianites?
a. The men were to break their pitchers, hold out their torches, blow on their trumpets, and shout, “The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon.”
5. What did God do to the Midianites?
a. God set the Midianites to fighting against one another, and they fled.
6. What did some of Israel’s men do?
a. They trapped and caught the Midianites at the Jordan River and killed their princes Oreb and Zeeb.
7. What did Gideon and his three hundred men do?
a. They pursued the remaining Midianites across the Jordan River.
8. What did the elders of Succoth and Penuel do when Gideon he asked for bread for his tired men?
a. The elders of Succoth and Penuel refused to give bread to Gideon and his men.
9. Did Gideon catch the rest of the Midianites?
a. Yes, and Gideon destroyed their army and killed their two princes Zebah and Zalmunna.
10. What did Gideon do to the men of Succoth and Penuel after that?
a. Gideon whipped the elders of Succoth with thorns, tore down the tower of Penuel, and killed all the men of Penuel.
Memory verse: “Make their nobles like Oreb, and like Zeeb: yea, all their princes as Zebah and Zalmunna.”—Psalm 83:11
Judges 7–8; Psalm 83:11–12; Isaiah 10:26
Lesson 5: The Defeat of the Midianites
1. Did the Israelites remain faithful to God after he had delivered them so many times?
a. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. They did not remain faithful, and they turned and worshiped all the gods of the Canaanites.
Whom did God use to chastise Israel?
a. God sent the Ammonites, who oppressed Israel for eighteen years.
Whom did God raise up to save Israel?
a. God raised up Jephthah, a mighty man of valor from Gilead.
What happened to Jephthah as a young man?
a. Jephthah was the son of a harlot, and his father’s sons drove out Jephthah from their house and land.
What did Jephthah tell the elders of Gilead when they wanted him to deliver
Gilead from the Ammonites?
a. Jephthah said that if the elders brought him home, he would lead them.
What did Jephthah vow to God if God would deliver the Ammonites into his hands?
a. Jephthah vowed that he would give to God whatever came out of his house first when he returned home.
7. Did Jephthah defeat the Ammonites?
a. Yes, and Jephthah destroyed twenty of Ammon’s cities.
8. Who came out of the house first to meet Jephthah?
a. His only daughter came out of the house first, and Jephthah dedicated her as a virgin to God.
9. What does Jephthah’s dedication of his daughter picture?
a. His dedication of his daughter pictures believing parents who baptize their children as the Lord’s.
10. Who threatened to burn Jephthah’s house?
a. Jephthah was threatened by the men of Ephraim, who were angry at his victory, and Jephthah killed forty-two thousand of them.
Memory verse: “Remember, O LORD, thy tender mercies and thy lovingkindnesses; for they have been ever of old.”—Psalm 25:6
Judges 10–12
Lesson 6: Jephthah
1. What was unique about Samson as judge?
a. Samson delivered Israel alone.
2. What was the secret of Samson’s great strength?
a. The Spirit of God was on Samson.
3. Against whom did God send Samson?
a. God sent Samson to fight the Philistines, who were oppressing and ruling Israel.
4. What did the angel of Jehovah say to Samson’s mother before he was born?
a. The angel told her that Samson would be a Nazarite from the womb until the day of his death.
5. What did it mean to be a Nazarite?
a. A Nazarite was separated unto the Lord and could not drink strong drink, cut his hair, or come near a dead body.
6. How did the angel of Jehovah identify himself?
a. The angel called himself by the name Wonderful.
7. Who was that angel of Jehovah?
a. The angel of Jehovah was our Lord Jesus Christ, and Samson would be a type of Jesus Christ.
8. Name some of Samson’s mighty acts.
a. He killed a lion with his bare hands. b. He killed thirty Philistine men. c. He burned the Philistines’ fields with foxes. d. He killed the Philistines with a great slaughter.
9. Were the men of Israel thankful for their deliverance through Samson?
a. No, they bound Samson and betrayed him to the Philistines.
10. What did Samson do to the Philistines?
a. Samson killed one thousand Philistines with the jawbone of an ass.
11. How long did Samson judge Israel?
a. Samson judged Israel for twenty years.
Memory verse: “Blessed be the LORD my strength which teacheth my hands to war, and my fingers to fight.”—Psalm 144:1
Judges 13–16:3; Numbers 6:1 –21
Lesson 7: Samson
1. What became clear throughout Samson’s judgeship?
a. It became clear that Samson was a sinful man whom God used.
2. What happened at Gath when Samson went to visit a harlot there?
a. The Philistines surrounded Samson, but at night he carried away the gates of the city of Gath.
3. Whom did Samson love next?
a. Samson loved the Philistine Delilah.
4. How did Delilah betray Samson?
a. She agreed with the lords of the Philistines to entice Samson to tell the secret of his great strength.
5. What did Samson tell Delilah?
a. Samson played along with her and told her to bind him with new ropes, and he would be weak like other men.
6. What did Samson say to Delilah after that?
a. Samson came closer to his secret and told her that she should weave his long hair to a beam.
7. What did Delilah do next?
a. She pestered Samson until he told her that he was a Nazarite and had never cut his hair.
8. What happened to Samson?
a. While Samson slept a man cut his hair, and the Philistines captured Samson, put out his eyes, and made him work in the prison.
9. How did Samson die?
a. Samson pulled the Philistines’ temple down on himself and three thousand Philistines.
10. What was the significance of Samson’s death?
a. Samson delivered Israel alone and by means of his death.
Memory verse: “Be not thou far from me, O LORD: O my strength, haste thee to help me.”— Psalm 22:19
Judges 16
Lesson 8: Samson’s Glorious End
1. Who were Elimelech and Naomi?
a. They were a husband and wife from the tribe of Judah, who went to live in Moab with their two sons during a famine in Israel.
2. Who were their two sons?
a. Their two sons were Mahlon and Chilion.
3. What happened while Elimelech and Naomi were in Moab?
a. Elimelech died, and then Naomi’s sons married women of Moab.
4. What happened to Mahon and Chilion.
a. They lived in Moab about ten years, and they also died.
5. What did Naomi do?
a. Naomi decided to return to Judah.
6. What did Naomi tell her two daughters-in-law?
a. Naomi told them to stay in Moab.
7. Did the women stay in Moab?
a. Orpah stayed in Moab, but Ruth would not, and she said to Naomi, “Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God.”
8. How did God bless Naomi and Ruth?
a. God caused Boaz to love Ruth and to marry her.
9. Of whom did Ruth become the mother?
a. Ruth became the mother of Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David.
10. What does the history of Ruth teach us?
a. We learn that we are unworthy, but God maintains our lot and brings to us the promised Christ, who was typified in David.
Memory verse: “The LORD is the portion of mine inheritance and of my cup: thou maintainest my lot.”—Psalm 16:5
Ruth 1–4
Lesson 9: Ruth
1. Who was Samuel’s mother?
a. Samuel’s mother was Hannah, who had prayed to God for Samuel and promised to give him to God.
2. What was the state of Israel when Samuel was born?
a. There was great wickedness in Israel and terrible oppression at the hands of the Philistines.
3. What explains the birth of Samuel?
a. Samuel was born because of God’s faithfulness to his covenant promise and God’s will that his people have his word.
4. Who was Israel’s high priest at that time?
a. The high priest was Eli.
5. Who were Eli’s sons?
a. Eli’s two sons were Hophni and Phinehas.
6. What does scripture tell us about Eli and his sons?
a. Scripture says that the sons were very wicked men and that Eli would not discipline them.
7. What did God tell the child Samuel one night?
a. God said that he would cut off the house of Eli, that Hophni and Phinehas would die in one day, and that God would raise up a faithful priest.
8. Who was that priest going to be?
a. That priest would be our Lord Jesus Christ.
9. Did Samuel tell Eli what God had said?
a. Samuel was afraid to tell him until Eli made Samuel tell him the word of God.
10. What does scripture tell us about Samuel?
a. Samuel was established in Israel as a prophet, and God did not let a word of Samuel fall to the ground even as a young child.
Memory verse: “We will rejoice in thy salvation, and in the name of our God we will set up our banners: the LORD fulfill all thy petitions.”—Psalm 20:5
1 Samuel 1–3
Lesson 10: The Birth of Samuel
1. What did the Israelites do when they could not defeat the Philistines in battle?
a. Hophni and Phinehas took the ark of the covenant into battle.
2. Did the Israelites defeat the Philistines then?
a. No, the Philistines killed thirty thousand Israelites, the ark was captured, and Hophni and Phinehas were killed.
3. What happened to Eli when he was told that the ark had been captured?
a. Eli fell backward off his seat, broke his neck, and died.
4. What did the Philistines do with the ark?
a. The Philistines put the ark in the temple of their idol god Dagon in Ashdod.
5. What did the priests of Dagon discover in the morning?
a. The priests discovered that the idol had fallen down, and its head and hands were cut off.
6. What else did God do to the Philistines?
a. God smote them with disease and mice, and very many Philistines died.
7. What did the Philistines do with the ark?
a. The Philistines sent the ark back to Israel on an ox cart with offerings of gold.
8. What did Samuel do?
a. Samuel told the Israelites to put away their idol gods, and Samuel sacrificed and prayed to God that he would forgive Israel’s sins.
9. How do we know that God forgave Israel’s sins?
a. When the Philistines attacked Israel, God sent a thunderstorm against the Philistines, and Israel defeated them.
10. What happened after that battle?
a. Samuel judged Israel, and God was against the Philistines all the days of Samuel.
Memory verse: “The LORD also thundered in the heavens, and the Highest gave his voice; hail stones and coals of fire.”—Psalm 18:13
1 Samuel 4–7
Lesson 11: The Ark Captured and the Defeat of the Philistines
1. What does scripture tell us about Samuel’s sons?
a. Scripture says that Samuel’s sons were wicked men who did not walk in the ways of their father.
2. When Samuel was old, what did the elders of Israel tell him?
a. The elders told Samuel to make them a king like the other nations.
3. Was that right of the elders of Israel?
a. No, for they rejected God as their king.
4. Whom did God give as a king to Israel?
a. God gave Saul of the tribe of Benjamin to Israel.
5. Was Saul a righteous king?
a. No, God gave Saul to Israel as a judgment on the nation.
6. How did Saul come to Samuel?
a. Saul was looking for his father’s lost donkeys, and God directed Saul to come to Samuel.
7. What did Samuel do?
a. Samuel made a feast for Saul and afterward anointed Saul with oil.
8. What did Saul’s anointing signify?
a. Saul’s anointing signified that God had chosen Saul to be Israel’s king.
9. What happened to Saul after his anointing?
a. The Spirit of the Lord came on Saul.
10. What can we say about the reign of Saul in Israel?
a. The reign of King Saul was trouble for Israel and prepared the way for David’s reign.
Memory verse: “I gave thee a king in mine anger, and took him away in my wrath.”—Hosea 13:11
1 Samuel 8–10
Lesson 12: Israel Demands a King
1. Who attacked Israel after Saul was made king?
a. Nahash, the king of Ammon, attacked the town of Jabesh-gilead.
2. What did Nahash tell the men of Jabesh-gilead when they asked to make a covenant with Nahash?
a. Nahash said that he would put out the right eyes of all the men of Jabesh- gilead.
3. What did Saul do when he heard that?
a. Saul gathered all Israel to fight against Nahash and the Ammonites.
4. Did Israel defeat the Ammonites?
a. Yes, God delivered the Ammonites into the hands of Israel, and Israel scattered the Ammonites.
5. What did Samuel preach to the people of Israel after that victory?
a. Samuel preached that the people had done very wickedly in asking for a king but that God would not forsake his people.
6. What did Samuel tell Saul when the Philistines attacked Israel at Gilgal?
a. Samuel told Saul to wait seven days until Samuel came to offer a sacrifice.
7. Did Saul listen to Samuel?
a. No, Saul offered a sacrifice himself.
8. What did Samuel tell Saul after he had disobeyed God’s command and offered a sacrifice?
a. Samuel told Saul that God would not establish Saul’s kingdom.
9. What else did Samuel tell Saul?
a. Samuel said that God had chosen as king another man who was after God’s own heart.
10. Who was that man?
a. That man was David, who would be king after Saul.
Memory verse: “The LORD will not forsake his people for his great name’s sake: because it hath pleased the LORD to make you his people.”—1 Samuel 12:22
1 Samuel 11–13
Lesson 13: Saul’s Failure at Gilgal
1. Who was one of Saul’s sons?
a. One of Saul’s sons was Jonathan, who was a child of God.
2. What did the Philistines do to Israel?
a. The Philistines raided Israel and oppressed the nation, so that Israel had no swords and spears for battle.
3. What did Jonathan do?
a. Jonathan took his armor-bearer and attacked the Philistines alone.
4. What was Jonathan’s confidence in attacking the Philistines alone?
a. Jonathan said that God can save by many or by few.
5. Did Jonathan defeat the Philistines?
a. Yes, God turned the Philistines against each other and saved Israel that day.
6. What rash thing did Saul tell the army of Israel when the army was pursuing the Philistines?
a. Saul took an oath and said that anyone who ate any food until the battle was finished was cursed.
7. What did Jonathan do?
a. Jonathan had not heard Saul’s oath, and Jonathan ate some honey he found.
8. Was Saul’s command good for Israel?
a. No, Saul’s command was very foolish, and the men of Israel were faint.
9. What did Saul say when he discovered that Jonathan had eaten some honey?
a. Saul said that Jonathan must die, but the people delivered him out of Saul’s hand.
10. What does this history show?
a. This history shows that Saul was a foolish and profane man who did not love God and made a show of righteousness.
Memory verse: “Put not your trust in princes, nor in the son of man, in whom there is no help. Happy is he that hath the God of Jacob for his help, whose hope is in the LORD his God.”—Psalm 146:3, 5
1 Samuel 14
Lesson 14: Jonathan’s Great Victory
1. Who was Amalek?
a. Amalek was the ungodly nation that always opposed God and his people Israel.
2. What had the Amalekites done to the Israelites in the wilderness after they came out of Egypt?
a. The Amalekites had attacked Israel and were defeated by Joshua.
3. What did God say about the nation of Amalek at that time?
a. God swore that he would have war with Amalek from generation to generation.
4. What did God tell Saul through Samuel about Amalek?
a. God told Saul to utterly destroy Amalek and to leave nothing alive.
5. Did Saul obey God and completely destroy Amalek?
a. No, Saul spared King Agag, the best of the sheep and oxen, and all that appeared good to the people.
6. How did Saul excuse his disobedience?
a. Saul said that the people had saved the sheep and oxen to sacrifice unto God.
7. What did Samuel tell Saul?
a. Samuel said to Saul, “Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice.”
8. What else did Samuel tell Saul?
a. Samuel said that God had rejected Saul from being king.
9. What did Samuel do to King Agag?
a. Samuel killed Agag as God had commanded.
10. Did Samuel have anything to do with Saul after that?
a. Samuel did not see Saul again until the day of Saul’s death.
Memory verse: “Rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft, and stubbornness is as iniquity and idolatry.”—1 Samuel 15:23
Exodus 17:8–16; 1 Samuel 15
Lesson 15: Saul’s Disobedience Regarding Amalek
1. What did God tell Samuel after God had rejected Saul as king?
a. God told Samuel to go to Jesse of Bethlehem because God had chosen a king from one of Jesse’s sons.
2. What did Samuel tell Jesse?
a. Samuel told Jesse to bring his sons to a sacrifice to God.
3. What did Samuel think when he saw the oldest son, Eliab?
a. Samuel thought that surely Eliab was God’s anointed.
4. What did God tell Samuel?
a. God told Samuel not to look on the outside because God judges the heart.
5. Were all the sons of Jesse at the sacrifice?
a. No, David, the youngest, was away keeping the sheep.
6. What did God tell Samuel when David was brought to the sacrifice?
a. God said to Samuel, “Arise, anoint him: for this is he.”
7. What happened to David when he was anointed?
a. The Spirit of God came on David from that day forward.
8. What happened to Saul about that time?
a. The Spirit of God left Saul, and God sent an evil spirit to trouble Saul.
9. Whom did Saul’s servants bring to him?
a. The servants sought out David, and brought him to Saul.
10. What did David do?
a. David played his harp for Saul, and then the evil spirit departed from Saul.
Memory verse: “I have made a covenant with my chosen, I have sworn unto David my servant.”—Psalm 89:3
1 Samuel 16; Psalm 89
Lesson 16: The Man After God’s Own Heart
1. Who defied the God of Israel when the Philistines came to battle against Israel?
a. The giant Goliath blasphemed God and challenged any man of Israel to fight him.
2. Was there found anyone who would fight Goliath?
a. No, all the men of Israel were afraid of Goliath.
3. What did David tell Saul?
a. David said, “Thy servant will go and fight this Philistine.”
4. How did David respond to Saul when he said that David was unable to fight Goliath?
a. David said that he killed a lion and a bear that had attacked his father’s sheep.
5. How did Saul try to protect David before he went to fight Goliath?
a. Saul gave his own sword and armor to David.
6. Did David use Saul’s armor and sword?
a. No, David took five stones and a sling to fight Goliath.
7. What did David say to Goliath when he mocked David?
a. David said to Goliath, “God will deliver you into my hand.”
8. Did David defeat and kill Goliath?
a. Yes, David hit Goliath’s forehead with a stone, so that he fell on his face, and David cut off Goliath’s head and took his armor and weapons for a spoil.
9. What explains David’s victory over Goliath?
a. David went out against Goliath in the name of God, and God fought against Goliath.
10. What did Saul do for David after that?
a. Saul made David a captain in the army, and David served Saul faithfully.
Memory verse: “All this assembly shall know that the LORD saveth not with sword and spear: for the battle is the LORD’S, and he will give you into our hands.”—1 Samuel 17:47
1 Samuel 17; 1 Samuel 18:16
Lesson 17: David Kills Goliath
1. What did the women of Israel sing that made Saul jealous and hate David?
a. The women sang, “Saul has slain his thousands and David his ten thousands.”
2. How did Saul show his hatred for David?
a. Twice Saul threw his spear at David while he was playing his harp for Saul.
3. What did Saul promise David if he would kill one hundred Philistines?
a. Saul promised his daughter Michal to David for a wife.
4. What was Saul hoping when he made that promise?
a. Saul was hoping that David would die in battle against the Philistines.
5. How did God show that he was with David?
a. David killed two hundred Philistines, and he was loved by all Israel because he fought for the people.
6. What did Saul finally tell Jonathan and all his servants?
a. Saul told them that David must die.
7. What did Jonathan do?
a. Jonathan loved David and pleaded for David’s life.
8. How did Saul answer Jonathan?
a. Saul swore an oath that he would not kill David.
9. Did Saul keep his oath?
a. No, once again Saul threw his spear at David to kill him while he was playing music for Saul.
10. What else did Saul do?
a. Saul sent his servants at night to surround David’s house to kill him, but David escaped to Samuel.
Memory verse: “I will sing of thy power; yea, I will sing aloud of thy mercy in the morning: for thou hast been my defence and refuge in the day of my trouble.”—Psalm 59:16
1 Samuel 18–19; Psalm 59
Lesson 18: Saul Hates David
1. What did Saul do when he heard that David had fled to Samuel?
a. Three times Saul sent men to capture David.
2. What happened to those men?
a. God sent his Spirit on those men, and they prophesied and could not capture David.
3. How did Saul show his extreme hardness against David?
a. Saul himself went to kill David, but Saul could not kill David because the Spirit of God made Saul prophesy too, and he lay naked all day and night.
4. Where did David flee after he was found with Samuel?
a. David met with his friend Jonathan.
5. What did David tell Jonathan?
a. David said that he would hide in a field while Jonathan talked with Saul.
6. What signal would Jonathan give to David to show that he could come back safely to Saul.
a. Jonathan would shoot three arrows to the side of David’s hiding place.
7. After Jonathan defended David to Saul, what did Saul tell Jonathan?
a. Saul was angry and said that as long as David was alive Jonathan would never be king.
8. How did Saul show his terrible hatred of David?
a. Saul threw a spear at the chair where David would have been sitting.
9. Why did Jonathan help David?
a. Jonathan loved David and believed that David was God’s anointed.
10. What did David and Jonathan do before David fled again?
a. They made a covenant that God would be between them and their seed forever.
Memory verse: “The righteous LORD loveth righteousness; his countenance doth behold the upright.”—Psalm 11:7
1 Samuel 19–20
Lesson 19: David’s Flees
1. Where did David flee after he met with Jonathan and could not return to Saul?
a. David and a small band of followers went to Ahimelech, a priest at Nob.
2. Why did David go to Ahimelech?
a. David was hungry, and he had no weapons.
3. What did David ask Ahimelech?
a. David asked him for the showbread and Goliath’s sword.
4. Was David allowed to eat the showbread?
a. No, only the priests were allowed to eat the showbread.
5. Why did Ahimelech give the showbread to David as God’s anointed and friend?
a. David had a right to the showbread as a type of Christ, who fulfilled the law of Moses.
6. Who saw Ahimelech help David?
a. Doeg, Saul’s reprobate Edomite servant, saw Ahimelech help David.
7. What did Saul do when he heard that Ahimelech had helped David?
a. Saul accused Ahimelech of conspiring against Saul.
8. What did Saul command his servants to do?
a. Saul commanded his servants to kill all the priests, but the servants would not.
9. Who then destroyed the priests at Nob?
a. Doeg killed the priests and all the men, women, children, and animals of the priests at Nob.
10. Did any escape Doeg’s murder of the priests?
a. Ahimelech’s son Abiathar escaped and fled to David with the ephod of the high priest.
Memory verse: “I will praise thee for ever, because thou hast done it: and I will wait on thy name; for it is good before thy saints.”—Psalm 52:9
1 Samuel 21; 1 Samuel 22:6–23; Psalm 52.
Lesson 20: David at Nob
1. After Ahimelech helped David, where did David flee?
a. In disguise David fled for safety to Achish, the king of Gath.
2. When the servants of King Achish recognized David, what did they say to him?
a. The servants said, “Is this not David the king? did they not sing, ‘Saul has slain his thousands and David his ten thousands’”?
3. What did David do when he heard that?
a. David was afraid and pretended to be a lunatic.
4. How did David escape?
a. God loved David, forgave him for his fears, and delivered him when Achish did not believe that the lunatic was David.
5. Where did David go after he had escaped from King Achish?
a. David stayed in the cave of Adullam.
6. Who came to David?
a. About four hundred people—including his brothers, father and mother, and the distressed and discontented in Israel—came to David.
7. Why did they come to David?
a. They came to David as God’s anointed and a type of Christ.
8. What did the prophet Gad tell David when he tried to stay in Moab for a time?
a. Gad told David that he could not stay in Moab because God had commanded David to return to the land of Judah.
9. What did Saul declare when he heard that David had been discovered again?
a. Saul complained that his servants conspired against him and did not tell him that Jonathan had made a covenant with David.
10. What did this history make plain regarding David’s life at that time?
a. The history showed that David was a fugitive and an outlaw in Israel but that God was with David.
Memory verse: “Be merciful unto me, O God, be merciful unto me: for my soul trusteth in thee: yea, in the shadow of thy wings will I make my refuge, until these calamities be overpast.”— Psalm 57:1
1 Samuel 21:10–15; 1 Samuel 22:1–8; Psalm 34; Psalm 56
Lesson 21: David in Gath and Adullam
1. What news did David hear after he had left Moab?
a. David heard that the Philistines were robbing the threshing floors and fighting against the city of Keilah.
2. How did David inquire about what he should do?
a. Abiathar the priest had brought the ephod to David with the Urim and Thummim, and David prayed and asked God.
3. What did God tell David?
a. God told David to go to Keilah, and God would deliver the Philistines into his hands.
4. Did David defeat the Philistines?
a. Yes, David killed the Philistines, took all their cattle, and saved the city of Keilah.
5. What did Saul do when he heard that David was discovered in Keilah?
a. Saul gathered his army to capture David because Saul said that God had delivered David into his hands.
6. What did God tell David when he asked whether the lords of Keilah would deliver him and his men to Saul?
a. God said that the lords of Keilah would deliver David and his men to Saul.
7. Was it wicked for the lords of Keilah to deliver David and his men to Saul?
a. Yes, for the lords of Keilah were called to defend David in thankfulness for his salvation of them and their city from the Philistines.
8. What did David and his six hundred men do when they heard that Saul was coming to war against them?
a. David and his men fled into the strongholds in the wilderness.
9. What was Saul busy doing?
a. Every day Saul was searching for David and his men, but God did not give them up to Saul.
10. Who came to David at this time?
a. Jonathan came to encourage David that God would make him the next king.
Memory verse: “What time I am afraid, I will trust in thee. In God I will praise his word, in God I have put my trust; I will not fear what flesh can do unto me.”—Psalm 56:3–4
1 Samuel 23:1–18
Lesson 22: Keilah’s Neutrality, and Jonathan Strengthens David
1. Who were the Ziphites?
a. The Ziphites were David’s kinsmen from the tribe of Judah.
2. What did the wretched Ziphites do to David?
a. The Ziphites betrayed David to Saul and agreed to spy on David and to lead Saul to David’s hideout.
3. What did Saul do with his army after that information?
a. Saul pursued David and surrounded him, so that he had no way of escape from his rocky hiding place in the wilderness.
4. How did God deliver David?
a. God sent the Philistines to invade the land, so that Saul had to give up pursuing David to go fight the Philistines.
5. What did David call his rocky hiding place after that?
a. David named it the rock of escape as a memorial to God, who had delivered him.
6. What did Saul do immediately after he had finished pursuing the Philistines?
a. Saul gathered three thousand men and went to kill David.
7. What did David do to Saul when he came into a cave where David was hiding?
a. While Saul relieved himself, David secretly cut off a piece of Saul’s robe.
8. What did David do later outside the cave?
a. David showed Saul the piece of his robe as proof that David could have killed Saul, and David said that he would not touch Saul because he was king.
9. What did Saul say to David?
a. Saul said to David, “You rewarded me good, while I rewarded you evil.”
10. Was Saul truly sorry for the way he had treated David?
a. Saul was not sorry, and later he tried again to kill David.
Memory verse: “Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens; let thy glory be above all the earth.”—Psalm 57:5
1 Samuel 23:19–29; 1 Samuel 24; Psalm 54; Psalm 57; Psalm 142
Lesson 23: David Betrayed, the Rock of Escape, and David Cuts Saul’s Robe
1. While David and his men were in the wilderness of Judah, whose sheep did they 2. 3. 4. 5. protect?
a. They protected the sheep of Nabal, a very rich man of the tribe of Judah.
What did David ask of Nabal when he made a feast at the time of sheep shearing?
a. David sent messengers to Nabal to ask for a gift for David’s young men.
How did Nabal respond to David’s messengers?
a. Nabal insulted David and sent the men away without a gift.
What did David tell his men to do?
a. David told his men to put on their swords and to destroy the whole house of Nabal.
What did one of Nabal’s young men do when he heard that Nabal had insulted
David?
a. a. The young man told Abigail, Nabal’s godly wife.
6. What did Abigail do when she heard of Nabal’s insults?
Abigail quickly took a gift to David and his men and told David that
Nabal’s was a man of Belial and a fool.
7. What else did Abigail tell David?
a. Abigail asked for forgiveness, acknowledged David as the Lord’s anointed, and pleaded with David not to avenge himself because God would avenge David.
8. Did David listen to Abigail?
a. Yes, David thanked God for sending her, and he blessed Abigail for keeping him from avenging himself.
9. What happened in the morning when Abigail told Nabal what had happened?
a. Nabal’s heart died in him, and God killed Nabal ten days later.
10. What did David do for Abigail?
a. David took Abigail to be his wife.
Memory verse: “The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God. They are corrupt, they have done abominable works, there is none that doeth good.”—Psalm 14:1
1 Samuel 25
Lesson 24: Nabal and Abigail
1. How did the miserable Ziphites betray David again?
a. The Ziphites told Saul that David was hiding in their area.
2. What did Saul do?
a. Saul gathered his army to hunt David and to kill him.
3. What did David do?
a. David slipped into Saul’s camp at night with Abishai, David’s cousin, and while Saul slept David took Saul’s spear that was by his head.
4. What did David do when he was safely away from Saul?
a. David showed the spear that he had taken as proof that he could have killed Saul.
5. What did David tell Saul?
a. David said that he had been driven out of his inheritance and hunted by Saul like a bird.
6. How did Saul respond to David?
a. Saul said, “I have sinned, and I will hurt you no more.”
7. Was that true?
a. No, Saul was only relieved that his life had been spared by David.
8. Where did David go after that encounter with Saul?
a. David went to Gath in Philistia and was given the city of Ziklag to live in with his men.
9. What did David do at Ziklag?
a. David warred against the Amalekite enemies of Israel.
10. What did David tell the king of Gath to make him think that David was his servant?
a. David said that he was invading Israel.
Memory verse: “My soul hath long dwelt with him that hateth peace. I am for peace: but when I speak, they are for war.”—Psalm 120:6–7
1 Samuel 26–27; Psalm 63
Lesson 25: David Takes Saul’s Spear, and David Lives Among the Philistines
1. When the Philistines gathered their armies to attack Israel, what did Saul do?
a. Saul went to see a witch so that he could talk to Samuel.
2. What did Samuel tell Saul?
a. Samuel said that God’s rejection of Saul as king and giving the kingdom to David happened to Saul just as Samuel had said.
3. What else did Samuel tell Saul?
a. Samuel said that God was Saul’s enemy who would deliver Saul into the hands of the Philistines the next day.
4. Who tried to join the army of the Philistines in the battle?
a. David with his men tried to join, but the lords of the Philistines would not have David in the battle, so he returned to Ziklag.
5. What happened in Saul’s battle with the Philistines?
a. The Philistines killed Saul’s three sons, and the archers sorely wounded Saul.
6. What did Saul tell his armor-bearer?
a. Saul said, “Take your sword and kill me,” but his armor-bearer would not.
7. What did Saul do?
a. Saul took his own sword, fell on it, and killed himself.
8. What did the Philistines do with Saul?
a. The Philistines cut off Saul’s head and hung his body and the bodies of his sons on the wall of a captured city of Israel.
9. Who recovered and buried the bodies of Saul and his sons?
a. The bodies were buried by the mighty men of Jabesh-gilead, whom Saul had delivered at the beginning of his reign.
10. What did David do when he heard of Saul’s death?
a. David lamented for Saul and his son Jonathan.
Memory verse: “Lo, this is the man that made not God his strength; but trusted in the abundance of his riches, and strengthened himself in his wickedness.”—Psalm 52:7
1 Samuel 28–31; 2 Samuel 1:17–27; 1 Chronicles 10:1–14.
Lesson 26: Saul’s Terrible End
1. Did David receive the kingdom and become king after the death of Saul?
a. The men of Judah anointed David as their king in Hebron, but the rest of Israel followed Ishbosheth, Saul’s son.
2. Who made Ishbosheth king?
a. Ishbosheth was made king by Abner, Saul’s uncle and captain of the host of Israel.
3. What was the result of that division in Israel?
a. There were seven years of war between the house of David and the house of Saul.
4. What was the outcome of that war?
a. The house of David grew stronger and stronger, and the house of Saul became weaker and weaker.
5. Who were David’s captains of war?
a. David’s captains were three brothers who were David’s cousins: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel.
6. What happened to Asahel in battle?
a. Asahel pursued Abner and would not stop, so Abner killed Asahel.
7. What did Abner do at the end of those seven years of war?
a. Abner came to David to make him king over all Israel.
8. What did Joab do when David sent Abner away in peace?
a. Joab tricked Abner to come back, and Joab secretly killed Abner because he had killed Joab’s brother Asahel.
9. What did David say about Joab?
a. David said that the blood of Abner was on Joab’s head, and David cursed the house of Joab.
10. What did David tell Israel?
a. David told the people to mourn for Abner.
Memory verse: “The LORD is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer; my God, my strength, in whom I will trust; my buckler, and the horn of my salvation, and my high tower.—Psalm 18:2
2 Samuel 2–3; Psalm 18
Lesson 27: War Between David and Abner
Old Testament History
Grades 5-8
The Judges to David Becomes King
1. What event marked the beginning of David’s reign over all Israel?
a. David’s reign over all Israel began after the murder of Saul’s son Ishbosheth by his servants.
2. What did David do to those servants?
a. David killed the servants and had their bodies hung up as a curse against them.
3. What did Israel do after the death of Ishbosheth?
a. The elders of all Israel made David king in Hebron.
4. How long did David reign as king?
a. David reigned seven years in Hebron over Judah and thirty-three years over all Israel.
5. What was one of David’s first acts as king?
a. David took the city of Jerusalem from the Jebusites and ruled there as king.
6. What did David want to bring up into Jerusalem?
a. David wanted to bring the ark of God’s covenant up into Jerusalem.
7. Of what was the ark a symbol, or type?
a. The ark was a type of God’s throne that is between the cherubim in heaven.
8. What happened in the first attempt to bring the ark up into Jerusalem?
a. The men of David put the ark on an ox cart, the cart wobbled, Uzza touched the ark, and God killed Uzza.
9. When did David successfully bring the ark up into Jerusalem?
a. David brought the ark about three months later by the hands of the priests, as God had commanded Moses.
10. To what did the bringing of the ark up into Jerusalem point?
a. It pointed to the ascension of Christ when he went up on high in might to reign as head of his church.
Memory verse: “He hath remembered his covenant for ever, the word which he commanded to a thousand generations.”–Psalm 105:8
2 Samuel 4–6; 1 Chronicles 11; 1 Chronicles 13–16; Psalm 68
Lesson 1: David Anointed King
1. Who was the first to attack Israel after David became king?
a. The Philistines heard that David was king and attacked Israel.
2. How did David defeat the Philistines?
a. God went before David into battle and overthrew the Philistines.
3. What characterized the reign of King David?
a. David’s reign was a period of wars and conquests, and God brought the fear of David upon all nations.
4. What nations did David defeat?
a. David defeated Philistia, Amalek, Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Syria.
5. What explains David’s victories in all those wars?
a. God had promised Abraham to give the whole land of Canaan to Israel and to put the nations under Israel’s feet.
6. What battle in particular brought that period of David’s warfare to a close?
a. David warred against the Ammonites, who had hired the Syrians to help them.
7. What happened in that battle?
a. The Syrians had surrounded David and his army, but when David defeated the Syrians, they made peace with Israel.
8. Whom else did David and his servants destroy at this time?
a. They had war with the Philistines and destroyed the brothers and children of Goliath.
9. Of what were all David’s might and wars a type?
a. They were a type of Christ and his kingdom and our victories in Christ over sin, Satan, and the whole world.
10. What purpose did David’s victories serve?
a. David’s victories enriched the kingdom of David and provided much of the gold, silver, brass, and iron with which Solomon would later build the temple.
Memory verse: “Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man. Through God we shall do valiantly: for he it is that shall tread down our enemies.”—Psalm 60:11–12
Genesis 15:18–21; 2 Samuel 8–10; 2 Samuel 12:26–31; 2 Samuel 21:15–22; 1 Chronicles 14; 1 Chronicles 18–20; Psalm 60; Psalm 105
Lesson 2: David’s Wars and His Might
1. What did David do when Joab went to fight against the Ammonites?
a. David stayed home in Jerusalem.
2. When David walked on the roof, whom did he see washing herself?
a. David saw Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah the Hittite, one of David’s mighty men.
3. What did David do to Bathsheba?
a. David took her and committed adultery with her, and Bathsheba became pregnant.
4. How did David attempt to cover his sins?
a. David called Uriah home from the battle and tried to trick Uriah to sleep with his wife.
5. When that scheme did not work, what did David do?
a. By the hand of Uriah, David sent a letter to Joab, ordering him to set Uriah in the front of the battle so that he would be killed.
6. Did God see all David’s wickedness?
a. Yes, God saw, and he sent Nathan the prophet to David to rebuke him for his wickedness.
7. Did David repent of his sins?
a. Yes, he repented, for God had forgiven all David’s trespasses.
8. What chastisement did Nathan announce to David?
a. Nathan told David that the child with Bathsheba would die and that the sword would not depart from David’s house.
9. What did God give to David through Bathsheba?
a. God gave to David his son Solomon, whom the Lord loved and who would sit on David’s throne.
10. What explains David’s sin?
a. We are all conceived and born dead in trespasses and sins.
Memory verse: “Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered. Blessed is the man unto whom the LORD imputeth not iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no guile.”— Psalm 32:1–2
2 Samuel 11:1–12:25; Psalm 32; Psalm 51
Lesson 3: David’s Sin with Bathsheba
1. Did David have trouble in his house as God had said?
a. Yes, David’s son Amnon assaulted his brother Absalom’s sister Tamar, so Absalom killed Amnon.
2. What did Absalom do after he killed Amnon?
a. Absalom fled and lived with the King of Geshur for three years.
3. What did Absalom do when he returned to King David?
a. Absalom began to plot a rebellion against David to take the kingdom for himself.
4. How did Absalom begin his plot to take the kingdom?
a. Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel.
5. Who joined Absalom’s conspiracy?
a. David’s friend Ahithophel joined Absalom to give counsel to him.
6. What did David do when he heard of that conspiracy?
a. David and his bodyguard left Jerusalem while David’s friend Hushai and Zadok the priest remained to spy for David.
7. Who met David as he was leaving Jerusalem?
a. Shimei, a man of the house of Saul, met David and cursed him.
8. What was Ahithophel’s counsel to Absalom?
a. Ahithophel said that he would attack David that night with twelve thousand men.
9. Did Absalom listen to the counsel of Ahithophel?
a. No, Absalom did not listen because God was determined to overthrow Absalom.
10. What happened in the battle with Absalom?
a. Absalom’s army was defeated, and when Joab found Absalom caught in a tree by his hair, Joab killed Absalom.
Memory verse: “Salvation belongeth unto the LORD: thy blessing is upon thy people.”—Psalm 3:8
2 Samuel 13–19; Psalm 3; Psalm 7
Lesson 4: Absalom’s Rebellion
1. How did Satan attack the kingdom of Israel?
a. Satan provoked David to number the people.
2. What was the meaning of the sin of numbering the people?
a. Numbering the people was unbelief in the promise that salvation is by grace alone.
3. Why did God sovereignly determine this sin of David?
a. God was angry at the Israelites for their stubborn unbelief in the promise.
4. When Gad the seer rebuked David for his sin, what chastisement did he choose of the three offered to him?
a. David chose to fall into the hands of God, and the angel of Jehovah destroyed seventy thousand Israelites by pestilence.
5. What did David see from Jerusalem?
a. David saw the angel of Jehovah with a sword in his hand stretched out over Jerusalem.
6. What did God tell the angel at that time?
a. God had compassion on Israel and told the angel that the chastisement of Israel was enough.
7. What did the angel through Gad tell David?
a. Gad told David to make a sacrifice for Israel at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.
8. What did God tell the angel after David made sacrifices?
a. God told the angel to put away his sword.
9. What would that threshing floor become?
a. The threshing floor became the place of Israel’s temple and the altar of burnt offering.
10. Of what was David’s sacrifices a sign?
a. David’s sacrifices were a sign of the perfect sacrifice of Christ, by which he made satisfaction to God to justify us and deliver us from the guilt of our sins.

Memory verse: “David said unto God, I have sinned greatly, because I have done this thing: but now, I beseech thee, do away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly.”—1 Chronicles 21:8
2 Samuel 24; 1 Chronicles 21
Lesson 5: David Numbers the People
1. What did God through Nathan the prophet tell David when he desired to make a temple for the ark?
a. Nathan told David that God would raise up a seed for David and that his son would build God’s temple.
2. What else did God tell David about that seed?
a. God said that he would establish the throne of David’s seed forever.
3. Who was that son of David who would build the temple?
a. That son was Solomon, a type of Jesus Christ.
4. What temple of God does Christ build?
a. Christ builds the church as the temple of God gathered from all nations.
5. What did David do to prepare for the building of the temple?
a. David laid aside much gold, silver, brass, iron, and precious stones, and collected cedars from Lebanon and hewn stone.
6. Whom did David charge to build the temple?
a. David charged Solomon to build the temple and instructed all the princes of Israel to help Solomon.
7. What did David give to Solomon?
a. David gave the pattern for all things in the temple that he had received from the Spirit of God.
8. What did David do for the priests?
a. David organized the priests by courses for the temple service to make sacrifices for the people of God.
9. What did David do for the Levites?
a. David instructed the Levites to help the priests, to sing, and to praise God for his faithfulness and mercy.
10. What did David say that God had given to Israel in David’s kingdom?
a. David said that God had given Israel rest as God had promised.

Memory verse: “When thy days be fulfilled, and thou shalt sleep with thy fathers, I will set up thy seed after thee, which shall proceed out of thy bowels, and I will establish his kingdom.”—2 Samuel 7:12
2 Samuel 7; 1 Chronicles 17; 1 Chronicles 22–29; Psalm 89; Psalm 95
Lesson 6: God’s Promise to David About Building God’s House
1. Who tried to take the kingdom when David was old and weak?
a. David’s son Adonijah made a conspiracy with Joab and Abiathar to take the kingdom.
2. What did David do?
a. David told Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet to anoint Solomon king.
3. Upon whom did Solomon execute judgment?
a. Solomon put Abiathar out of the priest’s office for following Adonijah, and Solomon executed Joab for murdering two men more righteous than himself.
4. What did Solomon tell Shimei, who had cursed David?
a. Solomon put Shimei under oath that he would not leave Jerusalem again.
5. What did Solomon do when Shimei left Jerusalem?
a. Solomon put Shimei to death because he broke his oath and because he had cursed David.
6. What was the great work of Solomon’s reign?
a. Solomon built the house of God, the temple, as David had commanded him.
7. How long did it take to build the temple?
a. It took seven years to build the temple.
8. What did Solomon pray at the dedication of the temple?
a. Solomon blessed God for fulfilling his promise to his chosen servant David and for choosing Jerusalem as the place where God would dwell.
9. What did Solomon ask God to do for his people?
a. Solomon prayed that God would hear his people and would forgive their sins.
10. How did God answer Solomon’s prayer?
a. Fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering, and the glory of Jehovah filled the temple, so that the priests could not enter in.

Memory verse: “Arise, O LORD God, into thy resting place, thou, and the ark of thy strength: let thy priests, O LORD God, be clothed with salvation, and let thy saints rejoice in goodness.”—2 Chronicles 6:41
1 Kings 1–3; 1 Kings 6–8; 1 Chronicles 29; 2 Chronicles 1; 2 Chronicles 3–7
Lesson 7: Solomon Crowned King, and Solomon Builds God’s House
1. What did Solomon ask of God when he appeared to Solomon in Gibeon?
a. Solomon asked of God wisdom and knowledge to rule God’s people, and God gave to Solomon glory, riches, and honor also.
2. What was the wisdom that God gave to Solomon?
a. That wisdom was the Spirit of the Lord Jesus Christ.
3. When did Solomon show this wisdom and knowledge?
a. Solomon showed his wisdom when two harlots came to him, arguing about which harlot’s baby had died and whose baby was living.
4. What else showed Solomon’s wisdom and knowledge?
a. Solomon wrote many proverbs that are found in the book of Proverbs.
5. How does scripture describe the astounding wealth of Solomon’s kingdom?
a. Solomon made silver in Jerusalem to be as stones and cedar trees to be like common sycamore trees.
6. Did Solomon have war?
a. No, God subdued Israel’s enemies round about, and Solomon’s kingdom had peace.
7. Who came to hear the wisdom and to see the wealth of Solomon?
a. The queen of Sheba came to prove Solomon with hard questions.
8. What did the queen say when Solomon had answered all her questions and she had seen all his prosperity?
a. The queen said that she had heard a true report but half had not been told to her.
9. Was she the only one to visit Solomon?
a. No, many kings of the earth sought to hear the wisdom that God had put in Solomon’s heart and to bring him their riches.
10. How are the wisdom, peace, and riches of Solomon pictures, or types, of Christ’s heavenly kingdom?
a. They are types of the righteousness of Christ, peace with God, and all the blessings and glories coming in the new heaven and new earth.

Memory verse: “Blessed be the LORD God, the God of Israel, who only doeth wondrous things. And blessed be his glorious name for ever: and let the whole earth be filled with his glory; Amen, and Amen.”—Psalm 72:18–19
1 Kings 3–4; 1 Kings 9–10; 2 Chronicles 1; 2 Chronicles 8-9; Psalm 72; Matthew 12:42; Luke 11:31
Lesson 8: Solomon’s Wisdom and Glory and the Peace and Fame of His Kingdom
1. What were Solomon’s sins?
a. Solomon loved many strange women of the nations around Israel, and they turned Solomon’s heart to worship other gods.
2. Why was the sin of going after other gods especially grievous?
a. That sin was grievous because twice God had appeared to Solomon and warned him about not worshiping other gods.
3. What did God tell Solomon he would do to his kingdom?
a. God said that he would rend the kingdom from Solomon but not during Solomon’s days, and God would reserve the tribe of Judah for his servant David’s sake.
4. What did God do to Solomon to chastise him?
a. God raised up adversaries against Solomon all the rest of his days.
5. Who was one of those adversaries of Solomon?
a. One adversary was the Edomite Hadad, who raised a revolt.
6. Who was another adversary of Solomon?
a. Another adversary was the Syrian, Rezon, who attacked Israel all the rest of Solomon’s days.
7. Who was the most dangerous adversary?
a. The most dangerous adversary was Jeroboam of the tribe of Ephraim, one of Solomon’s mighty men.
8. What did Ahijah the prophet do to Jeroboam?
a. Ahijah caught Jeroboam in a field and tore his coat into twelve pieces and gave Jeroboam ten pieces because God would give ten tribes to him.
9. What did Solomon do when he heard that?
a. Solomon sought to kill Jeroboam.
10. Where did Jeroboam go?
a. Jeroboam fled to Shishak, king of Egypt, until the death of Solomon.
Memory verse: “Unto his son will I give one tribe, that David my servant may have a light always before me in Jerusalem, the city which I have chosen me to put my name there.”—1 Kings 11:36
1 Kings 11
Lesson 9: Solomon’s Failure and God’s Chastisement
1. Where did Rehoboam, the son of Solomon, go to be crowned king?
a. Rehoboam went to Shechem.
2. Why did Rehoboam go to Shechem and not Jerusalem?
a. Rehoboam went to Shechem because Israel would not accept him as king unless he lightened the burdens that Solomon had laid on them.
3. Whom had Israel called out of Egypt as her leader?
a. Israel had called the rebel Jeroboam from Egypt.
4. What did the old counselors of Solomon tell Rehoboam?
a. The old counselors assured Rehoboam that if he spoke kind and good words to the people, then Israel would serve him.
5. What did the young men with whom Rehoboam had grown up counsel him?
a. The young men told Rehoboam to tell the people that he would be harder on them than his father Solomon.
6. What did Rehoboam tell the people?
a. Rehoboam told the people that if his father had chastised them with whips, he would use scorpions.
7. How did Israel respond?
a. Israel said, “What portion have we in David? We have no inheritance in the son of Jesse. Every man to your tents, O Israel.”
8. How did Israel show her anger?
a. Israel stoned Rehoboam’s tax collector, and Rehoboam had to flee for his life to Jerusalem.
9. What was Israel’s sin in that?
a. Israel rebelled against God and departed from Christ by rejecting his servant David.
10. Who was sovereign over that wickedness?
a. God said that the cause was from him so that he might perform the word that he had spoken by the prophet Ahijah to Jeroboam.

Memory verse: “Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.”—Luke 12:51–52
1 Kings 12; 2 Chronicles 10–11:4
Lesson 10: The Division of the Kingdom
1. Who strengthened the kingdom of Judah after the division of the kingdom?
a. The priests and Levites and many believing Israelites forsook their possessions and came to Judah.
2. Why did they come to the kingdom of Judah?
a. They came because Judah was God’s kingdom, and the temple and its promises were in Judah.
3. Was Rehoboam a godly king?
a. No, Rehoboam forsook the law of God along with the princes and the people.
4. What helps to explain that unfaithfulness?
a. Rehoboam’s mother was an Ammonite, and his wife was the granddaughter of Absalom.
5. Whom did God send to chastise the nation of Judah?
a. God sent Shishak, king of Egypt, with an enormous army.
6. What did Shishak do to the kingdom of Judah?
a. Shishak stripped the nation of all its treasures, and Judah became servants to Shishak.
7. What did God tell Rehoboam and the princes through the prophet Shemaiah?
a. God told Rehoboam and the princes that God left them in the hands of Shishak because they had forsaken God.
8. What was the result of God’s word through the prophet?
a. Rehoboam and the princes humbled themselves.
9. Was Rehoboam’s repentance genuine?
a. No, Rehoboam was a hypocrite.
10. Why did God not destroy the nation of Judah entirely because the people had forsaken him?
a. God did not destroy the nation entirely because there was an elect remnant in Judah whom God loved.
Memory verse: “Know ye that the LORD he is God: it is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves; we are his people, and the sheep of his pasture.”—Psalm 100:3
1 Kings 14:21–31; 2 Chronicles 11:13–21; 2 Chronicles 12
Lesson 11: Shishak’s Invasion of Judah
1. What did Jeroboam do to strengthen the kingdom of Israel, the ten tribes, in his hand?
a. Jeroboam made golden calves for the people to worship at Dan and Bethel and ordained priests for the calves from the lowest of the people.
2. Whom did God send to Israel to warn against that wickedness?
a. God sent a prophet who told Jeroboam that a future king of Judah would burn the bones of the priests of the calves on the altar at Bethel.
3. What characterized the kingdom of Israel and its kings?
a. The kingdom of Israel never departed from the worship of the golden calves, and all her kings were wicked.
4. Who destroyed the house of Jeroboam?
a. Baasha, king of Israel, conspired against Jeroboam’s son, killed him, and then destroyed all of Jeroboam’s house according to the word of the Lord.
5. What happened after King Baasha died?
a. A civil war erupted in Israel that finally led to Omri becoming king.
6. Who became king of Israel after Omri?
a. Ahab, Omri’s son, reigned after him.
7. What do we read of Ahab?
a. Ahab married Jezebel, brought Baal worship into Israel, and did more wickedly than all the kings of Israel before him.
8. What is an example of Abah’s extreme wickedness?
a. Ahab murdered the godly Naboth and his whole family and took Naboth’s inheritance.
9. What did God say to Ahab through the prophet Elijah?
a. Elijah said that God would destroy Ahab’s whole family.
10. What does this history of Israel make plain?
a. The history shows that Israel was a rebel kingdom against God and a type of the false church.
Memory verse: “The wicked are like the troubled sea, when it cannot rest, whose waters cast up mire and dirt.”—Isaiah 57:20
Lesson 12: Jeroboam to Ahab, Kings of the Ten Tribes
1. Who as King Asa?
a. Asa was the godly grandson of Rehoboam, and Asa reigned forty-one years in Judah.
2. What did Asa’s reign as king show?
a. Asa’s reign showed that God was faithful to his promise to his servant David to give him a son to sit on his throne always.
3. What did Asa pray to God when Judah was attacked by the army of Zerah the Ethiopian?
a. Asa prayed that God would help Judah because the people had no strength themselves.
4. How did God show that he was with Asa?
a. God fought against the Ethiopians and defeated them.
5. Who strengthened the kingdom of Judah at that time?
a. Many people from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon left Israel because they saw that God was with Asa.
6. What sin did Asa commit during his war with Baasha, king of Israel?
a. Asa made an alliance with Benhadad, king of Syria.
7. What rebuke did Hanani the seer give to Asa for that alliance?
a. Hanani said that Asa had done very foolishly and that the rest of Asa’s reign would be full of warfare.
8. Did Asa receive that word of the prophet?
a. No, Asa was angry and put the prophet in prison and persecuted the people who supported the prophet.
9. When God made Asa sick at the end of his reign, what did Asa do?
a. Asa looked to doctors for help and not to the Lord.
10. What do the failures of godly King Asa show us?
a. Asa’s failures show that the sons of David were sinful men and that God’s promise to David rested on God’s faithfulness alone.

Memory verse: “LORD, it is nothing with thee to help, whether with many, or with them that have no power: help us, O LORD our God; for we rest in thee, and in thy name we go against this multitude. O LORD, thou art our God; let no man prevail against thee.”—2 Chronicles 14:11
1 Kings 15:9–24; 2 Chronicles 14:1–16:14
Lesson 13: Asa
1. Who reigned as king in Judah after Asa?
a. Asa’s son Jehoshaphat reigned as king.
2. Did Jehoshaphat do what was right in God’s eyes?
a. Yes, Jehoshaphat removed the high places and the idol groves out of the land.
3. How else did Jehoshaphat show that he loved God?
a. Jehoshaphat sent princes and Levites to teach the law of God to the people, and he appointed judges in all the cities.
4. How is Jehoshaphat a type of Christ in that work?
a. Jehoshaphat shows us Christ our king, who by his Spirit removes idolatry in our hearts and instructs us in the truth.
5. Did God bless Jehoshaphat’s reign?
a. Yes, Jehoshaphat had riches and honor in abundance, a mighty army, and the nations around Judah made no war on Judah.
6. What sin did Jehoshaphat commit?
a. Jehoshaphat married his son to Ahab’s daughter Athaliah and made an alliance with the house of Ahab.
