7. What happened when Jehoshaphat went with Ahab to battle at Ramoth-gilead?
a. The Syrian armies defeated Israel, and Ahab was killed in battle.
8. How did God rebuke Jehoshaphat for his alliance with Ahab?
a. God sent the prophet Jehu, who asked Jehoshaphat, “Should you help the ungodly and love those who hate the Lord?”
9. What does that rebuke teach us?
a. That rebuke teaches us that there is no peace between the true church and the false church.
10. What gracious deliverance did God give to Jehoshaphat?
a. When Moab, Ammon, and Edom attacked Jehoshaphat, God turned those nations against each other, and they destroy each other.
Memory verse: “Ye shall not need to fight in this battle: set yourselves, stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD with you, O Judah and Jerusalem.”—2 Chronicles 20:17
1 Kings 22:1–38; 2 Kings 3; 2 Kings 8:16–18; 2 Chronicles 17–20
Lesson 14: Jehoshaphat
1. Whom did God send to prophesy against Ahab?
a. God sent Elijah the Tishbite, who shut the heavens so that it did not rain for three years and six months.
2. How did God preserve Elijah alive during the famine?
a. God sent Elijah to the brook Cherith and fed him by the ravens and then sent him to the widow of Zarephath.
3. What miracles did God perform for Elijah, the widow, and her son?
a. Their flour and oil never failed, and when the son died from a sickness, Elijah laid on the child three times and raised him from the dead.
4. How were the heavens opened again, so that it rained?
a. Elijah held a contest with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel to see who was God, Jehovah or Baal.
5. What was that contest?
a. The contest was to find out whether God or Baal could send fire from heaven to consume a sacrifice, and whoever sent the fire was the true God.
6. What did Elijah say when the prophets of Baal called on their god all day, but he did not answer?
a. Elijah mocked the Baal prophets and said that they should call louder because maybe Baal was talking, hunting, on a journey, or sleeping.
7. What did Elijah do after the prophets of Baal were finished calling on their god?
a. Elijah put his sacrifice on the altar of God and poured four barrels of water on the altar three times.
8. How did God answer Elijah’s prayer?
a. The fire of Jehovah fell from heaven and burned up the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and all the water.
9. What did Elijah do to 450 prophets of Baal and the 400 prophets of the grove?
a. Elijah killed them at the brook Kishon.
10. Did God send rain again?
a. Yes, Elijah prayed, and a powerful storm came from the sea with a great rain.
Memory verse: “Hear me, O LORD, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their hearts back again.”—1 Kings 18:37
1 Kings 17–18
Lesson 15: Elijah the Prophet (1)
1. What did Jezebel do when she heard from Ahab that Elijah had killed all the prophets of Baal?
a. Jezebel sent messengers to Elijah to say that she was going to kill Elijah by the next day.
2. Where did Elijah go?
a. Elijah traveled through Judah into the wilderness and asked that God would take away his life.
3. Where did Elijah go after that?
a. The angel of Jehovah came and gave Elijah some bread and water, and he traveled forty days and forty nights to Mount Horeb.
4. What did Elijah tell God?
a. Elijah said that Israel had forsaken God’s covenant, thrown down his altars, and killed his prophets and that Elijah was the only faithful one left.
5. What did God show Elijah?
a. God sent a strong wind that tore the mountain and broke the rocks, but God was not in the wind.
6. What did God send after the wind?
a. God sent an earthquake and then a fire, but God was not in them either.
7. How did God come to Elijah?
a. God came in a still small voice.
8. What did God command Elijah to do?
a. God commanded Elijah to anoint Hazael as king of Syria, Jehu as king of Israel, and Elisha as the next prophet.
9. How did God comfort Elijah?
a. God said that he had a remnant of seven thousand in Israel who had not bowed the knee to Baal.
10. What was that remnant?
a. It was the remnant according to the election of grace, for the Lord is faithful to his covenant.
Memory verse: “Even so then at this present time also there is a remnant according to the election of grace.”—Romans 11:5
1 Kings 19; 2 Kings 1
Lesson 16: Elijah the Prophet (2)
1. What was the glorious end of Elijah’s ministry as a prophet in Israel?
a. God took Elijah to heaven in a whirlwind.
2. Who became prophet after Elijah, and what did he ask from Elijah?
a. God appointed his servant Elisha to be prophet, who asked for a double portion of the Spirit to be upon him.
3. What notable miracle did Elisha perform first?
a. Elisha healed the waters of Jericho with salt in an earthen vessel.
4. What happened when the children of Bethel mocked Elisha?
a. Elisha cursed those children in the name of God, and two she bears came out of the woods and killed forty-two children.
5. Who came to Israel to be healed of leprosy?
a. Naaman the Syrian general came to Elisha.
6. What did Elisha tell Naaman to do?
a. Elisha told Naaman to wash seven times in the waters of the Jordan River.
7. Did Naaman do that?
a. Not at first, for Naaman was angry with Elisha, but Naaman’s servants convinced him to wash in the Jordan River.
8. What happened when Naaman washed in the Jordan River?
a. Naaman’s skin came out of the water new like a little child’s skin.
9. What happened to Elisha’s servant Gehazi when he took a present from Naaman?
a. The leprosy of Naaman came on Gehazi and his children forever.
10. Of whom was Naaman a type?
a. Naaman was a type of God’s salvation of the Gentiles in the new dispensation.
Memory verse: “In that day there shall be a root of Jesse, which shall stand for an ensign of the people; to it shall the Gentiles seek: and his rest shall be glorious.”—Isaiah 11:10
2 Kings 2–5
Lesson 17: Elisha the Prophet (1)
1. How did Elisha help the king of Israel in battle against the Syrians?
a. Elisha told the king of Israel where the Syrian army was camped.
2. What did the king of Syria do when he learned that Elisha had helped Israel?
a. The king of Syria sent his army to capture Elisha at Dothan.
3. When Elisha’s servant was afraid because the Syrian army surrounded the city of Dothan, what did Elisha ask God to do?
a. Elisha asked God to open his servant’s eyes, and the servant saw that the mountains around the city were full of horses and chariots of fire.
4. What did Elisha do to the Syrian army?
a. Elisha smote the Syrians with blindness and led them to the king of Israel at Samaria and made the king feed the Syrians and send them away.
5. What did the king of Syria do next?
a. The king of Syria besieged the city of Samaria, and there was a very great famine.
6. Whom did the king of Israel blame for the famine?
a. The king of Israel blamed Elisha and said that he would kill Elisha.
7. What did Elisha promise the king would happen the next day.
a. Elisha said that God would provide much food for the city.
8. What did Elisha tell the nobleman who did not believe Elisha’s promise?
a. The nobleman would see the food, but he would not eat of the food.
9. How did God drive away the Syrians?
a. God caused the Syrians to hear the noise of an army coming, and the Syrians fled.
10. What happened to the nobleman who did not believe?
a. When the inhabitants of Samaria went out to spoil the camp of the Syrians, the people trampled him, and he died.
Memory verse: “What shall we then say to these things? If God be for us, who can be against us?”—Romans 8:31
2 Kings 6–8; 2 Kings 13:14–21
Lesson 18: Elisha the Prophet (2)
1. Whom had God chosen as king of Israel to destroy the wicked house of Ahab?
a. God had chosen Jehu, so Elisha sent a young prophet to anoint Jehu as king of Israel.
2. Did Jehu wait on God to become king?
a. No, Jehu conspired against Ahab’s son, King Jehoram, and slew him with his bow and arrow.
3. After Jehu had killed King Jehoram, what did Jehu do with Jehoram’s body?
a. Jehu left Jehoram’s body in the inheritance of Naboth to be eaten by the birds, as God had said by Elijah the prophet.
4. What did Jehu tell the servants of Jezebel?
a. Jehu told Jezebel’s servants to throw her down out of a window, and Jehu ran over her with his horses and chariot.
5. When Jehu’s servants went to bury Jezebel, what did they find?
a. The servants found only a few parts of Jezebel because the dogs had eaten her, as God had said by Elijah the prophet.
6. Did Jehu leave any of Ahab’s house alive?
a. No, Jehu destroyed the whole house of Ahab, as God had said.
7. What else did Jehu do?
a. Jehu destroyed Baal worship by killing all the prophets, priests, and worshipers of Baal in the house of Baal in Samaria.
8. Was Jehu a righteous and godly king?
a. No, Jehu was a hypocrite, and he never departed from the sin of Jeroboam’s false worship of the golden calves.
9. Were there any godly kings in Israel after Jehu?
a. No, the kings of Israel were all wicked as God’s judgment on Israel because the nation had forsaken the house of David.
10. What was the end of the kingdom of Israel?
a. God sent the Assyrians against Israel, and the Assyrians carried away the people as captives and scattered them.
Memory verse: “Therefore the LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight: there was none left but the tribe of Judah only.”—2 Kings 17:18
2 Kings 9–10
Lesson 19: Jehu to the Assyrian Destruction of the Ten Tribes
1. What judgment did God send on Judah because Jehoshaphat had married his son Jehoram to Ahab’s daughter Athaliah?
a. After Jehoram and his son Ahaziah died, Athaliah killed all the seed royal and took the throne of David for herself.
2. How was the royal line preserved?
a. The aunt of baby Joash hid him.
3. Who was Joash’s aunt?
a. She was Jehoshaba, wife of the high priest Jehoiada.
4. Where was Joash safely kept for six years?
a. Joash was hidden in the temple.
5. What did the high priest Jehoiada do when Joash was seven?
a. Jehoiada secretly gathered all the priests to Jerusalem and proclaimed Joash king.
6. What did Jehoiada command concerning Athaliah?
a. Jehoiada commanded the guards to put Athaliah out of the temple and to kill her.
7. Was Joash a godly king?
a. Joash did what was right before God all the days of Jehoiada the priest.
8. What notable work took place during Joash’s reign?
a. The priests and Levites repaired the temple of God.
9. Why was it necessary to repair the temple?
a. The wicked sons of Athaliah had broken up God’s house and stolen its treasures.
10. What sin did Joash commit at the end of his reign?
a. When Zechariah the son of Jehoiada rebuked Joash for his idolatry, he had Zechariah stoned in the temple by the altar.

Memory work: “Howbeit the LORD would not destroy the house of David, because of the covenant that he had made with David, and as he promised to give a light to him and to his sons for ever.”—2 Chronicles 21:7
2 Kings 8:16–29; 2 Kings 9:16–29; 2 Kings 11–12; 2 Chronicles 21–24
Lesson 20: Joash
1. Who were the kings of Judah who followed Joash?
a. In order of their reigns, the kings of Judah were Amaziah, Uzziah, Jotham, and Ahaz.
2. What do we read of King Amaziah?
a. Amaziah did not serve God with a perfect heart.
3. How did Amaziah show that he did not serve God?
a. After God gave Judah the victory over Edom, Amaziah worshiped the gods of the Edomites.
4. How did God chastise Amaziah?
a. Amaziah was defeated in battle by the kingdom of Israel.
5. What do we read of Amaziah’s son Uzziah?
a. King Uzziah greatly strengthened the kingdom of Judah and defeated the nations around Judah.
6. What sin did King Uzziah commit?
a. Uzziah tried to offer incense in the temple, and God struck Uzziah with leprosy.
7. What do we read of King Jotham?
a. We read that “Jotham became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God.”
8. How did wicked King Ahaz bring trouble to the kingdom of Judah?
a. Ahaz forsook God and worshiped idols and even burned his children in sacrifice to the idol Moloch.
9. How did God judge Ahaz?
a. God gave Ahaz into the hands of the Syrians and the king of Israel.
10. To whom did Ahaz turn for help?
a. Ahaz paid tribute to the king of Assyria, who distressed Ahaz but gave no help to him.
11. What does the history of these kings of Judah demonstrate?
a. Their history shows that God was faithful to his covenant promise to David to give him a son always on the throne despite their unfaithfulness to God.

Memory verse: “I visit their transgression with a rod, and their iniquity with stripes. Nevertheless my lovingkindness will I not utterly take from him, nor suffer my faithfulness to fail.” Psalm 89:32–33
Lesson 21: Amaziah to Ahaz
1. Whom did God raise up to be king in Judah after Ahaz?
a. In his faithfulness and mercy, God gave Hezekiah, who loved the Lord.
2. What was the first act of King Hezekiah’s reign?
a. Hezekiah opened again the house of God that Ahaz had shut.
3. What did Hezekiah do when Judah celebrated the passover?
a. Hezekiah sent messengers into all Israel to call the people to celebrate the passover.
4. Did any people from Israel come?
a. The majority in Israel mocked the messengers, but some from the tribes of Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem.
5. Who came up to destroy Hezekiah and Jerusalem?
a. Sennacherib king of Assyria came with his army and besieged Jerusalem.
6. How did the king of Assyria try to discourage the people of Jerusalem?
a. Sennacherib sent his general Rabshakeh to frighten the people on the walls by saying that there was no hope for Judah.
7. How did Hezekiah comfort the people regarding Sennacherib?
a. Hezekiah told the people, “With him is an arm of flesh; but with us is the LORD our God to help, and to fight our battles.”
8. What did the king of Assyria say about God?
a. Sennacherib wrote letters to Hezekiah and railed against God as though he were but an idol.
9. How did God answer the king of Assyria?
a. The angel of Jehovah went out of Jerusalem that night and killed 185,000 Assyrians.
10. How did God answer Hezekiah when he prayed after God told him he would die?
a. God added fifteen years to Hezekiah’s life.

Memory verse: “Out of Jerusalem shall go forth a remnant, and they that escape out of mount Zion: the zeal of the LORD of hosts shall do this. For I will defend this city to save it for mine own sake, and for my servant David’s sake.”—Isaiah 37:32, 35
2 Kings 18–20; 2 Chronicles 29–32; Isaiah 10; Isaiah 36–39
Lesson 22: Hezekiah
1. Who followed Hezekiah on the throne of David in Judah?
a. Hezekiah was followed by his wicked son Manasseh.
2. What do we read of Manasseh?
a. Manasseh did more wickedly than the heathen whom God had cast out before Israel.
3. How did God chastise Manasseh?
a. The Assyrians took Manasseh and put him in prison where he humbled himself before God.
4. Was Manasseh’s son Amon like his father?
a. Yes, Amon did after all his father’s wickedness, but Amon did not humble himself.
5. Who became king after Amon?
a. God in his faithfulness gave Judah the God-fearing Josiah.
6. What did the priests find as they were cleansing the temple?
a. The priests found the book of the law and read it to Josiah.
7. What did Huldah the prophetess say to Josiah about what God would do to Jerusalem according to the book of the law?
a. Huldah said that God would perform all the curses that were written in the book of the law because Judah had forsaken God, but that would not happen in Josiah’s lifetime.
8. What did Josiah do throughout all the land of Israel?
a. Josiah destroyed all the idols and tore down their altars in the land.
9. What did Josiah do to the altar of the golden calves at Bethel?
a. Josiah burned the bones of the priests of the golden calves on the altar, as God had said.
10. How did Josiah die?
a. Josiah was killed in a battle between the kings of Egypt and Babylon.
Memory verse: “It is of the LORD’S mercies that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not. They are new every morning: great is thy faithfulness.”—Lamentations 3:22–23
1 Kings 13; 2 Kings 21–23:28; 2 Chronicles 33–35
Lesson 23: Manasseh to Josiah
1. Who became king in Judah after Josiah?
a. The people of Judah made Jehoahaz king, but Pharaoh took Jehoahaz prisoner.
2. Whom did Pharaoh make king?
a. Pharaoh appointed Jehoiakim as king to collect tribute for Pharaoh.
3. Whom did Jehoiakim serve after Pharaoh was defeated in battle?
a. Jehoiakim served Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon but later rebelled against him.
4. Who became king after Jehoiakim?
a. Jehoiakim’s very evil son Jehoiachin reigned.
5. What did Nebuchadnezzar do because of Judah’s rebellion?
a. Nebuchadnezzar took Jehoiachin, the princes, mighty men, and skilled craftsman as captives to Babylon.
6. Whom did Nebuchadnezzar make king in place of Jehoiachin?
a. Nebuchadnezzar made Zedekiah king and made him swear an oath to serve the king of Babylon.
7. Did Zedekiah keep his oath?
a. No, Zedekiah rebelled, and Nebuchadnezzar came and burned the city of Jerusalem and the house of God and removed all but a few of the people.
8. Who was the prophet of God to Judah at that time?
a. Jeremiah was the prophet, and he said that the people of Judah would be captives in Babylon for seventy years.
9. What did the destruction of Jerusalem make plain?
a. Jerusalem’s destruction showed that Israel had not kept the law of God, so that the promise could not have come through Israel’s obedience.
10. Was the destruction of Jerusalem the end of the house of David and of God’s promise?
a. No, it was God's sovereign purpose to fulfill his promise in Jesus Christ, who is David’s greater son.
Memory verse: “The LORD hath sworn in truth unto David; he will not turn from it; Of the fruit of thy body will I set upon thy throne.”—Psalm 132:11
2 Kings 23:29–37; 2 Kings 24–25; 2 Chronicles 36
Lesson 24: The Destruction of Jerusalem
1. Who were among the captives taken to Babylon?
a. Daniel was taken to Babylon with his three friends—Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego.
2. What dream did Daniel interpret for Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon?
a. Nebuchadnezzar saw a dream of a great image whose head was gold, its chest and arms were silver, its belly and thighs were brass, its legs were iron, and its feet were part iron and part clay.
3. What happened to that image?
a. A stone cut without hands from a mountain struck the image and destroyed it.
4. What did that vision mean?
a. Nebuchadnezzar and his kingdom was represented by the head of gold, and after him would come other kingdoms, each represented by a different metal.
5. What is that stone cut without hands that Nebuchadnezzar saw?
a. The stone is the kingdom of Jesus Christ that will break in pieces the antichristian kingdoms of the world.
6. How did Nebuchadnezzar oppose God’s word in the vision?
a. Nebuchadnezzar made a copy of the image that he had seen, but he made the whole image of gold.
7. What did Nebuchadnezzar command his servants?
a. Nebuchadnezzar commanded all his servants to worship his image; if they would not, he would cast them into the burning fiery furnace.
8. What did Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego tell the king when they refused to worship his image?
a. They said, “Our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace.”
9. What did Nebuchadnezzar do?
a. Nebuchadnezzar ordered that the furnace be made seven times hotter, and then he cast Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego into the furnace.
10. What did Nebuchadnezzar see when he looked into the furnace?
a. Nebuchadnezzar saw Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego walking around in the furnace unharmed, and he saw another one who looked like the Son of God.
Memory verse: “All that dwell upon the earth shall worship [the beast], whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world.”—Revelation 13:8
Daniel 1–6
Lesson 25: Daniel
1. Who led the return of the remnant from captivity in Babylon?
a. The return was led by Zerubbabel as prince of the house of David.
2. What was the remnant’s calling when they returned to Jerusalem?
a. The remnant was commanded to rebuild the temple of God.
3. Whom did God provide to encourage the remnant in that work?
a. God raised up the prophets Haggai and Zechariah.
4. Of what was the rebuilding of the temple a type?
a. Rebuilding the temple was a type of the coming of Christ and the gathering of God’s church from all the nations.
5. What did those in the land who opposed the people of Judah and the building of the temple do?
a. Those who opposed wrote letters to the kings of Persia, asking them to stop the work of building.
6. Who came to Jerusalem after the temple was built to teach the people the law?
a. God raised up Ezra the priest to teach the people.
7. Whom did God raise up to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem?
a. God sent Nehemiah from Babylon to be governor of the people.
8. Were there enemies who opposed that work of rebuilding the walls?
a. Yes, especially Sanballat and Tobiah tried in many ways to make the work stop.
9. What did the rebuilding of the walls of Jerusalem signify?
a. Rebuilding the walls signified that the church dwells in safety in antithetical separation from the false church.
10. What sin manifested itself among the people who returned from captivity?
a. There were many mixed marriages between the people of Judah and the nations around Judah.
Memory verse: “Save us, O LORD our God, and gather us from among the heathen, to give thanks unto thy holy name, and to triumph in thy praise.”—Psalm 106:47
Ezra and Nehemiah
Lesson 26: The Remnant Returns: Zerubbabel, Ezra, Nehemiah
1. Where does the history in the book of Esther take place?
a. The book of Esther reveals that the events took place in the palace of Ahasuerus, king of Persia.
2. What is significant about the book of Esther?
a. The book of Esther teaches God’s sovereignty over the hearts of all men for the good of his church.
3. What did Mordecai the Jew do with his cousin Esther?
a. Mordecai sent Esther to the king of Persia so that she could try to become queen.
4. What did Esther do when she became queen?
a. Esther did not tell anyone that she was a Jew.
5. Why did Haman try to destroy all the Jews?
a. Haman hated Mordecai, who was a Jew, because he would not bow to Haman.
6. What did Haman build to kill Mordecai?
a. Haman constructed large gallows to hang Mordecai
7. What did Mordecai tell Esther she had to do?
a. Mordecai told Esther to tell the king of Persia that she was a Jew and to plead for the life of her people.
8. What happened to Haman when he went to ask the king for permission to destroy Mordecai?
a. The king told Haman to honor Mordecai by leading him through the city on the king’s horse.
9. What did the king of Persia do when he learned that Esther was a Jew?
a. The king was enraged at Haman and had him hanged on the gallows that Haman had made to hang Mordecai.
10. How did the King Ahasuerus deliver the Jews?
a. The king sent out decrees to all his kingdom that the Jews could defend themselves against their enemies.
Memory verse: “Our God is in the heavens: he hath down whatsoever he hath pleased.”— Psalm 115:3
Esther
Lesson 27: Esther
Old Testament History
Grades 5–8
David Anointed King to Esther
1. How does the apostle John begin his account of the gospel of Jesus Christ?
a. “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same in the beginning was with God.”
2. Who is the Word?
a. The Word is Jesus Christ, the second person of the Trinity, the Son of God.
3. What does it mean that Jesus Christ is the Word?
a. It means that Jesus Christ is God and the perfect expression of God.
4. What does John mean when he says that Jesus Christ is Light?
a. John means that in Jesus Christ is the fullness of God’s covenant life.
5. What does John tell us about men?
a. John says that all men are darkness, so that they are totally depraved and enemies of God.
6. What power does the Word give to some men?
a. The Word makes them sons of God who are born not of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God.
7. What does John tell us about the Word?
a. The Word was made flesh and dwelt among us.
8. What does it mean that the Word was made flesh?
a. Being made flesh is the incarnation of Jesus Christ when the Word took flesh from the virgin Mary and was made a man.
9. What was the purpose of the incarnation?
a. Jesus Christ was incarnated so that he could declare to his people who God is and reveal all God’s purposes.
10. What does John mean when he says, “The law came by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ”?
a. John teaches that the law was but a shadow of all the gracious promises that are true and fulfilled in Christ.
Memory verse: “No man hath seen God at any time, the only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, he hath declared him.”—John 1:18
John 1:1–18
Lesson 1: In the Beginning Was the Word
1. Where did Jesus go after his baptism?
a. Jesus with his disciples attended a wedding feast in Cana of Galilee.
2. What did Jesus’ attendance at the wedding teach us?
a. We learn that Jesus Christ is a husband to his church, and he highly honors marriage with his presence, gifts, and miracles.
3. What did Mary, Jesus’ mother, tell him at the wedding feast?
a. Mary told Jesus that there was no more wine.
4. Why did Jesus tell Mary that his hour was not yet come?
a. Jesus said that because Mary was hoping that Jesus would reveal himself as the Christ at that moment.
5. When Jesus spoke of his hour, what did he point to?
a. Jesus pointed to his cross where he would accomplish the miracle of our redemption.
6. What did Jesus tell the servants who were at the wedding feast?
a. Jesus told the servants to fill six water pots with water and to bring them to the governor of the feast.
7. What did the governor say after he had tasted the wine?
a. The governor asked the groom, “Why did you serve the good wine last?”
8. What does scripture say concerning Jesus’ miracle of changing water into wine?
a. Scripture says that this was the beginning of miracles that Jesus did.
9. What does the beginning of miracles mean?
a. Changing water into wine pointed to the work of Jesus by his cross and resurrection to change the misery of the earthly into the joy of the heavenly marriage supper of the Lamb.
10. What does scripture say concerning Jesus’ disciples when they saw that miracle?
a. Scripture says that Jesus had manifested his glory, and the disciples believed on him.
Memory verse: “As we have borne the image of the earthy, we shall also bear the image of the heavenly.”—1 Corinthians 15:49
John 2:1–11
Lesson 2: Jesus at the Wedding Feast in Cana
1. Where did Jesus go after he had labored for some time in Galilee?
a. Jesus went to Jerusalem at the time of the passover.
2. What did Jesus find in the temple?
a. Jesus found men selling oxen, sheep, and doves and men exchanging money.
3. What did Jesus do?
a. Jesus made a whip out of cords and drove out the sellers and their oxen, sheep, and doves and threw over the tables of the money exchangers.
4. What did Jesus tell those who sold the doves?
a. “Take these things away, and do not make my Father’s house a house of merchandise.”
5. To what was Jesus pointing when he said that the buyers and sellers made God’s house a house of merchandise?
a. Jesus was pointing to their doctrine, which taught that man works for his righteousness before God as though salvation is sold for a price.
6. What did Jesus’ disciples remember?
a. The disciples remembered that God had prophesied through David, “The zeal of thine house has eaten me up.”
7. What does that passage mean?
a. It means that Jesus was zealous that the glory of God alone shine out in his house.
8. What did Jesus tell those who demanded a sign of his authority to rule in God’s house?
a. Jesus said, “Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.”
9. What did Jesus mean by that?
a. Jesus spoke of his body, which is the true temple of God that the Jews would destroy at the cross, but he would raise from the dead the third day.
10. What did Jesus mean when he spoke of his body?
a. Jesus meant that in him righteousness is a free gift of God by which Jesus Christ builds God’s elect church into a holy habitation of God by his Spirit.
Memory verse: “The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up; and the reproaches of them that reproached thee are fallen upon me.”—Psalm 69:9
Lesson 3 The Cleansing of the Temple
1. What does scripture say about many in Jerusalem when they saw Jesus’ miracles?
a. Scripture says that they believed on Jesus and that he did not believe them because he knew what was in their hearts.
2. How was such a false faith illustrated?
a. It was shown in Nicodemus, a Pharisee and a ruler of the Jews, who came to Jesus by night.
3. How did Jesus respond when Nicodemus confessed that no man can do the miracles that Jesus did except God is with him?
a. Jesus said that no man can see the kingdom of God except he is born again.
4. Did Nicodemus understand what Jesus meant?
a. No, Jesus had to explain to Nicodemus that being born again is the work of God by his Spirit to cleanse a man from sin and to bring him into God’s kingdom.
5. To what did Jesus compare this work of the Spirit when Nicodemus could not comprehend what Jesus was saying?
a. Jesus said that the power of the Spirit in making sons of God is like the wind.
6. What accounts for this work of God to bring us into his kingdom?
a. We are brought in God’s kingdom because he forgave all our sins and accounted us righteous before him and worthy of eternal life without our works but by faith alone.
7. How did Jesus reprove Nicodemus’ proud unbelief?
a. Jesus said that Nicodemus was a teacher in Israel, but he did not know those simple, earthly things.
8. What did Jesus mean when he said that no man has ascended into heaven but he who came down from heaven?
a. Jesus was addressing Nicodemus’ unbelief in such a free righteousness without works.
9. How did Jesus explain the accomplishment of this free righteousness?

a. Jesus said that as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so the Son of man must be lifted up on the cross in order that whosoever believes in him should not perish but have everlasting life.
10. To what was Jesus referring when he said that the Son of Man had to be lifted up?
a. Jesus was referring to God’s eternal purpose in love to save his creation with the elect church at its heart at the cross of Christ.
11. What did Jesus tell Nicodemus about faith and unbelief in the Son of God?
a. Jesus said that some believe because there is no condemnation to them from eternity, and others do not believe because they are condemned from eternity.
Memory verse: “God so loved the world that he gave his only-begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.”—John 3:16
John 3:1–21
Lesson 4: The Visit of Nicodemus by Night
1. What do we read concerning Jesus as he returned to Galilee from Judea?
a. We read that Jesus had to go through Samaria.
2. Why was it necessary for Jesus to go through Samaria?
a. Jesus had to gather from the Samaritans those whom God had elected and ordained to eternal life.
3. Where did Jesus stop on his journey?
a. Jesus stopped in Sychar at Jacob’s well, and being weary he sat down on the well and sent his disciples away to buy food.
4. What did Jesus tell a Samaritan woman who came to draw water from the well?
a. Jesus said, “Whoever drinks from Jacob’s well will thirst again, but whoever drinks of the water that I give will never thirst but have in him a well of water springing up to everlasting life.”
5. What is that well of water?
a. The well of water is the Spirit of Christ who gives life to the elect and brings Christ’s righteousness and the true life of God’s covenant fellowship to them.
6. How did Jesus expose the woman’s need for that living water?
a. Jesus taught her that her life was carnal because she had been married to five husbands and was living with yet another man.
7. In what other way did Jesus reveal the source of her carnal life?
a. Jesus told her that she was ignorant of God, but the hour was coming when the true worshiper of God would worship him in spirit and in truth.
8. What did the woman do when she returned to the city?
a. She told everyone that she had met a man who told her all she ever did and that the man was the Christ.
9. What did Jesus do when the Samaritans asked him to stay?
a. Jesus stayed two days in Samaria, and many believed by the power of his word.
10. What did Jesus tell his disciples when they tried to get him to eat?
a. Jesus said, “My meat is to do the will of him who sent me, and to finish his work.”
11. What is the will and work of God?
a. The will and work of God are the salvation and gathering of his elect church from all nations, which Christ pictured for the disciples as a field ripe for harvest.
Memory verse: “God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.”—John 4:24
John 4:1–42
Lesson 5: Jesus at Jacob’s Well
1. Where did Jesus go after he left Samaria?
a. Jesus went back to Cana of Galilee, where he had made water into wine.
2. What does scripture teach about the Galileans’ reception of Jesus Christ?
a. Scripture teaches that their reception was not of true faith but was because they saw the signs and wonders that Jesus had performed in Jerusalem at the feast.
3. Who heard that Jesus was in Galilee and came to him?
a. A nobleman from Capernaum came to Jesus and pleaded with him to come to Capernaum to heal his son who was about to die.
4. What did Jesus say to the nobleman?
a. Jesus said, “Except you see signs and wonders, you will not believe.”
5. What did that rebuke mean?
a. It was a rebuke of all the Galileans who did not believe the doctrine of Jesus, to which the signs and wonders pointed.
6. What did the nobleman say to Jesus?
a. The nobleman said, “Lord, come down ere my child die!”
7. How did that reveal the nobleman’s faith?
a. He called Jesus “Lord,” and no one does that apart from faith.
8. What did Jesus say to the nobleman?
a. Jesus said, “Your son lives.”
9. How did the nobleman man demonstrate his faith?
a. He did not travel home immediately but went home the next day.
10. What did the nobleman learn about his son when he arrived home?
a. He learned that his son had been healed the day before about the seventh hour when Jesus had said, “Your son lives.”
11. What effect did that word of Jesus have on the nobleman and his house?
a. Jesus spoke and worked faith in the hearts of the nobleman and his whole household.
Memory verse: The mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting upon them that fear him, and his righteousness unto children’s children.—Psalm 103:17
Lesson 6: The Healing of the Nobleman’s Son in Capernaum
1. Whom did Jesus heal at the pool of Bethesda?
a. Jesus healed a man who had been lame for thirty-eight years.
2. Why did Jesus seek the man to heal him?
a. Jesus sought an occasion against the Jews, who hated him and did not believe in him.
3. Why did the Jews persecute Jesus and seek to slay him?
a. The Jews sought to slay Jesus because he had healed the lame man on the Sabbath.
4. What did Jesus answer the Jews when they accused him of breaking the Sabbath?
a. Jesus said, “My Father works hitherto, and I work.”
5. What did Jesus mean by that?
a. Jesus meant that he is God and coequal with God.
6. What did that imply for Jesus’ healing on the Sabbath?
a. It meant that Jesus’ healing was a work of God and therefore not a violation of the Sabbath.
7. Why did Jesus turn from the matter of working on the Sabbath to the truth of who he is?
a. Jesus turned to who he is because the Jews used Jesus’ work on the Sabbath to deny that he is the Christ.
8. What did Jesus say about those who believe in him?
a. Jesus said that believers have everlasting life and will not come into condemnation.
9. What authority did the Father give to Jesus as the Son of man?
a. The Father gave to Jesus the authority to judge.
10. What did Jesus tell the Jews concerning the witness of his works?
a. Jesus said that his works testify that he is from God.
11. How did Jesus judge the Jews?
a. Jesus said that the Jews did not believe in him because they did not have God’s love nor his word in their hearts.
12. What did Jesus say about the Jews’ who claimed to trust in Moses and the scriptures?
a. Because the Jews did not believe in Jesus, he called them unbelievers in Moses and the scriptures, for the scriptures speak of Jesus and not of man’s works.
Memory verse: “Search the scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which testify of me.”—John 5:39
John 5
Lesson 7: Healing the Lame Man at the Pool of Bethesda
1. Where did Jesus go after he heard about the death of John the Baptist?
a. Jesus went into a desert area by himself near the city of Bethsaida.
2. What miracle did Jesus perform in the desert area for the multitudes who had followed him?
a. With five loaves and two fish, Jesus fed five thousand men besides women and children.
3. What did the people want to do?
a. They wanted to take Jesus by force and make him their king.
4. What did Jesus tell the people when they came to him the next day?
a. Jesus told them that they sought him only because their bellies had been filled, but they had to labor for the meat that endures unto everlasting life.
5. Who is the meat that endures to everlasting life?
a. That meat is Jesus Christ as the bread of life, who came down from heaven to save those whom the Father elected.
6. How did Jesus explain the murmuring of the people and their unbelief in him?
a. Jesus said, “No man can come to me, except the Father who has sent me, draw him.”
7. What was Jesus’ explanation of the bread that he gives?
a. Jesus said that the bread is his flesh, and whoever eats his flesh and drinks his blood has everlasting life.
8. How does Jesus give us his flesh to eat?
a. He gives us his flesh by the work of his Spirit.
9. What does it mean to eat and drink Jesus Christ?
a. We eat and drink Christ by faith and we are more and more united to him.
10. What did the multitude do after Jesus had finished speaking about himself as the bread of life?
a. The people all left Jesus and followed him no more.
11. What did Peter say when Jesus asked if the disciples also wanted to go away?
a. Peter said, “Lord, to whom shall we go? Thou hast the words of eternal life.”

Memory verse: “I am the living bread which came down from heaven: if any man eat of this bread, he shall live for ever: and the bread that I will give is my flesh, which I will give for the life of the world.”—John 6:51
Lesson 8: Feeding the Five Thousand
1. Why did Jesus remain in Galilee for a time and not go to Jerusalem to celebrate the feast of tabernacles?
a. Jesus did not go to Jerusalem because the Jews sought to kill him, and his time was not yet.
2. How did Jesus reprove his unbelieving brethren when they mocked him for not going to Jerusalem?
a. Jesus told his brethren that the false church could not hate them and that his brethren could be on friendly terms with the world because they were spiritually one with the world.
3. Why did the Jews of the false church hate Jesus?
a. They hated Jesus because he testified against them that all their works were evil and that salvation was found in him alone.
4. Why did Jesus go later to the feast of tabernacles?
a. Jesus went to teach and to reveal the division that was among the people because of him.
5. What did Jesus tell the Jews at that time?
a. Jesus said that Moses had given the law to the Jews, and none of them kept it.
6. What was the feast of tabernacles?
a. The feast of tabernacles was a celebration of the grace of God that had separated his people from Egypt, preserved them in the wilderness, and given to them their promised inheritance.
7. What was the Jews’ custom at the feast of tabernacles?
a. At that feast the Jews remembered that God had given them water from the rock in the wilderness.
8. What did Jesus cry in the temple at the feast?
a. Jesus cried, “If any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink.”
9. What did Jesus promise to one who believes on him?
a. Jesus promised, “Out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water.”
10. Who is that living water of whom Jesus spoke?
a. Jesus referred to the Spirit, whom Jesus would give in fulfillment of the covenant promise.
11. What was the result of Jesus’ appearance at the feast?
a. The result was a division among the people because of him.
Memory verse: “This spake he of the Spirit, which they that believe on him should receive: for the Holy Ghost was not yet given; because that Jesus was not yet glorified.”—John 7:39
John 7
Lesson 9: The River of Living Water
1. Whom did the Pharisees bring to Jesus as he taught the multitudes in the temple?
a. The Pharisees brought to Jesus a woman whom they had caught in the act of adultery.
2. How did the Pharisees tempt Jesus, hoping to accuse him?
a. The Pharisees said to Jesus, “Moses in the law commanded that such be stoned, but what do you say?”
3. What did Jesus do?
a. Jesus said nothing, and he stooped and wrote on the ground with his finger.
4. Of what was that writing on the ground a sign?
a. That writing was a sign of the curse of the law against all who break the law.
5. What did Jesus say after the Pharisees kept pressing him?
a. Jesus said, “He that is without sin among you, let him cast the first stone at her.”
6. What happened to the Pharisees, scribes, and the people gathered around Jesus and the woman?
a. They were convicted in their consciences, and they went out one by one until only Jesus and the woman were left.
7. What was that conviction in their consciences?
a. It was the conviction of the law that they had transgressed all the commandments and kept none of them.
8. What did the woman answer when Jesus asked if any man still accused her?
a. She said, “No man, Lord.”
9. What did Jesus say to the woman?
a. Jesus said, “Neither do I condemn thee: go and sin no more.”
10. What did Jesus mean when he said “neither do I condemn thee”?
a. Jesus meant that he had justified her and forgiven all her sins.
11. By “go and sin no more,” did Jesus mean that the woman should never sin again?
a. No, Jesus meant that she was to live in peace out of her gracious justification in Christ.
Memory verse: “I say unto thee, Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little.”—Luke 7:47
John 8:1–11
Lesson 10: The Woman Taken in Adultery
1. What did Jesus say as he stood in the treasury of the temple among the multitudes?
a. Jesus said, “I am the light of the world.”
2. What did Jesus say is true of all who follow him?
a. All who follow Jesus will not walk in darkness but will have the light of life.
3. On what occasion did Jesus say that?
a. Jesus said that when the Jews were celebrating a feast and had filled the temple with light from large candles.
4. Who is light?
a. God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all.
5. What does it mean that God is light?
a. That God is light means that in God alone is perfect knowledge and life.
6. What did Jesus mean when he said, “I am light”?
a. Jesus meant that he is God, and he gives the knowledge of God and the life of God’s covenant to his people.
7. How does Jesus give the knowledge of God and life in his covenant to his people?
a. Jesus freely forgives their sins and guilt by faith only.
8. What does Jesus’ statement that he is light imply concerning men?
a. It implies that for those outside Jesus Christ there is only the ignorance and darkness of sin and death because all men are guilty.
9. Did the Jews receive this teaching from Jesus?
a. No, the Jews said that his testimony about himself was not true.
10. What did Jesus say concerning his testimony?
a. Jesus said that in the law the testimony of two witnesses is true, and both he and the Father testify to the truth concerning Christ.
11. What did Jesus testify against the Jews for their unbelief?
a. Jesus said that despite the Jews’ claims to know the Father, they did not know Jesus Christ, so they did not know the Father either.
Memory verse: “Then spake Jesus again unto them, saying, I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life.”—John 8:12
Lesson 11: I Am the Light of the World
1. Did many who believed on Jesus as he taught have genuine faith?
a. Their faith was not genuine, but they believed on Jesus only according to their own carnal conceptions.
2. What did Jesus say to them concerning his word?
a. Jesus said, “If you continue in my word, then you are my disciples, and you shall know the truth, and the truth shall set you free.”
3. What did the Jews respond?
a. The Jews said, “We are Abraham’s seed and were never in bondage to any man.”
4. How did Jesus expose that the Jews indeed were in bondage?
a. He told the Jews that whoever commits sin is the servant of sin.
5. How were the Jews in bondage?
a. The Jews were outside Christ and thus in bondage to the law, wrath, sin, and death.
6. What was the freedom about which Jesus spoke?
a. Jesus spoke of the justified believer’s freedom from the condemnation of the law and eternal life in the Spirit as a son of God free from the law, sin, and death.
7. What did Jesus say about those who did not believe on him?
a. Jesus said that their father was the devil, a murderer and that they did the devil’s works and went about to kill Jesus.
8. What did Jesus say of Abraham?
a. Jesus said, “Abraham rejoiced to see my day, and he saw it and was glad.”
9. How did Jesus respond to the Jews who said that Jesus was not yet fifty years old so he could not have met Abraham?
a. Jesus said, “Before Abraham was I am.”
10. What did Jesus mean?
a. Jesus meant that he is the eternal and unchangeable I AM THAT I AM.
11. What did the Jews do to Jesus.
a. The Jews picked up stones to stone him, but he passed through the midst of them.
Memory verse: “Jesus said unto them; Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am.”—John 8:58
Lesson 12: Before Abraham Was I Am
1. Of whom was the man who was born blind a type?
a. The blind man was a type of every man who is conceived and born dead in trespasses and sins.
2. What did Jesus mean when he said that the man was born blind so that the works of God should be made manifest in him?
a. Jesus meant that God determined that the whole human race would fall into sin in order to show that salvation is of God.
3. How did Jesus give sight to the man?
a. Jesus spit on the ground, made clay, anointed the man’s eyes, and told him to wash in the pool of Siloam.
4. Of what was that clay a sign?
a. The clay was a sign of the sovereignty of God as the potter, who has power to make one vessel to honor and another to dishonor.
5. Of what was the washing in the pool of Siloam a sign?
a. That washing was a sign of the power of Christ’s blood by the application of the Spirit to forgive sins and to make the spiritually blind to see.
6. What did the Jews say about Jesus after that miracle?
a. The Jews said that since Jesus had broken the law by healing the man on the Sabbath, Jesus could not be from God.
7. Did the Jews believe that the man had been born blind?
a. No, not until they called his parents to ask them who the man was.
8. What had the Jews already agreed upon about Jesus?
a. The Jews had agreed that if any man confessed that Jesus was the Christ, he would be cast out of the synagogue.
9. What did the Jews do to the man when he confessed that Jesus Christ was of God?
a. The Jews reviled the man and cast him out of the synagogue.
10. How did Jesus explain the meaning of the miracle?
a. Jesus said, “For judgment I am come into the world that those who see not might see and that those who see might be made blind.”
11. What did Jesus mean when he said that he came for judgment?
a. Jesus meant that by the forgiveness of sins he delivers men from their darkness and spiritual blindness according to God’s decree of predestination.
12. Who are those who see whom Jesus makes not to see?
a. They are those who are confident in their own works for salvation.
Memory verse: “Therefore hath he mercy on whom he will have mercy, and whom he will he hardeneth.”—Romans 9:18
John 9
Lesson 13: Healing the Man Born Blind
1. What did Jesus mean when he said, “I am the door of the sheepfold”?
a. Jesus meant that he alone has the power to bring into the fellowship of the kingdom of God and to shut out of the kingdom of God.
2. What did Jesus say about all those who teach any other way into God’s kingdom?
a. Jesus said that they are nothing but thieves and robbers who come to steal, kill, and destroy.
3. What else did Jesus say about himself regarding his relationship to his sheep?
a. He said, “I am the good shepherd.”
4. What does it mean that Jesus Christ is the good shepherd?
a. It means that God has given the sheep to Christ and that he is responsible for their salvation.
5. What does Jesus Christ as the good shepherd do for his sheep?
a. He lays down his life for his sheep and gathers them into the sheepfold.
6. How does Jesus gather his sheep?
a. Jesus gathers his sheep by his Spirit through the preaching of the gospel.
7. Who are Christ’s sheep?
a. His sheep are all the elect that God has appointed to eternal life in Christ.
8. What do the elect do when they hear the preaching of the gospel?
a. The elect hear Christ speak to them in the preaching, and they follow him.
9. Why did the Jews did not believe in Jesus Christ?
a. The Jews did not believe because they were not of Christ’s sheep.
10. What did Jesus teach about himself and his Father?
a. Jesus said, “I and my Father are one.”
11. What was the response of the Jews to Jesus’ teaching?
a. The Jews took up stones to stone him for blasphemy.

Memory verse: “Ye believe not, because ye are not of my sheep, as I said unto you. My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me: and I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.”—John 10:26–28
John 10
Lesson 14: I Am the Door; I Am the Good Shepherd
1. What news reached Jesus when he was in Perea?
a. Messengers told Jesus that his friend Lazarus was sick.
2. Why did Jesus wait to go to Lazarus?
a. Jesus waited until Lazarus had died so that he could raise Lazarus from the dead.
3. What did Jesus tell Martha about himself at that time?
a. Jesus said, “I am the resurrection and the life: he that believes in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live.”
4. What was Jesus’ response when he saw Mary and many of the Jews weeping at the tomb?
a. Jesus groaned in the spirit and was troubled.
5. Why did Jesus pray aloud to his Father before raising Lazarus?
a. Jesus prayed aloud so that the people around would hear and know that God had sent him.
6. What did Jesus do at the tomb of Lazarus?
a. Jesus wept.
7. How did Jesus raise Lazarus from the dead?
a. In a loud voice Jesus commanded, “Lazarus, come forth.”
8. What was the response of the people to that miracle?
a. Many who had come to comfort Mary and Martha believed on Jesus.
9. What did the chief priests and Pharisees do when they heard of that miracle?
a. The chief priests and Pharisees took counsel to put Jesus to death.
10. What did Jesus do after raising Lazarus?
a. Jesus walked no more openly among the Jews, and he went to the city of Ephraim.

Memory verse: “Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: and whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die.”—John 11:25–26
John 11
Lesson 15: I Am the Resurrection and the Life
1. What controversy about Jesus arose among the Jews?
a. Many Jews wondered whether Jesus would come to Jerusalem to celebrate the feast of passover.
2. How did Jesus come to Jerusalem?
a. Jesus came riding on the colt of a donkey.
3. How did the multitudes respond to Jesus’ royal entry in Jerusalem?
a. The people took palm branches, went to meet Jesus, and shouted, “Hosanna, blessed is the king of Israel who comes in the name of the Lord.”
4. Why did the multitudes come out to meet Jesus?
a. The people heard that Jesus had raised Lazarus from the dead.
5. What did the Pharisees and chief priests decide to do?
a. The Pharisees and chief priests took counsel to put Lazarus to death because the whole world was going after Christ.
6. About what did Jesus speak to his disciples when certain Greeks at the feast wanted to see him?
a. Jesus spoke of his death and resurrection.
7. What did Jesus say concerning his death and resurrection?
a. Jesus, said, “If I be lifted up from the earth, I will draw all men unto me.”
8. What was Jesus teaching when he spoke of being lifted up?
a. Jesus was teaching that he must die the accursed death of the cross.
9. What does John teach concerning the unbelief of many Jews even though Jesus had performed so many miracles?
a. John said that the Jews’ unbelief fulfilled this prophecy of Isaiah: “Lord, who has believed our report?”
10. How does John explain the Jews’ unbelief?
a. John says that the Jews could not believe because Isaiah had said, “God has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts.”
11. Was it a discredit to Christ that many did not believe on him?
a. No, Isaiah had prophesied these things when he saw the glory of Christ.
Memory verse: “He hath blinded their eyes, and hardened their heart; that they should not see with their eyes, nor understand with their heart, and be converted, and I should heal them.”— John 12:40
John 12
Lesson 16: The Royal Entry
1. What does scripture say about Jesus the night in which he was betrayed?
a. Scripture says that having loved his own who were in the world, Jesus loved them to the end.
2. What did Jesus do to his disciples as a sign of his love?
a. Jesus wrapped himself in a towel, poured water into a basin, and began to wash his disciples’ feet.
3. What did Jesus tell Peter when he refused a foot washing by Jesus?
a. Jesus said to Peter, “If I do not wash you, you have no part in me.”
4. Why did Jesus tell his disciples that not all of them were clean?
a. Jesus said that because he knew that Judas Iscariot would betray him.
5. What instruction did Jesus give to his disciples after he had washed their feet?
a. Jesus instructed, “If I your Lord and Master has washed your feet, you ought to wash one another’s feet.”
6. What did Jesus mean by that instruction?
a. Jesus meant that we must follow his example of love and love one another as servants to each other.
7. What sign did Jesus give to John to indicate who would betray Jesus?
a. The sign was that Jesus would give a sop to the betrayer.
8. What prophecy did Jesus say was fulfilled by giving the sop?
a. David has prophesied, “He who eats bread with me has lifted up his heel against me.”
9. How did Jesus explain Judas’ betrayal?
a. Jesus explained that he knew those whom he had chosen.
10. What did Jesus tell Peter when he boasted that he would lay down his life for Jesus?
a. Jesus said, “The cock shall not crow, till you have denied me thrice.”
Memory verse: “A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.”—John 13:34
John 13
Lesson 17: Jesus in the Upper Room
1. What did Jesus instruct his disciples concerning his going away and coming glory?
a. Jesus taught his disciples that he was going away to prepare a place for his people in his Father’s house of many mansions.
2. Who is the Father of Jesus Christ?
a. The Father of Jesus Christ is the triune God.
3. What is the Father’s house of many mansions?
a. The Father’s house of many mansions is the perfection of our salvation in the eternal covenant of grace in heaven.
4. Why did Jesus describe the perfection of the covenant as a house?
a. Jesus did so because God’s covenant is a relationship of fellowship and friendship between God and his elect people in Christ.
5. What did Thomas ask Jesus about the way to glory?
a. Thomas asked, “How can we know the way?”
6. What did Jesus respond to Thomas?
a. Jesus said, “I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man comes to the Father but by me.”
7. What did Jesus mean by that response?
a. Jesus meant that he alone is the way of salvation.
8. What did Jesus exclude from that way of salvation?
a. Jesus excluded all man’s willing and working for salvation.
9. What did Jesus say about his words and works?
a. Jesus said that he did not speak and do them himself, but the Father who dwells in Christ speaks the words and does the works.
10. What did Jesus say about the relationship between himself and his Father?
a. Jesus said, “I am in the Father, and the Father is in me.”
11. What does that relationship mean for our fellowship with God?
a. It means that we must be engrafted into Jesus Christ by a true faith and that in him we have fellowship with the Father.

Memory verse: “In my Father’s house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also.”—John 14:2–3
Lesson 18: I Am the Way
1. What did Jesus mean when he said, “I am the true vine”?
a. Jesus meant that he is the source and power of all our life.
2. How did Jesus describe his elect people in relationship to himself as the vine?
a. Jesus said that we are the branches that abide in him.
3. What does the figure of the vine and the branches teach us?
a. The figure teaches us that our fellowship is with Christ and that we receive all things necessary for salvation in him.
4. What do we call this union between Christ and his elect people?
a. This union is faith, which is the gift of God.
5. How did Jesus refer to his Father?
a. Jesus called his Father the husbandman.
6. What was Jesus teaching when he told us to abide in him?
a. Jesus was teaching that apart from himself we have no life in us and can do nothing.
7. Who keeps us in communion with Jesus Christ?
a. God by the power of his grace keeps us in communion with Christ.
8. To whom did Jesus refer when he spoke of branches that did not abide in him?
a. Jesus referred to the reprobate, who for a time appear to have a part in Christ.
9. What is the fruit of our union with Jesus Christ?
a. By the power of Christ in us, we bear much fruit.
10. What did Jesus give as the reason for our union with him?
a. We are united to Christ because he has chosen us and ordained us so that we bear much fruit.
11. How did Jesus explain the world and the false church’s hatred and persecution of God’s people?
a. Jesus explained that he has chosen us out of the world, and the world hates us for Christ’s sake because the world does not know God.
Memory verse: “I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing.”—John 15:5
John 15
Lesson 19: I Am the True Vine
1. Whom did Jesus promise to send to his church after his ascension?
a. Jesus promised to send the Comforter, the Spirit of truth.
2. Who is the Comforter?
a. The Comforter is the Holy Spirit, the third person of the Trinity, as the Spirit of Christ.
3. Why is the Spirit of Christ called the Comforter?
a. The Spirit of Christ is called the Comforter because he brings Christ to us and assures us that we belong to Christ, our faithful savior.
4. How is it that the Holy Spirit is the Spirit of Christ?
a. The Holy Spirit was given to Christ at his ascension to be his Spirit.
5. What did Jesus say about the relationship between his people and the Comforter?
a. Jesus said that his people know the Comforter because he dwells in them.
6. Why is the Holy Spirit called the Spirit of truth?
a. The Spirit teaches the church all things and guides the church into all the truth.
7. What does the Holy Spirit do to the world?
a. The Spirit convicts the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment.
8. What is this conviction?
a. This conviction is the Spirit’s hardening the reprobate in their own sin and unbelief outside of Christ and in the certainty of their own destruction.
9. What did Jesus promised to give to his people through the Spirit?
a. Jesus promised, “Peace I leave with you, my peace I give to you.”
10. What is this peace?
a. It is the Holy Spirit’s assurance of our righteousness before God so that we rest in God and the certainty of our salvation.

Memory verse: “When…the Spirit of truth, is come, he will guide you into all truth: for he shall not speak of himself; but whatsoever he shall hear, that shall he speak: and he will shew you things to come.”—John 16:13
Lesson 20: The Spirit of Truth
1. What do we call the prayer of Christ in John 17?
a. We call that prayer Christ’s high priestly prayer.
2. Why do we call this prayer the high priestly prayer?
a. When he prayed, Christ was our high priest who intercedes for us with God.
3. What power did God give to Jesus Christ?
a. God gave to Christ power over all flesh to give eternal life to as many as God had given to him.
4. What is eternal life?
a. Eternal life is to know the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he has sent.
5. What is the glory that Christ asked of God?
a. Christ asked for the glory that he had with God before the world was.
6. What is that glory?
a. It is the eternal glory of Christ in God’s counsel as the head of all things.
7. What did Christ confess concerning God’s people in the world?
a. While Christ was in the world, he kept all God’ people and lost none of them.
8. What did Christ say concerning God’s people in the world?
a. Christ said, “They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.”
9. What did Christ pray for God’s people in the world?
a. Christ asked that God would sanctify them through his truth.
10. What is the meaning of that sanctification?
a. Sanctification means that through the truth God’s people are united in fellowship with God and Christ and with one another.
11. How did Christ describe sanctification?
a. Christ said, “That they all may be one: as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be one in us.”

Memory verse: “Father, I will that they also, whom thou hast given me, be with me where I am; that they may behold my glory, which thou hast given me: for thou lovedst me before the foundation of the world.”—John 17:24
John 17
Lesson 21: The High Priestly Prayer
1. Where did Jesus go after he had finished celebrating the last passover and instructing his disciples in the upper room?
a. Jesus took his disciples across the brook Kidron into the garden of Gethsemane, where he had often gone with his disciples.
2. Who also knew of this place?
a. Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, who had betrayed Jesus and would capture him there.
3. How did Judas come against Jesus to capture him?
a. Judas came with weapons and with a band of soldiers that he had received from the chief priests and Pharisees.
4. What did Jesus respond when the soldiers said that they sought Jesus of Nazareth?
a. Jesus responded, “I am.”
5. What happened when Jesus said that?
a. Judas and the whole band of soldiers fell down before Jesus.
6. What did Jesus intend to show by his answer “I am”?
a. Jesus intended to show that his name is I AM because he is God and that the soldiers could have no power against him except he went willingly.
7. What did Peter do in the garden when the soldiers tried to take Jesus?
a. With his sword Peter cut off the ear of Malchus, the servant of the high priest.
8. What did Jesus tell Peter?
a. Jesus said, “Put up your sword: the cup that the Father has given me, shall I not drink it?
9. What was the cup that his Father had given Jesus to drink?
a. The cup was the will of God for the salvation of all his elect people and the coming of Christ’s eternal kingdom.
10. What did Jesus teach when he told Peter to put away his sword?
a. Jesus taught that his kingdom is not advanced by earthly power but by his death on the cross.

Memory verse: “He went a little further, and fell on his face, and prayed, saying, O my Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me: nevertheless not as I will, but as thou wilt. ”—Matthew 26:39
Lesson 22: Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane
1. Where did the band of soldiers take Jesus?
a. The soldiers led Jesus away to the palace of Caiaphas the high priest.
2. What did Jesus respond to the high priest when he asked regarding Jesus’ disciples and doctrine?
a. Jesus said, “I have ever taught in the synagogues and in the temple, and I have said nothing in secret. Ask those who heard me.”
3. What did an officer do after he heard that?
a. The officer struck Jesus and said, “Do you answer the high priest so?”
4. Who followed Jesus to the palace?
a. Peter and John followed.
5. What did John do?
a. John knew the high priest, so John went into the palace and later came out to bring Peter in.
6. What did the young girl who kept the door of the palace ask Peter as he went in?
a. She asked, “Are you not also one of his disciples?”
7. What did Peter do?
a. Peter denied, saying, “I am not.”
8. What did the men around the fire say to Peter as he warmed himself in the palace?
a. The men asked Peter whether he was also one of Jesus’ disciples.
9. What did Peter do?
a. Peter denied again, saying, “I am not.”
10. What did the kinsman of the high priest’s servant, whose ear Peter had cut off, ask?
a. The man asked, “Did I not see you in the garden with him?”
11. What did Peter do?
a. Peter denied Jesus with cursing and swearing, and immediately the cock crew.

Memory verse: “The Lord said, Simon, Simon, behold, Satan hath desired to have you, that he may sift you as wheat: But I have prayed for thee, that thy faith fail not: and when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren.”—Luke 22: 31–32
Lesson 23: Peter Denies Jesus
1. What wicked thing did the Jews do when they delivered Jesus to Pilate?
a. The Jews refused to enter the judgment hall so as not to defile themselves so they could eat the passover.
2. What did Pilate ask Jesus after the Jews accused him of making himself a king?
a. Pilate asked, “Art thou the king of the Jews”?
3. What did Jesus respond to Pilate?
a. Jesus responded, “My kingdom is not of this world.”
4. What did Jesus mean when he said that?
a. Jesus meant that he is the king of the heavenly kingdom of God.
5. What did the Jews cry out when Pilate said he would release Jesus?
a. The Jews cried, “Not this man, but Barabbas!”
6. What did Pilate do with Jesus?
a. Pilate delivered Jesus to the soldiers to beat and mock him as the king of the Jews.
7. What did the Jews cry out when Pilate presented Jesus to them?
a. The Jews cried, “Crucify him, crucify him!”
8. What did the Jews tell Pilate when he told them to crucify Jesus themselves?
a. The Jews said that they had a law by which Jesus ought to die because he made himself the Son of God.
9. What did the Jews tell Pilate when he sought still to release Jesus?
a. The Jews said to Pilate, “If you let this man go, you are not Caesar’s friend.”
10. What did the Jews say when Pilate asked whether he should crucify their king?
a. The Jews said, “We have no king but Ceasar.”

Memory verse: “Jesus answered, My kingdom is not of this world: if my kingdom were of this world, then would my servants fight, that I should not be delivered to the Jews: but now is my kingdom not from hence.”—John 18:36
Lesson 24: Jesus Before Pilate
1. With whom did the soldiers crucify Jesus?
a. Jesus was crucified between two thieves, one on either side and Jesus in the midst.
2. What superscription did Pilate write above Jesus’ head on the cross?
a. Pilate wrote, “Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.”
3. What did the Jews want Pilate to write?
a. The Jews wanted Pilate to write, “He said that he is the King of the Jews.”
4. What did the soldiers do with Jesus’ clothes?
a. The soldiers took Jesus’ clothes and divided them among themselves and cast lots for his coat that was woven without seam.
5. What did Jesus say to Mary his mother and to John his disciple?
a. Jesus said, “Woman, behold they son,” and to John, “Behold thy mother.”
6. What did that mean?
a. It meant that the new family of the human race is Christ’s people who believe in him.
7. What did Jesus cry after the soldier gave him vinegar to quench his thirst?
a. Jesus cried, “It is finished!”
8. What did his cry mean?
a. “It is finished” means that Jesus had accomplished fully all the will and counsel of God for our salvation.
9. What did the Jews ask Pilate at the end of the day?
a. The Jews asked if the prisoners’ legs could be broken so that the men would die quickly and their bodies could be taken down before the Sabbath.
10. What did the soldiers do when they came to Jesus?
a. The soldiers found that Jesus was dead, so they pierced his side, and out of it came blood and water.
11. Of what is that blood and water a sign?
a. The blood and water are signs of Christ’s cross as perfect satisfaction for sin and the power of his blood to cleanse from all sin and guilt.
Memory verse: “Even as the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many.”—Matthew 20:28
Lesson 25: The Cross
1. What did Mary Magdelene do when she saw the stone rolled away from Jesus’ sepulcher?
a. Mary ran to the disciples to tell them that the Jews had taken away the body of Jesus.
2. What did Peter and John find when they came to investigate?
a. Peter saw the clothes lying in the tomb as they had been wrapped around Jesus’ body and the napkin that had been around his head folded in a corner.
3. What did Mary do?
a. Mary stood weeping outside the grave of Jesus.
4. Did Mary recognize Jesus when he appeared to her?
a. No, Mary thought he was the gardener, and she asked him where he had taken Jesus’ body.
5. When did Mary recognize Jesus?
a. Jesus said, “Mary, ” and then she recognized him.
6. To whom did Jesus appear later that evening?
a. Jesus appeared to all the disciples except Thomas as they gathered in the upper room.
7. What did Thomas say when the disciples told him that they had seen Jesus?
a. Thomas said, “Except I see in his hands the print of the nails, put my finger into the print of the nails, and thrust my hand into his side, I will not believe.”
8. When Jesus appeared to all the disciples eight day later, what did he say to Thomas?
a. Jesus said to Thomas, “Reach hither your finger, behold my hands, thrust your hand into my side, and be not faithless but believing.”
9. What did Thomas say to Jesus?
a. Thomas said, “My Lord and my God.”
10. What did Jesus say to Thomas?
a. Jesus said, “Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.”
11. Who are those blessed ones?
a. They are all God’s elect in the world to whom the gospel of Jesus comes and works faith in their hearts that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.

Memory verse: “Many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of his disciples, which are not written in this book: but these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name.”—John 20:30–31
John 20
Lesson 26: Thomas
1. Where did Jesus appear to Peter and John and several other disciples?
a. Jesus appeared to them on the shore of the Sea of Galilee.
2. What did Jesus tell the disciples after they had fished all night and caught nothing?
a. Jesus told them to cast their nets on the other side of the ship.
3. What happened when the disciples cast their nets on the other side?
a. They caught a great multitude of fish, one hundred fifty-three in number.
4. What did Peter do when he learned that the person on the shore was Jesus?
a. Peter cast himself into the sea and swam to shore.
5. What did Jesus do on the shore with the disciples?
a. Together they ate fish and bread that Christ had prepared.
6. What did Jesus ask Peter after supper?
a. Jesus asked Peter, “Lovest thou me, more than these?”
7. What did Peter reply?
a. Peter said, “Lord, thou knowest that I love thee.”
8. What did Peter respond after Jesus asked Peter that question a third time?
a. Peter was grieved and said, “Lord, thou knowest all things; thou knowest that I love thee.”
9. What did Jesus tell Peter?
a. Jesus told Peter, “Feed my sheep and lambs.”
10. Why did Jesus appear to Peter and ask him that question?
a. Jesus came to comfort Peter in the knowledge of the forgiveness of his sins in order that he might preach that truth to God’s people.

Memory verse: “There are also many other things which Jesus did, the which, if they should be written every one, I suppose that even the world itself could not contain the books that should be written. Amen.”—John 21:25
John 21
Lesson 27: Peter Converted
New Testament History
The Word Became Flesh
Grades 5–8
1. What had God promised David?
a. God had promised that he would set up David’s seed in a kingdom that would have no end.
2. Who is that promised seed?
a. The promised seed is our Lord Jesus Christ.
3. What does scripture make clear about the royal line of David?
a. Matthew 1 says that the royal line of David ran through Joseph.
4. What had God said of that royal line?
a. After Jehoiachin God had said that not a man from David’s royal line would sit on the throne of David.
5. Was this the end of God’s promise to David?
a. Not at all, for God would raise up David’s greater son, Jesus Christ, to sit on David’s throne forever.
6. How was Jesus a son of David?
a. Jesus was of a son of David through his mother Mary, who was from another branch of David’s line.
7. What did the angel of the Lord announce to Joseph about the birth of Jesus?
a. The angel of the Lord said that Mary would have a son who must be named Jesus because he will save his people from their sins.
8. What does scripture make clear about Mary?
a. Scripture says that Mary was a virgin who was with child by the Holy Ghost.
9. Who is Jesus Christ?
a. Jesus Christ is Immanuel, that is, God with us.
10. What is Christ’s kingdom?
a. Christ’s kingdom is the rule of Christ by his grace in the hearts and lives of his people.
11. What is the nature of Christ’s kingdom?
a. Christ’s kingdom is spiritual and not of this world.
Memory verse: “I have made a covenant with my chosen, I have sworn unto David my servant, thy seed will I establish for ever, and build up thy throne to all generations.”—Psalm 89:3–4
2 Samuel 7; Psalm 89; Jeremiah 22:30; Matthew 1
Lesson 1: The Coming of the Seed of David
1. Who was the forerunner of Jesus Christ?
a. John the Baptist was the forerunner.
2. Why did John minister and preach in the wilderness?
a. John’s preaching in the wilderness was a testimony against Israel that she was nothing but a spiritual wasteland with no life.
3. What did John tell the priests and Levites who were sent to ask him who he was?
a. John said, “I am the voice of one crying in the wilderness. Make straight the way of the Lord.”
4. What did that mean?
a. It meant that God was coming to accomplish the salvation of his people, and as sinners the people had to move out of the way.
5. What did John say about Jesus to the people in the wilderness?
a. John said, “Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.”
6. Why did John call Jesus the Lamb of God?
a. John meant that Jesus came out of God’s purpose to save his elect people and that Jesus is the perfect sacrifice for sin to which all the Old Testament sacrifices pointed.
7. Why was it necessary that John baptize Jesus?
a. Jesus had to be baptized to show that he could enter his kingdom no other way than by his own death for the sins of his people.
8. What did John see when he baptized Jesus?
a. John saw heaven opened and the Spirit descend and rest on Jesus in the form of a dove.
9. What did that sign mean regarding Jesus’ work?
a. The sign meant that Jesus Christ was anointed with the Spirit for his work and that Jesus would baptize with the Holy Spirit and with fire.
10. What did John testify when he saw that sign?
a. John bare record to all that Jesus is the Son of God.
Memory verse: “The next day John seeth Jesus coming unto him, and saith, Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world.”—John 1:29
Matthew 3:1–17; Mark 1:1–11; Luke 3:1–22; John 1:19–51
Lesson 2: Jesus Is Baptized
1. What does scripture say about Jesus’ ministry in Galilee?
a. Jesus went throughout every city and village, preaching and showing the glad tidings of the kingdom of God.
2. How did Jesus show the glad tidings of the kingdom of God?
a. Jesus healed all manner of sickness and disease and cast out many devils to show that the kingdom had come in power.
3. What sermon did Jesus preach on the kingdom?
a. Jesus preached the sermon on the mount.
4. How did Jesus begin his sermon?
a. Jesus taught the power of the kingdom shown in the beatitudes—the spiritual characteristics given to the citizens of the kingdom.
5. What is the central characteristic of the citizens of the kingdom?
a. The citizens hunger and thirst after the righteous of the kingdom.
6. How do the citizens of the kingdom show their hunger for righteous?
a. The citizens are poor in spirit, accounting themselves nothing, and they mourn over their sins.
7. How do the citizens of the kingdom show that they have been filled with righteousness?
a. The citizens are merciful toward their neighbors, God gives them a pure heart, and they are peacemakers.
8. What is the reaction of the world and the false church to the coming of the kingdom in its citizens?
a. The world and the false church hate the kingdom and its king, and they persecute and slander God’s people as the false church of old killed the prophets.
9. What did Jesus tell the persecuted and reviled citizens of his kingdom?
a. Jesus said that the citizens are blessed and that their reward in heaven is great.
10. Upon whom did Jesus pronounce the dreadful woe of the kingdom?
a. Jesus spoke against those who for the sake of this world’s riches and the honor of men cleave to the false church and its doctrine of salvation by man’s works.

Memory verse: “Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God. Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.”— Matthew 5:3–10
Matthew 5–7; Luke 6:17–49; Luke 8:1
Lesson 3: The Sermon on the Mount (1): The Citizens of the Kingdom
1. What did Jesus say he would do with the law and the prophets?
a. Jesus said, “I came not to destroy the law and the prophets but to fulfill them.”
2. What was the testimony of the law and the prophets?
a. Their testimony was that God’s people cannot be righteous with God through the law and that God promised a redeemer to save them from the bondage of the law.
3. What did Jesus tell the citizens of his kingdom about righteousness in his kingdom?
a. Jesus said their righteousness must exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees.
4. What was the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees?
a. Their righteousness was of man’s works and superficial, outward conformity to the law.
5. Give an example of what the scribes and Pharisees said about the law.
a. They said, “Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, thou shalt not commit adultery.”
6. What did the scribes and Pharisees mean when they said that?
a. They made adultery a matter of only the outward act.
