7. How did Jesus oppose their false teaching about the law?
a. Jesus said, “If a man looks at a woman to lust after her in his heart, he has committed adultery.”
8. What did Jesus mean about the law when he taught that?
a. Jesus taught that the law condemned man in his nature as one who hates God and his neighbor and that righteousness through the law is impossible.
9. What righteousness exceeds the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees?
a. That righteousness is the righteousness of Jesus Christ our Lord that consists of his obedience, perfect holiness, and atoning death.
10. What is true of those who have this righteousness?
a. Those who have this righteousness are perfect as God is perfect and are citizens of the kingdom of God.
11. What is the privilege of those who have Christ’s righteous?
a. As good trees before God, they bring forth good fruits of love toward the neighbor.
Memory verse: “Except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven.”—Matthew 5:20
Matthew 5:17–48
Lesson 4: Sermon on the Mount (2): A Righteousness That Exceeds the Righteousness of the Pharisees
1. How did Jesus instruct the citizens of his kingdom regarding seeking the kingdom?
a. Jesus said, “Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness.”
2. What is the righteousness of God?
a. The righteousness of God is the righteousness of Christ through his cross and resurrection received by faith.
3. What does it mean to seek the kingdom?
a. To seek the kingdom is to cast off all confidence in ourselves and to rely on God alone for our righteousness in Christ.
4. What does seeking the kingdom first mean for our earthly lives?
a. It means that being righteous in Christ, we are at peace with God; and we trust that as God feeds the birds, he more certainly will care for us.
5. What warning did Jesus give about seeking earthly things?
a. Jesus warned, “No man can serve two masters: ye cannot serve God and mammon.”
6. What did Jesus tell the people about entrance into the kingdom of heaven?
a. Jesus said that they must enter at the straight gate.
7. What is the straight gate?
a. The straight gate is entrance into the kingdom that consists of Christ’s work and excludes all man’s works.
8. What did Jesus say about those who enter the kingdom?
a. Jesus said that not every one who says “Lord, Lord” to him will enter the kingdom, but only those who do the will of his heavenly Father will enter.
9. What is the will of Christ’s heavenly Father?
a. The will of God is that we believe on the name of his Son, Jesus Christ.
10. Where does this faith in Christ come from?
a. Faith is the gift of God to his people by the operation of the Holy Spirit in their hearts.
11. What was the reaction of the people to Jesus’ teaching on the kingdom?
a. The people were astonished at his doctrine because he taught as one having authority and not as the scribes.
Memory verse: “No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.”—Matthew 6:24
Matthew 6:19–7:29
Lesson 5: Sermon on the Mount (3) Seek First the Kingdom and Entering at the Straight Gate
1. Who were present while Jesus was teaching in a house in Capernaum?
a. The house was filled with the multitudes of people, who came to hear Jesus, and many Pharisees and scribes were present too.
2. What did four friends try to do for their paralytic friend whom they were carrying on a bed?
a. The friends tried to bring their friend to Jesus, but they could not because of the multitudes.
3. What did the friends do when they could not reach Jesus?
a. They climbed on the roof, broke a hole in the roof, and let their paralytic friend down into the house in front of Jesus.
4. What did Jesus see in the actions of the four men and the paralytic?
a. Jesus saw their faith.
5. What is faith?
a. Faith is union with Jesus Christ and confidence that we are righteous in him alone.
6. What did Jesus say to the paralytic?
a. Jesus said, “Son, thy sins be forgiven thee.”
7. What did the scribes and Pharisees say within themselves?
a. They asked themselves, “Why does this man speak blasphemies: who can forgive sins but God only?”
8. What did Jesus ask the scribes and Pharisees when he knew what they had said in their hearts?
a. Jesus asked them, “What is easier: to say your sins be forgiven or arise up and walk?”
9. How did Jesus show that the Son of man has power on earth to forgive sins?
a. Jesus said to the paralytic, “Arise, take up your bed, and walk,” and immediately the man walked before the multitudes.
10. What did that miracle show?
a. The miracle showed that the root of all man’s misery is his guilt and that Jesus Christ takes away our guilt through the forgiveness of sins.
Memory verse: “Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered. Blessed is the man unto whom the LORD imputeth not iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no guile.”— Psalm 32:1-2
Matthew 9:1–8; Mark 2:1–13; Luke 5:17–26
Lesson 6: The Son of Man Has Power to Forgive Sins
1. One time when Jesus had finished praying, what did a disciple ask of Jesus?
a. The disciple said, “Lord, teach us to pray as John taught his disciples.”
2. What did Jesus teach the disciples?
a. Jesus taught them the Lord’s prayer.
3. What is prayer?
a. Prayer is the chief part of thankfulness to God for the forgiveness of sins.
4. What parable did Jesus teach his disciples to show how we pray to God?
a. Jesus taught the parable of the man who went at midnight to a friend to ask for bread to feed a guest.
5. What was Jesus’ point with that parable?
a. Jesus’ point was that the Christian in prayer is importunate.
6. What is meant by importunity?
a. Importunity is shamelessness to ask for something that you do not deserve.
7. How does importunity apply to the Christian and prayer?
a. The Christian comes to God as an undeserving sinner and asks for all things necessary for his body and soul in confidence that God will give him what he asks.
8. To what does Jesus liken God’s willingness to give us his grace and Holy Spirit?
a. Jesus says that we are evil, but we know how to give good gifts to our children; so much more God will give his Holy Spirit to his children.
9. How can we say that God gives his Holy Spirit to his children when they already have the Holy Spirit?
a. To ask for the Holy Spirit from God is not to ask for something we do not already have, but to acknowledge our complete dependence on God and to give thanks for his gifts.
10. What did Jesus mean when he taught his disciples that God will not forgive them if they do not forgive their neighbor?
a. Jesus meant that our forgiveness of the neighbor is the sure fruit of the God-given assurance that we are forgiven.

Memory verse: “When thou prayest, enter into thy closet, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly. But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking. Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him. After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen.—Matthew 6:6–13
Matthew 6:5–15; Luke 11:1–13
Lesson 7: Lord, Teach Us to Pray
1. Whom did Jesus appoint to preach throughout the land of Israel?
a. Jesus appointed his twelve disciples plus seventy others.
2. Where did Jesus tell them to go?
a. Jesus sent them to preach to the lost sheep of the household of Israel.
3. What did Jesus tell them to preach?
a. Jesus said, “Preach that the kingdom of heaven is at hand.”
4. What did that mean?
a. It meant that the disciples were to declare Christ, in whom all the promises of God are yes and amen.
5. What would be the result of the disciples’ preaching?
a. Some would believe the preaching, but others would reject the preaching, and it would be more tolerable for Sodom and Gomorrah in the day of judgment than for those who did not believe.
6. What warning did Jesus give to his disciples about the effect of their preaching?
a. Jesus said, “Think not that I am come to send peace on the earth, not peace but a sword.”
7. What is this sword?
a. The sword that Jesus brings is the enmity that is created by the truth and that divides even between family members.
8. Was the idea of a sword something new?
a. No, the sword was the fulfillment of God’s promise that he would put enmity between the seed of the serpent and the seed of the woman.
9. What did Jesus say when the disciples came back rejoicing that even devils were subject to them in Christ’s name?
a. Jesus said, “Rejoice not that devils are subject to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.”
10. What did Jesus pray to God at that time?
a. Jesus thanked God for his sovereign good pleasure to hide the truth from the wise and prudent and to reveal the truth to babes.

Memory verse: “In that hour Jesus rejoiced in spirit, and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes: even so, Father; for so it seemed good in thy sight.”—Luke 10:21
Matthew 10; Mark 3:13–19; Luke 9:1–6
Lesson 8: Jesus Christ Commissions the Twelve and the Seventy
1. How did the scribes and Pharisees respond to Jesus’ power to cast out devils?
a. They said that Jesus cast out devils by the power of Beelzebub, the prince of the devils.
2. How did Jesus show the folly of that accusation?
a. Jesus said, “If Satan cast out Satan, he destroys his own kingdom.”
3. How did Jesus teach the power by which he cast out devils?
a. Jesus said that he cast out devils by the power of the Spirit of God.
4. What did Jesus say concerning that accusation of the scribes and Pharisees?
a. Jesus said that all manner of blasphemy and sin is forgiven men, but the scribes and Pharisees had committed the sin against the Holy Spirit and that sin is not forgiven.
5. Why is the sin against the Holy Spirit not forgiven?
a. The one who commits that sin shows by his words that God has hardened his heart against the truth, and God does not will that person’s salvation.
6. To what did Jesus liken his generation with all their excuses for their unbelief?
a. Jesus said that his generation was like children playing in a market and saying to their friends, “We have piped and you did not dance; we have mourned, and you did not lament.”
7. What did Jesus mean by that parable?
a. Jesus meant that the scribes and Pharisees always had excuses for their unbelief: When John came, they said, “He has a devil.” When Christ came, they said, “He is a glutton and winebibber.”
8. Why did Jesus pronounce woe upon the scribes and Pharisees?
a. Jesus pronounced woe because by their doctrine they bound men with heavy burdens, but they would not lift one finger to help them.
9. Why did Jesus pronounce woe on the cities of Capernaum, Chorazin, and Bethsaida?
a. Jesus pronounced woe on those cities because they had seen all his mighty works and did not repent.
10. What remarkable thing did Jesus say about those cities and their unbelief?
a. Jesus said that if the mighty works that he had done in those cities would have been done in Tyre and Sidon or in Sodom and Gomorrah, those cities would have repented in dust and ashes.
11. How did Jesus call his elect unto himself?
a. Jesus said, “Come unto me all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.”

Memory verse: “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.”—Matthew 11:28–30
Matthew 9:34; Matthew 11; Matthew 12:22–50; Mark 3:22–35; Luke 11:14–54
Lesson 9: Beelzebub and Woes on the Unbelieving
1. What did the disciples do as they were following Jesus through a field on the Sabbath?
a. The disciples picked some corn, shelled it in their hands, and ate the corn because they were hungry.
2. What did the Pharisees charge against the disciples?
a. The Pharisees charged the disciples with working on the Sabbath and breaking the law of Moses.
3. What is the Sabbath?
a. The Sabbath is the day of rest, which in the Old Testament was the seventh day of the week.
4. Why do we worship God on Sunday, the first day of the week?
a. Jesus Christ changed the Sabbath to Sunday by his resurrection from the dead and made Sunday the Lord’s day.
5. What was the Pharisees’ wicked doctrine of the Sabbath?
a. The Pharisees taught that the Sabbath is kept holy by outward observances and especially by observing their rules.
6. How did Jesus first point out the Pharisees’ hypocrisy?
a. Jesus said that David in his need had eaten of the showbread and broken the law because God did not make the laws to kill his people but to lead them to Christ.
7. How did Jesus point out the folly of the Pharisee’s doctrine?
a. Jesus said that the priests worked in the temple on the Sabbath, and their work was sanctified by the temple and the service of God.
8. What did Jesus mean when he called himself the Lord of the Sabbath?
a. Jesus meant that he fulfills all that the Sabbath pointed to and that the Sabbath is his day.
9. What did Jesus mean when he said that the greater than the temple has come?
a. Jesus meant that the Sabbath cannot be kept apart from faith in Christ and his truth.
10. How did Jesus prove his doctrine of the Sabbath?
a. Jesus went into the synagogue on the Sabbath and healed a man with a withered hand.
11. Did the Pharisees repent of their wicked doctrine of the Sabbath?
a. No, the Pharisees were filled with madness and held a council how they could destroy Jesus.
Memory Work: “I desired mercy, and not sacrifice; and the knowledge of God more than burnt offerings.”—Hosea 6:6
Matthew 12:1–21; Mark 2:23–28; Luke 6:1–11
Lesson 10: The Disciples Pick Corn and Confrontation Concerning the Sabbath
1. What form of teaching did Jesus begin to use later in his ministry?
a. Jesus began to teach in parables.
2. What are parables?
a. Parables are earthly stories with spiritual meanings.
3. Why could Jesus teach spiritual truths through earthly stories?
a. Jesus could do that because God had made the earthly after the pattern of the heavenly.
4. How did Jesus explain the reason that he taught in parables?
a. Jesus said that to God’s people it is given to understand the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to others it is not given.
5. What did Jesus mean by that?
a. Jesus meant that God’s purpose of election and reprobation controls who understands and believes and who does not.
6. How did Jesus illustrate what he had said about his reason for teaching in parables?
a. Jesus told the parable of a sower who sowed seeds in his field.
7. What happened to the seeds?
a. Some seeds fell on good soil, and other seeds fell on thorny soil, stoney soil, and the hard path.
8. What is the good soil?
a. The good soil is God’s elect who hear his word, and the word bears fruit in them.
9. What is the thorny soil?
a. The thorny soil is the reprobate who appear to believe, but they leave the truth because they love the world.
10. What is the stoney soil?
a. The stoney soil is also the reprobate who appear to believe, but they do not endure when persecution comes.
11. What is the hard path?
a. The hard path is the reprobate who hear the word, and the devil steals away that word.
Memory verse: “In them is fulfilled the prophecy of Esaias, which saith, By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and shall not perceive.”—Matthew 13:14
Matthew 13:1–23; Mark 4:1–20; Luke 8:4–15
Lesson 11: The Mysteries of the Kingdom and the Parable of the Sower
1. What instruction did Jesus give about how we must deal with a brother who privately sins against us?
a. We must go to the brother and tell him his sin privately.
2. What if the brother does not listen?
a. Then we are to go to the brother again with two or three witnesses.
3. What do we do if the brother still does not hear?
a. Then we are to bring the matter to the consistory.
4. What if the man does not heed the admonitions of the consistory?
a. He is to be excommunicated and be as a heathen man to the church.
5. What power did Jesus give to his church in excommunication?
a. Jesus said, “Whatever the church binds on earth is bound in heaven, and whatever the church loosens on earth is loosed in heaven.”
6. What if the brother hears us when we bring his sin to him?
a. We are to assure him of our forgiveness of him even as God has forgiven us.
7. How did Jesus illustrate the truth of the forgiveness of the brother?
a. Jesus taught the parable of the two debtors.
8. In the parable of the two debtors, what did the lord do to the servant who owed him ten thousand talents?
a. The lord had compassion on the debtor and forgave his debt.
9. What did that man who had been forgiven that great debt do to his fellow servant who owed him only one hundred pence?
a. The man took his fellow servant by the throat and said, “Pay me what you owe!” And when the servant could not pay, the man cast the servant into debtors’ prison.
10. What did the lord do to that wicked man who would not forgive his fellow servant’s debts?
a. The lord was angry with that man and delivered him to the tormentors until he had paid his debt to the lord.
Memory verse: “Likewise shall my heavenly Father do also unto you, if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses.”—Matthew 18:35
Matthew 18
Lesson 12: The Way of Matthew 18 and Forgiveness of the Brother
1. What question did the Pharisees ask Jesus to tempt him?
a. The Pharisees asked, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any cause?”
2. How did Jesus respond to that question?
a. Jesus pointed the Pharisees to God’s creation of marriage when he made Adam and Eve one flesh.
3. What did Jesus instruct concerning that history of marriage?
a. Jesus said, “What God has joined together, let not man put asunder.”
4. How did the Pharisees respond to Jesus’ teaching about marriage?
a. The Pharisees asked why Moses had commanded that the men give their wives a writing of divorce and send them away.
5. How did Jesus explain what Moses had written?
a. Jesus said that Moses allowed wives to be put away because of the hardness of the hearts of the Israelites.
6. What did Jesus do as king of the kingdom concerning marriage?
a. Jesus overturned what Moses had said and taught that from the beginning putting away wives was not allowed.
7. What did Jesus teach as the law of the kingdom concerning marriage?
a. Jesus taught that marriage is a one-flesh union for life as God created in the beginning.
8. What did Jesus call divorce?
a. Jesus said that if a man divorces his wife except for adultery, then he commits adultery.
9. If a man divorces his wife for adultery, may he then remarry?
a. No, the marriage bond is not broken in divorce, so that whoever marries a divorced person commits adultery.
10. Of what is marriage a picture?
a. Marriage is a picture of the one-flesh relationship between Christ and his elect church.

Memory verse: “I say unto you, Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication, and shall marry another, committeth adultery: and whoso marrieth her which is put away doth commit adultery.”—Matthew 19:9
Lesson 13: The Pharisees Tempt Jesus Concerning Marriage
1. Who came running to Jesus to ask about how he could inherit eternal life?
a. A rich, young ruler came to Jesus and asked, “Good master, what good thing must I do to inherit eternal life?”
2. What did Jesus say to the young ruler?
a. Jesus said, “Why call me good? There is none good but God.”
3. Why did Jesus point the rich, young ruler to God’s goodness?
a. Jesus did that because the ruler did not understand the perfection of God’s goodness.
4. What is the meaning of God’s goodness?
a. God’s goodness means that God always justifies the righteous and always condemns the wicked.
5. What did Jesus tell the young ruler to do?
a. Jesus told him to keep the ten commandments.
6. Why did Jesus point the young ruler to the commandments to inherit eternal life?
a. Jesus pointed the young ruler to the commandments because the law makes salvation by man’s works impossible.
7. How did the rich, young ruler respond?
a. He said, “I have kept all the commandments from my youth up.”
8. What did Jesus tell the young ruler to do?
a. Jesus said, “Go, sell all that you have, give to the poor, and follow me.”
9. Why did Jesus say that to the young ruler?
a. Jesus said that because the young ruler thought he had no sin, and Jesus wanted to expose the root of sin in the ruler’s covetous nature and love of riches.
10. What does scripture say about Jesus’ attitude toward that rich, young ruler?
a. Scripture says that Jesus loved him, which means that he was elect and that Jesus would deliver the ruler from his covetousness and false doctrine.
11. What did Christ tell his disciples about rich men?
a. Jesus said, “a rich man shall hardly enter into the kingdom of heaven.”
Memory verse: “I say unto you, It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God.”—Matthew 19:24
Lesson 14: The Rich, Young Ruler and Rich Men
1. How did Jesus respond to Peter’s question about what the disciples could have because they had left all and followed Christ?
a. Jesus said, “Everyone who forsakes all in my name will receive a hundredfold more in this life with persecutions and in the world to come eternal life.”
2. What parable did Jesus teach to show that this reward is of pure grace?
a. Jesus spoke the parable of the laborers in the vineyard.
3. What did the lord of the vineyard do in the morning?
a. The lord of the vineyard hired laborers, and each would be paid a penny a day.
4. What did the lord do from the third hour until the eleventh hour of the day?
a. The lord continued to hire laborers and promised to give them for their labors whatever was right.
5. What did the lord give to those whom he had hired even at the eleventh hour?
a. The lord of the vineyard gave a penny to each laborer.
6. What did those who had been hired first receive?
a. They likewise each received a penny.
7. What was the complaint of the laborers who were hired first?
a. Because they had worked longer and in the heat of the day, they complained that the lord was unfair when he made all the workers equal by giving a penny to each laborer.
8. How did the lord of the vineyard respond to those complainers?
a. The lord asked them, “Is thine eye evil, but I am good?”
9. What did the lord mean when he asked that question?
a. The lord of the vineyard meant that his goodness in giving everyone the same wage brought out that those who complained evilly thought that they deserved more than a penny.
10. Of what was their evil complaint a picture?
a. Their evil complaint was a picture of the evil doctrine that God gives more blessings or a higher place in heaven to those who labor more in his kingdom.
11. How did Jesus explain the pure graciousness of his reward of eternal life?
a. Jesus said, “Many are called, but few are chosen.”
Memory verse: “The last shall be first, and the first last: for many be called, but few chosen.”— Matthew 20:16
Lesson 15: The Parable of the Laborers in the Vineyard
1. What parable did Jesus speak concerning the preaching of the gospel?
a. Jesus spoke the parable of the wedding feast.
2. What did the king in the parable do?
a. The king made a marriage supper for his son, and the king sent his servants to command the guests to come to the feast.
3. What did those who were commanded to come to the feast do?
a. Many made light of that command, ignored it, and went their way, the rest took the king’s servants and beat them and killed them.
4. What did the king do when he heard that?
a. The king was angry, and he sent his armies to destroy those murderers and to burn their city.
5. Of what was that portion of the parable a type, or picture?
a. It was a picture of God’s just destruction upon many who hear the call of the gospel that goes out, reject it, and persecute those who bring it.
6. Then what did the king command his servant to do?
a. The king told his servant to go into the highways and byways and to gather whomsoever he found in order to furnish the wedding with guests.
7. What did the king of the kingdom find when he came into the wedding feast?
a. The king found a man who did not have on a wedding garment.
8. What did the king of the kingdom do with that man?
a. The king had his servants cast the man into outer darkness.
9. What did the wedding garment signify?
a. The wedding garment signified the righteousness of Jesus Christ, whereby we have a right to sit at the marriage supper of the Lamb in everlasting life.
10. How did Jesus explain the reception and rejection of the gospel of Christ’s righteousness?
a. Jesus said, “Many are called, but few are chosen.”
Memory verse: “Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.”—Romans 5:1
Matthew 22:1–14; Luke 14:15–24
Lesson 16: The Parable of the Wedding Feast
1. How did the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Herodians try to destroy Jesus?
a. They sent out spies who pretended to be just men in order to catch Jesus in his words so they could deliver Jesus to the governor.
2. What did the Pharisees and the Herodians ask Jesus?
a. They asked, “Is it lawful to pay tribute to Caesar?”
3. How did Jesus respond?
a. He said, “Render unto Caesar the things that are Caesar’s and unto God the things that are God’s.”
4. How did the Sadducees, who did not believe in the resurrection, start their attack on Jesus?
a. The Sadducees said that Moses wrote that if a man died having no children, then his brother must take that man’s wife and raise up seed for him.
5. What story did the Sadducees tell?

a. They told of a married man who died having no children, and that man’s wife married his brother, who then died having no children; and that was repeated with five other brothers who all died having no children.
6. After telling that story, what did the Sadducees ask Jesus?
a. They asked, “Whose wife of the seven brothers will she be in the resurrection?”
7. How did Jesus respond?
a. Jesus said, “You do err, for in the resurrection there is no marriage, but we will be like the angels.”
8. How did Jesus prove that there is a resurrection?
a. Jesus said that at the burning bush God said to Moses, “I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob,” and he is the God of the living and not of the dead.”
9. What question did Jesus ask of his enemies about David’s words ‘The LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand’?
a. He asked, “If Christ is David’s son, then why did David call him Lord?”
10. How did Jesus warn the people about the scribes?
a. Jesus said that they made long prayers for a pretense, devoured widows houses, and they will receive the greater damnation.
Memory verse: “The LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool.”—Psalm 110:1
Lesson 17: Controversy with the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Herodians
1. What did the disciples ask Jesus after he had spoken of the destruction of Jerusalem?
a. The disciples asked, “What will be the sign of your coming and of the end of the world?”
2. What signs did Jesus speak about that will indicate his second coming?
a. Jesus said, “There will be wars and rumors of wars and earthquakes in many places.”
3. About what did Jesus warn his disciples?
a. Jesus warned that there will be many deceivers and false prophets, iniquity will abound, and many will forsake the truth.
4. How did Jesus describe the power of the lie in the last days?
a. Jesus said that if it were possible the very elect would be deceived by the lie.
5. Did Jesus tell his disciples exactly when the end of the world will come?
a. He did not because no man knows the day nor the hour.
6. What did Jesus say about the relationship between the preaching of the gospel and the end of the world?
a. Jesus said, “This gospel of the kingdom must be preached to all nations, and then shall the end come.”
7. Did Jesus say that his church will suffer at the end?
a. Yes, Jesus warned of a great tribulation for the church.
8. How did Jesus comfort his church regarding that tribulation?
a. He said, “The days will be shortened for the elect’s sake.”
9. What parable did Jesus give concerning the signs of his coming?
a. Jesus told the parable of the fig tree: When we see a fig tree put out its leaves, then we know it is summer; so when we see these signs, we know the end is near.
10. To what did Jesus liken the days before the end of the world?
a. Jesus likened those days to the days of Noah when the flood came and destroyed the world; so Jesus also will come suddenly on the world and destroy it.
11. What calling did Jesus give to his church?
a. Jesus said, “Watch and be ready.”
Memory verse: “Be ye also ready: for in such an hour as ye think not the Son of man cometh.”—Matthew 24:44
Matthew 24; Mark 13; Luke 21:5–36
Lesson 18: Instruction Regarding the End of the World
1. How did Jesus describe the scene of the final judgment?
a. He said that the Son of man will come in his glory with his angels and will gather all nations before him for judgment.
2. How did Jesus describe his separation of the nations in the final judgment?
a. Jesus described the nations as sheep and goats, which he will separate.
3. What does that picture of separating sheep and goats mean?
a. Separating sheep and goats means that in the final judgment all men will be divided according to God’s decree of election and reprobation.
4. What significance does this have for the final judgment?
a. The significance is that God’s verdict in the final judgment was determined in his counsel from eternity and not by men’s works.
5. How did Jesus describe his elect sheep?
a. Jesus called them the righteous.
6. How are the elect righteous?
a. The elect are righteous by faith only and not by their works.
7. How did Jesus demonstrate that the elect are his own people?
a. He showed how his elect people had treated Christ as he came to them in various circumstances in this life.
8. What did Jesus call the reprobate goats?
a. Jesus called them the wicked.
9. How did Jesus show their wickedness?
a. Jesus showed how the reprobate wicked had mistreated him as he came to them through his people, those who were strangers, sick, hungry, thirsty, and naked.
10. What will Christ say to his elect sheep in the final judgment?
a. Christ will say, “Come, ye blessed of the Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world.”
11. What will Christ say to the reprobate goats in the final judgment?
a. Christ will say, “Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire prepared for the devil and his angels.”

Memory verse: “I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works.”—Revelation 20:12
Matthew 25:31–46
Lesson 19: The Parable of the Final Judgment
1. Where did Christ eat the last passover before his death?
a. Christ ate the passover with his twelve disciples in a large upper room in Jerusalem.
2. What did Christ say to his disciples concerning the last passover?
a. Christ said, “I will not eat of it anymore until it be fulfilled in the kingdom of God.”
3. To what did Christ refer when he said that?
a. Christ referred to his death as the fulfillment of the passover.
4. What sacrament did Christ institute for his church after the last passover?
a. Christ gave the sign and seal of the Lord’s supper to his church.
5. What sign did Christ give to his disciples?
a. He took bread, broke it, gave it to the disciples, and said, “Take, eat, this is my body which is broken for you.”
6. What other sign did Christ give to his disciples?
a. He took wine and said, “This cup is the new testament in my blood, which is shed for you and for many for the remission of sins.”
7. How did Christ come into this kingdom?
a. Christ entered his kingdom through his death and resurrection.
8. How do we come into the kingdom?
a. We enter the kingdom through the remission of our sins.
9. What is the significance in the Lord’s supper of eating the bread and drinking the wine?
a. The significance is that we eat and drink Jesus Christ by faith and are more and more united to his sacred body.
10. What did Christ promise to his disciples that night in the upper room?
a. Christ promised that he would not drink of the fruit of the vine until he drank it new with them in his Father’s kingdom.
11. What did Christ say about his Father’s kingdom?
a. Christ appointed to us a kingdom as his Father had appointed a kingdom to him.
Memory verse: “Of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption.”—1 Corinthians 1:30
Lesson 20: Christ in the Upper Room and His Institution of the Lord’s Supper
1. Where did Jesus take his disciples after the last passover in the upper room?
a. Jesus and his disciples crossed the brook Kidron and came into the garden of Gethsemane.
2. What did Jesus do there?
a. He prayed to God, “If it be possible, let this cup pass from me, nevertheless not my will, but thine be done.”
3. How did Christ show his suffering under the weight of our sins in the Garden?
a. He sweat great drops of blood.
4. Who appeared to Jesus in Gethsemane?
a. An angel from heaven came to strengthen him.
5. Who came into the garden after Jesus had finished praying?
a. The traitor Judas Iscariot came with a multitude that had swords and staves from the chief priests.
6. What sign had Judas given to the soldiers?
a. Judas said that he would betray Jesus with a kiss.
7. How did Jesus show that he went to his suffering willingly?
a. Jesus told the disciples that if he asked, God would give him twelve legions of angels.
8. What did Peter do in the garden?
a. Peter struck the high priest’s servant and cut off his ear.
9. What did Jesus tell Peter?
a. Jesus said, “Put away your sword, for those who take the sword will perish with the sword.”
10. What did Jesus teach by that?
a. He taught that the kingdom of God does not come through a man’s physical fight but only through the cross of Christ and his righteousness.
11. What did the disciples do when they saw Jesus willingly give himself up to the soldiers?
a. The disciples were offended at him, so they forsook Jesus and fled.

Memory verse: “Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, and against the man that is my fellow, saith the LORD of hosts: smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered: and I will turn mine hand upon the little ones.”—Zechariah 13:7
Lesson 21: Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane
1. Where did the soldiers bring Jesus first?
a. The soldiers brought Jesus first to Caiaphas, the high priest, and his father-in-law Annas.
2. What did Peter do after Jesus was captured?
a. Peter followed Jesus to the high priest’s house.
3. What happened to Peter there?
a. Three times Peter was accused of knowing Jesus, and three times he denied that he knew Jesus.
4. What did Peter do when the cock crew that morning?
a. Peter remembered Jesus’ words to him that he would deny him before the cock crew, and Peter went out and wept bitterly.
5. How did the Sanhedrin first try to condemn Jesus?
a. The Sanhedrin hired false witnesses, who testified that Jesus had said that he would destroy the temple and raise it in three days.
6. What had Jesus said?
a. Jesus had said that the Jews would destroy the temple of his body, and he would raise it on the third day.
7. When the false witnesses could not agree, what did the high priest do?
a. The high priest put Jesus under oath to tell the Sanhedrin whether he was the Christ, the Son of God.
8. How did Jesus respond?
a. Jesus said, “I am: and hereafter you will see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power and coming in the clouds of heaven.”
9. What did the high priest say?
a. He said that Jesus had spoken blasphemy.
10. What did the Sanhedrin do to Jesus after that?
a. The Sanhedrin blindfolded Jesus, buffeted him, spit on him, and said “Prophesy, thou Christ, who is he that smote thee?”
Memory verse: “I am a worm, and no man; a reproach of the men, and despised of the people.”—Psalm 22:6
Lesson 22: The King Before the Sanhedrin
1. Of what did the Jews accuse Jesus before Pilate?
a. The Jews said, “He perverts the nation, forbids the people to give tribute to Caesar, and says that he is Christ, a king.”
2. What did Pilate tell the Jews after he had examined Jesus?
a. Pilate said, “I find no fault in this man.”
3. What did Pilate understand about the reason that the Jews had sent Jesus to him?
a. Pilate knew that for envy the Jews had delivered Jesus to him.
4. How did Pilate make a spectacle of Jesus Christ?
a. Pilate arrayed Jesus in a purple robe, brought him out to the people, and said, “Behold, your king!”
5. What did the Jews say?
a. The Jews said, “We have no king but Caesar.”
6. Whom did the Jews want to be released instead of Jesus?
a. The Jews wanted the murderer Barabbas released.
7. What did the Jews respond when Pilate asked what he should do with Jesus?
a. The chief priests persuaded the Jews to demand that Pilate crucify Jesus.
8. What did Pilate do when he saw that he could not change the mind of the Jews?
a. Pilate washed his hands and said, “I am free of the blood of this innocent man.”
9. How did the Jews respond?
a. The Jews said, “His blood be on us, and on our children.”
10. What did Pilate’s soldiers do to Jesus?
a. The soldiers dressed Jesus in a purple robe, put a crown of thorns on his head, and mocked him, saying, “Hail, King of the Jews!”
Memory verse: “He was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed.”—Isaiah 53:5
Lesson 23: The King Before Pilate
1. Where did the soldiers crucify Jesus?
a. The soldiers crucified Jesus at Golgotha between two thieves.
2. What did Pilate write on the cross above Jesus’ head?
a. Pilate wrote the superscription: “JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS.”
3. How did the soldiers mock Jesus?
a. The soldiers taunted, “If thou be the king of the Jews, save thyself!”
4. How did the two thieves mock Jesus?
a. They said, “If thou be the Christ, save thyself and us.”
5. What did the chief priests and the people say to Jesus?
a. They said, “If he be the King of Israel, let him now come down from the cross, and we will believe him.”
6. How did one thief show that Jesus did indeed save him?
a. The thief said, “Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom.”
7. How did Jesus comfort that thief?
a. Jesus said, “Today, shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
8. What did Jesus cry during the three hours of darkness?
a. He cried, “My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?”
9. What happened when Jesus died?
a. When Jesus died, the veil of the temple was rent, the earth quaked, and the graves of many saints were opened.
10. Who asked Pilate for the body of Jesus?
a. Joseph of Arimathea asked for the body, and he laid it in his own new tomb.
Memory verse: “Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain.”—Acts 2:23
Lesson 24: The Death of the King
1. What happened on the Sunday after Jesus Christ was buried?
a. Jesus arose from the dead as the victor over sin and death.
2. Who came first to the tomb Sunday morning?
a. Mary Magdalene and the other women came to anoint Jesus’ body with spices.
3. What did the angel at the tomb tell the woman?
a. The angel said, “He is not here: for he is risen, as he said.”
4. Who appeared to the woman as they returned to tell the disciples?
a. Jesus came to the women, and they held him by the feet and worshiped him.
5. What did Peter find when he went into the tomb later that day?
a. Peter saw the linen grave clothes and the napkin that had been around Jesus’ face folded by itself in a corner.
6. What did Jesus tell the disciples when he appeared to them on a mountain in Galilee?
a. Jesus said, “All power is given to me in heaven and on earth.”
7. What did Jesus instruct the disciples to do after he had ascended into heaven?
a. Jesus told the disciples to teach all nations and baptize them.
8. What promise did Jesus give to his church at that time?
a. Jesus said, “Lo, I am with you always, even unto the end of the world.”
9. By whose power did Jesus arise from the dead?
a. God raised him, and Jesus arose by his own power as the Word of God.
10. Why did Jesus Christ rise from the dead?
a. He arose because he had justified his elect church and saved them from bondage to the law by his death.

Memory verse: “Therefore being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him, that of the fruit of his loins, according to the flesh, he would raise up Christ to sit on his throne.”—Acts 2:30
Matthew 28; Mark 16:1–18; Luke 23:50–56; Luke 24:1–48; John 20–21
Lesson 25: The Resurrection of the King
1. What did Jesus do for forty days after his resurrection?
a. Jesus appeared to his disciples and instructed them concerning the kingdom of heaven.
2. What happened forty days after Jesus’ resurrection?
a. Jesus Christ ascended up into heaven in the sight of his disciples.
3. What was the ascension?
a. By the wonder of grace, Jesus Christ was taken up from the earth to heaven and set at God’s right hand.
4. What did Christ do in heaven at his ascension?
a. Christ cast Satan out of heaven and cleansed the heavenly sanctuary.
5. What did God give to Christ at his ascension?
a. God gave the Holy Spirit to Jesus Christ to be his Spirit.
6. What did Jesus tell the disciples at his ascension?
a. Jesus told them to wait in Jerusalem for the promise of the Spirit.
7. Who is that Spirit?
a. The Spirit is God as the third person of the Trinity.
8. How is Christ with his church always when he is in heaven?
a. Christ is with his church by his Spirit.
9. What did the angels tell the disciples at the ascension?
a. The angels said that Jesus would return as the disciples had seen him go.
10. What does Christ do in heaven for his church?
a. Christ rules over all things in the interest of his church and makes intercession for his people.
Memory verse: “He that descended is the same also that ascended up far above all heavens, that he might fill all things.”—Ephesians 4:10
Lesson 26: The Ascension of the King
1. What glory did Christ receive in the ascension?
a. Christ received all power and authority in heaven and on earth, and he rules over all things as king.
2. How is the glorified Christ pictured in Revelation 5?
a. Christ is pictured as the slain Lamb, who has seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God.
3. What is the significance of this description of Christ?
a. The significance is that Christ received of God the Holy Spirit, who is the power by which Christ does all things.
4. How is Christ’s power and authority pictured in the vision?
a. The slain Lamb takes the book with the seven seals from the hand of God, who sits on the throne.
5. What is that book?
a. The book is the counsel of God for the full revelation of God’s eternal kingdom.
6. What does it mean that Christ opens the seals of this book?
a. That Christ opens the seals means that he makes known the eternal purpose of God for his kingdom.
7. What else does it mean that Christ opens the seals?
a. It also means that Christ controls all things that must come to pass so that God’s kingdom may come in its perfection.
8. When will the kingdom of God be fully revealed?
a. The kingdom of God will be fully revealed when Christ comes again and declares himself to be judge of the quick and the dead.
9. Will Christ then stop being king over all?
a. No, Christ is an eternal king, and he shall rule the new heavens and earth, world without end.
10. What makes Christ worthy to take and open that book?
a. Christ is worthy because he prevailed at his cross and resurrection over sin and Satan.
11. What is the power of the cross?
a. The power of the cross is the blood of the cross by which Christ redeemed his entire elect church to God.
Memory verse: “Worthy is the Lamb that was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honour, and glory, and blessing.”—Revelation 5:12

Grades 5–8
Introductory Notes

The Heidelberg Catechism is one of our Reformed creeds. The other two Reformed creeds are the Belgic Confession and the Canons of Dordrecht. Together these three creeds make up the three forms of unity. These three creeds are called the forms of unity because in their confession of the truth they serve as the basis for unity among the churches where they are confessed. These three forms of unity together with the Apostles’ Creed, the Nicene Creed, and the Athanasian Creed make up the six creeds that are authoritative statements of doctrine in the Reformed Protestant Churches.

The word creed is taken from the Latin word credo. That word means I believe. A creed is an official statement adopted by the churches as a summary of what those churches believe to be the truth of the word of God. These creeds are also called symbols. The word symbol refers to a flag or banner. These creeds are the flags or banners that the churches fly and that distinguish them in their confession of the truth. These creeds are also called confessions. The word confession means to say together with someone else. The creeds are a statement of believers together with one another as churches and with their Lord Jesus Christ of the truth of the word of God.

The authority of the creeds in their confession of the truth is strictly derivative. The sole and unquestioned authority of all things in the churches is the infallibly inspired word of God found in the sixty-six books of the Bible. The creeds have authority in the churches because they fully agree with the word of God. The authority of the creeds is the truth of the word of God that they teach.

Because the creeds do fully agree with the word of God and teach the truth of the word of God, the officebearers of the churches subscribe to the creeds by signing the Formula of Subscription. In this subscription they promise faithfully to teach and to defend the truth of the creeds and never publicly or privately to contradict the creeds without first revealing their disagreement to the consistory or classis of the churches.

The Belgic Confession was written in 1561 by the Reformed pastor Guido de Brès during the terrible persecution and slaughter of the Reformed Christians in the Lowlands of Europe by Philip, the evil king of Spain. The Belgic Confession was written as a defense of the Reformed faith as faithful to the Word of God.

The Canons of Dordrecht were written in 1618–19 by the great Synod of Dordrecht. The Canons was written as a defense of the Reformed truth of salvation by grace alone rooted in God’s eternal election, and the Canons was written as a condemnation of the Arminian false doctrine of salvation by the willing and working of man.

The Heidelberg Catechism was originally written by the order of Elector Frederick of the territory of the Palatinate in Germany. Its name Heidelberg refers to the capital city of Frederick’s domain. Frederick was Reformed in his convictions and desired a creed that reflected those convictions both for himself and for the people of his territory. Frederick intended that the Catechism be used for the instruction of the youth and that it be preached in the churches every Lord’s day. For this reason the Catechism is divided into fifty-two Lord’s Days with the intention that one Lord’s Day be preached every Sunday of the year and that this practice be repeated year after year.

The Heidelberg Catechism was written mainly by two men: Zacharias Ursinus and Caspar Olevianus. Ursinus was the professor of theology at the University of Heidelberg, and Olevianus was the court preacher to Frederick. The Heidelberg Catechism was published in 1563. Although it was written for Frederick and his people, the Heidelberg Catechism quickly found wide acceptance throughout the Reformed churches of Europe. The Catechism was officially adopted as the creed of the Dutch Reformed Churches by the Synod of Dordrecht in 1618–19.

The theme of the Heidelberg Catechism is the believer’s only comfort in life and in death. This only comfort is that he belongs to his faithful savior, Jesus Christ. The Catechism develops this theme in three main sections over fifty-two Lord’s Days. The first section explains how great our sins and miseries are and runs from Lord’s Days 2 to 4. The second section explains how we are delivered from our sins and miseries by our Lord Jesus Christ and runs from Lord’s Days 5 to 31. The third section explains how we are to show our gratitude to God for our deliverance and runs from Lord’s Days 32 to 52.
Formula of Subscription

We, the undersigned, professors of the Reformed Protestant Churches, ministers of the gospel, elders and deacons of the Reformed Protestant congregation of ................................................ , do hereby sincerely and in good conscience before the Lord declare by this, our subscription, that we heartily believe and are persuaded that all the articles and points of doctrine contained in the Confession and Catechism of the Reformed Churches, together with the explanation of some points of the aforesaid doctrine made by the National Synod of Dordrecht, 1618–19, do fully agree with the Word of God.
We promise therefore diligently to teach and faithfully to defend the aforesaid doctrine, without either directly or indirectly contradicting the same, by our public preaching or writing.

We declare, moreover, that we not only reject all errors that militate against this doctrine, and particularly those which were condemned by the above-mentioned synod, but that we are disposed to refute and contradict these, and to exert ourselves in keeping the church free from such errors. And if hereafter any difficulties or different sentiments respecting the aforesaid doctrines should arise in our minds, we promise that we will neither publicly nor privately propose, teach, or defend the same, either by preaching or writing, until we have first revealed such sentiments to the consistory, classis, and synod, that the same may be there examined, being ready always cheerfully to submit to the judgment of the consistory, classis, and synod, under the penalty in case of refusal to be, by that very fact, suspended from our office.

And further, if at any time the consistory, classis, or synod, upon sufficient grounds of suspicion and to preserve the uniformity and purity of doctrine, may deem it proper to require of us a further explanation of our sentiments respecting any particular article of the Confession of Faith, the Catechism, or the explanation of the national synod, we do hereby promise to be always willing and ready to comply with such requisition, under the penalty above mentioned, reserving for ourselves, however, the right of an appeal, whenever we shall believe ourselves aggrieved by the sentence of the consistory, the classis, or the synod, and until a decision is made upon such an appeal, we will acquiesce in the determination and judgment already passed.
Frederick’s Preface to the Heidelberg Catechism

We, Frederic, Archcarver and Elector of the Holy Roman Empire, Duke in Bavaria, by the grace of God, Elector Palatine on the Rhine, etc., present to all and each of our Superintendents, Pastors, Preachers, Officers of the Church, and Schoolmasters, throughout our Electorate of the Rhenish Palatinate, our grace and greeting, and do them; herewith, to wit:

Inasmuch as we acknowledge that we are bound by the admonition of the Divine word, and also by natural duty and relation, and have finally determined to order and administer our office, calling, and government, not only for the promotion and maintenance of quiet and peaceable living, and for the support of upright and virtuous walk and conversation among our subjects, but also and above all, constantly to admonish and lead them to devout knowledge and fear of the Almighty and his holy word of salvation, as the only foundation of all virtue and obedience, and to spare no pains, so far as in us Iies, with all sincerity to promote their temporal and eternal welfare, and to contribute to the defence and maintenance of the same:

And, although apprised on entering upon our government, how our dear cousins and predecessors, Counts Palatine, Electors, etc., of noble and blessed memory, have instituted and proposed divers Christian and profitable measures and appliances for the furtherance of the glory of God and the upholding of civil discipline and order:

Notwithstanding this purpose was not in every respect prosecuted with the appropriate zeal, and the expected and desired fruit did not accrue therefrom, we are now induced not only to renew the same, but also, as the exigencies of the times demand, to improve, reform, and further to establish them. Therefore we also have ascertained that by no means the least defect of our system is found in the fact, that our blooming youth is disposed to be careless in respect to Christian doctrine, both in the schools and churches of our principality—some, indeed, being entirely without Christian instruction, others being unsystematically taught, without any established, certain, and clear catechism, but merely according to individual plan or judgment; from which, among other great defects, the consequence has ensued, that they have, in too many instances, grown up without the fear of God and the knowledge of His word, having enjoyed no profitable instruction, or otherwise have been perplexed with irrelevant and needless questions, and at times have been burdened with unsound doctrines.

And now, whereas both temporal and spiritual offices, government and family discipline, cannot otherwise be maintained—and in order that discipline and obedience to authority and all other virtues may increase and be multiplied among subjects—it is essential that our youth be trained in early life, and above all, in the pure and consistent doctrine of the holy gospel, and be well exercised in the proper and true knowledge of God:

Therefore, we have regarded it as a high obligation, and as the most important duty of our government, to give attention to this matter, to do away with this defect, and to introduce the needful improvements:

And accordingly, with the advice, and cooperation of our entire theological faculty in this place, and of all Superintendents and distinguished servants of the Church, we have secured the preparation of a summary course of instruction or catechism of our Christian Religion, according to the word of God, in the German and Latin languages; in order not only that the youth in churches and schools may be piously instructed in such Christian doctrine, and be thoroughly trained therein, but also that the Pastors and Schoolmasters themselves may be provided with a fixed form and model, by which to regulate the instruction of youth, and not, at their option, adopt daily changes, or introduce erroneous doctrine:

We do herewith affectionately admonish and enjoin upon every one of you, that you do, for the honour of God and our subjects, and also for the sake of your own soul’s profit and welfare, thankfully accept this proffered Catechism or course of instruction, and that you do diligently and faithfully represent and explain the same according to its true import, to the youth in our schools and churches, and also from the pulpit to the common people, that you teach, and act, and live in accordance with it, in the assured hope, that if our youth in early life are earnestly instructed and educated in the word of God, it will please Almighty God also to grant reformation of public and private morals, and temporal and eternal welfare. Desiring, as above said, that all this may be accomplished, we have made this provision.
Given at Heidelberg, Tuesday, the nineteenth of January, in the year 1563 after the birth of Christ, our dear Lord and Saviour.
Source: Original Preface of Heidelberg Catechism (1563).pdf
Guido de Brès’ Preface to the Belgic Confession
Dedicatory Epistle to Reformed Confession of Faith, addressed to Philip II, 1561
The Faithful in the Low Countries who Desire to Live according to the True Reformation of the Gospel of Our Lord Jesus Christ to the Invincible King Philip their Sovereign Lord.

If we had been permitted, most gracious Lord, to appear before your Majesty to defend ourselves against the charges which have been laid against us, we would not have resorted to this clandestine means to bring to your notice the lamentations of your people, sending you a dumb request or a written confession. But our enemies have stopped your ears with so many false accusations and reports that we are not only prevented from appearing before you, but driven from your territories, murdered and burnt wherever we may be. Therefore grant us at least, most gracious Lord, in the name of God that which no one can deny even to the animals, namely to hear from afar our cries. If your Majesty, having heard us, judges us to be guilty, let the burnings increase and the tortures and torments multiply throughout your kingdom. And contrariwise, if our innocence is manifest to you, may you be for us a support and refuge against the violence of our enemies.

But alas, most gracious Lord, if it is sufficient merely to make accusations and if the accused is deprived of an opportunity to defend himself against the charges, who would be found righteous? Whose innocence would be safe among men? We are, it is alleged, disobedient rebels, intent only on the destruction of civil government, throwing the world into confusion and disorder, bent only on breaking free from your authority and power, indeed on snatching the scepter from your hands. Crimes unworthy of our confession, unworthy of a Christian, indeed unworthy of any man, but crimes which justify that ancient saying of the Tyrants: the Christians to the lions. But it is not enough to accuse; the charges must be proved. The Prophets, the Apostles and even the members of the primitive Church of Jesus Christ were accused, indeed to outward appearances and in the carnal opinion of man, overwhelmed with similar calumnies. But just as these in their day publicly testified and protested, so also do we protest and testify before God and His angels that we desire nothing but to live in obedience to the magistrates with clear consciences, to serve God, to reform our lives in conformity with His Word and holy commandments.

Besides the secret witness of our consciences, those who sit in judgment at our trials, and pass sentence on us, shall testify truly they have never found in us any inclination to disobey, to conspire against your Majesty or to disturb the public peace. On the contrary, they have discovered that in our assemblies we pray for earthly kings and princes and especially for you, most gracious Lord, and for those whom you have charged with the regiment and the government of your countries, provinces and lordships. For we are instructed both by the Word of God and by the continual teaching of our ministers that kings, princes and magistrates are ordained of God and that whoever resists the authorities, resists the ordinance of God and shall be condemned. We own and acknowledge that by God's eternal wisdom kings rule and princes dispense justice; in short, that they have come [to their office] not by usurpation or by tyranny, but by God's own ordinance. And to prove that this is not only professed with our lips but imprinted and engraved in our hearts, who among us has ever refused to pay the tribute or tolls laid on by you, most gracious Lord? On the contrary, we have been as prompt to obey as the ordinance to pay. What weapons, what plots have ever been uncovered, even though we have been so cruelly tortured and tormented by those who cover themselves with your name and authority in order that they might commit all manner of cruelties which would be enough to provoke the patience of the kindest and gentlest and bring about rage and despair? But we thank our God that the blood shed by our brethren for our cause and suit, or rather for the cause of Jesus Christ cries out in witness to the truth. The banishments, prisons, racks, exiles, tortures and countless other persecutions plainly demonstrate that our desire and conviction is not carnal, for we would lead a far easier life if we did not embrace and maintain this doctrine. But having the fear of God before our eyes, and being in dread of the warning of Jesus Christ, who tells us that He shall forsake us before God and His Father if we deny Him before men, we suffer our backs to be beaten, our tongues to be cut, our mouths to be gagged and our whole body to be burnt, for we know that he who would follow Christ must take up his cross and deny himself. A reasonable man would never suppose, unless he were purblind and robbed of his senses, that those who leave their own country, their family and friends in order to live in peace and tranquility would seek to throw everything into confusion; or that those who die for the gospel, where it is written, ‘Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s and to God the things that are God’s’ would ever attempt to take the crown from the King, or engage in any conspiracy against him. While they offer and submit their bodies and property to the King, they humbly pray his Majesty that they may be permitted to render to God what He requires and what we cannot properly refuse because He has made us His own and bought us for a very high price.

May our enemies not so abuse your goodness and patience that you are obliged to listen to them when they tell you that only our small numbers restrain us from rebellion, as if each of us were in his heart disobedient and seditious and were only awaiting larger numbers to wage war, to assail you and to give free rein to his fury. Let them misrepresent matters to their heart’s desire, but we assure you, most gracious Lord, that more than one hundred thousand men in these your Low Countries hold and follow the Religion, whose confession we now present to you, and yet you see none of these making preparations for rebellion, or even hear a word from them of that sort. We do not mention, most gracious Lord, the large number of our brethren in order to frighten or intimidate in any way your officers and servants, but merely to refute the calumnies of those who would render us odious by their lies and also to move you to compassion. For, alas, if you extend your arm to imbrue and to wash it in the blood of so many men, good God, what destruction shall you inflict on your subjects, what wounds on your own people, what crying, groaning and sobbing from women, children, family and friends? What eye could remain dry and look on without weeping while so many honorable burghers, esteemed by all and hated by none, are, after a dark and hideous confinement and after enduring the rack and persecution, delivered to a shameful death and torment, which is the cruelest and most barbaric any pagan and unholy tyrant could devise? And meanwhile their wives, if they have already escaped, wander in foreign parts, going from door to door to beg for bread with their children about their necks. Most gracious Lord, let not posterity describe your reign as cruel and bloody, let it not be said that the honour of your forefathers, the greatness of your father and your own virtues were overshadowed by cruelty, a cruelty, I say, which may be natural in beasts but which is beneath the dignity of man and most unworthy of, indeed foreign to, a Prince whose greatness and chief virtue lies above all in mercy and gentleness, which qualities distinguish a true king from a tyrant.

And because we are persecuted as if we were not only enemies of your throne and the commonwealth, but also enemies of God and His Church, we humbly pray you to judge this by the Confession of faith, which we lay before you and which we are ready and willing, if necessary, to seal with our own blood. From this Confession we trust that you will see that we are wrongly called schismatics, promoters of disunity, rebels and heretics, for we not only uphold and profess the chief heads of the Christian faith comprehended in the Symbolum or Creeds, but also the whole teaching revealed by Jesus Christ for our life, justification and salvation, proclaimed by the evangelists and apostles, sealed with the blood of so many martyrs and preserved pure and complete by the primitive Church until at length it was perverted through the ignorance, greed and ambition of her ministers with human inventions and additions, contrary to the purity of the gospel. Our opponents shamelessly deny that this Gospel is the power of God, bringing salvation to all who believe, when they condemn and kill us because we do not uphold what is not found there [in the Gospel]. However they cannot excuse their blasphemy against the Holy Spirit by saying that all the treasures of God's wisdom and all the means, which are more than sufficient for our salvation, are not contained in the Old and New Testaments, but that their inventions are necessary and that anyone who does not consider these to be of like or greater worth to the Gospel is damned, unworthy of human society, his body to be destroyed and his soul to be cast into the abyss of hell. Our weak flesh trembles at such words, terrified by the threats of those who have the power to reduce us to ashes. But from the other side we hear what the Apostle says: ‘Though an angel were to descend from heaven and to preach anything other than the Gospel, let him be accursed’ [Gal. 1. v. 8]. We hear St. John who concluded his prophecy with these words: ‘For I warn every man that hears the prophecies in this book: if anyone adds anything to them, God shall add to him the plagues described in this book’ [Rev. 22 v. 18]. In short, we see that we are bidden to follow only the Word of God and not what we think fit, and that we are forbidden to add or subtract anything from the holy commandments of Almighty God. Jesus Christ tells us that He has imparted to us everything which He had heard from His Father. If he concealed nothing from the Apostles on account of their weakness, which He had promised to reveal to them (by the Holy Spirit, which He would send to them), we may be certain that He, who is Truth itself, has kept His promise. Therefore the mysteries are contained in the Gospel and the writings of the Apostles, which were written after the said promise and the reception of the Holy Spirit. It is therefore evident that those who interpret the ‘mystery’ which the Apostles could not bear to name to mean their ceremonies and vain superstitions, contrary to the Word of God, abuse the words of the evangelist. We would offer to show this—as we easily could—from the testimony of the scriptures, were we, being mindful of the form and brevity proper to an epistle, not afraid of troubling your Majesty. We humbly pray you in the name of He who has established and maintained you in your kingdom that you do not suffer those who are carried away by greed, ambition and other wicked affections to use your power, authority and dominion to satisfy their own appetites in order to defile and gorge themselves with the blood of your subjects while they bury all good zeal, inspired by the fear of God and piety, under the words ‘sedition’, ‘rebellion’, ‘scandal’ and such like in order to bring your wrath upon us. But alas, most gracious Lord, remember that the world has always hated the light and rebelled against the truth. Is he who has the word of truth in his mouth a rebel merely because he is opposed by men? On the contrary, rebelliousness and scandal should be imputed to him who is the implacable enemy of God and men, namely the Devil, who in order not to lose his own kingdom, which consists in idolatry, false worship, fornication and countless other vices, that the Gospel has excised, stirs up rebellion and tumult to hinder the advance [of the Word of truth]. In addition there is the ingratitude of the world, which, instead of receiving thankfully the Word of its Master, Shepherd and God, sets itself against the same for no other reason than that it has lived for a long time in error. By making this appeal to the prescription of time the world tries to withstand Him who made the world and all times and to Whom all things are present. It is your duty, most gracious Lord, to take cognizance of these matters, to oppose error, no matter how deeply rooted by the passage of time, and to defend the innocence of those who have hitherto been oppressed, rather than heard, by the courts.
May the Lord bless you and keep you, the Lord make His face shine on you and protect and maintain you in all prosperity. Amen.

Source: French and Dutch versions in De Nederlandse belijdenisgeschriften, ed. J. N. Bakhuizen van den Brink, 2nd ed. (Amsterdam, 1976), 62–69. A modernized Dutch text, in J. N. Bakhuizen van den Brink, Protestantse pleidooien, II (Kampen, 1962), 153–59.
Conclusion of the Canons of Dordrecht

And this is the perspicuous, simple, and ingenuous declaration of the orthodox doctrine respecting the five articles which have been controverted in the Belgic churches, and the rejection of the errors, with which they have for some time been troubled. This doctrine the synod judges to be drawn from the Word of God, and to be agreeable to the confessions of the Reformed churches. Whence it clearly appears that some whom such conduct by no means became have violated all truth, equity, and charity, in wishing to persuade the public: That the doctrine of the Reformed churches concerning predestination, and the points annexed to it, by its own genius and necessary tendency, leads off the minds of men from all piety and religion; that it is an opiate administered by the flesh and the devil, and the stronghold of Satan, where he lies in wait for all, and from which he wounds multitudes and mortally strikes through many with the darts both of despair and security; that it makes God the author of sin, unjust, tyrannical, hypocritical; that it is nothing more than interpolated Stoicism, Manicheism, Libertinism, Turcism; that it renders men carnally secure, since they are persuaded by it that nothing can hinder the salvation of the elect, let them live as they please; and, therefore, that they may safely perpetrate every species of the most atrocious crimes; and that, if the reprobate should even perform truly all the works of the saints, their obedience would not in the least contribute to their salvation; that the same doctrine teaches that God, by a mere arbitrary act of His will, without the least respect or view to any sin, has predestinated the greatest part of the world to eternal damnation, and has created them for this very purpose; that in the same manner in which the election is the fountain and the cause of faith and good works, reprobation is the cause of unbelief and impiety; that many children of the faithful are torn, guiltless, from their mothers’ breasts and tyrannically plunged into hell, so that neither baptism, nor the prayers of the church at their baptism, can at all profit them; and many other things of the same kind, which the Reformed churches not only do not acknowledge, but even detest with their whole soul.

Wherefore, this synod of Dordt, in the name of the Lord, conjures as many as piously call upon the name of our Savior Jesus Christ to judge of the faith of the Reformed churches, not from the calumnies which on every side are heaped upon it; nor from the private expressions of a few among ancient and modern teachers, often dishonestly quoted or corrupted and wrested to a meaning quite foreign to their intention; but from the public confessions of the churches themselves, and from the declaration of the orthodox doctrine, confirmed by the unanimous consent of all and each of the members of the whole synod. Moreover, the synod warns calumniators themselves to consider the terrible judgment of God which awaits them for bearing false witness against the confessions of so many churches, for distressing the consciences of the weak, and for laboring to render suspected the society of the truly faithful.

Finally, this synod exhorts all their brethren in the gospel of Christ to conduct themselves piously and religiously in handling this doctrine, both in the universities and churches; to direct it, as well in discourse as in writing, to the glory of the divine name, to holiness of life, and to the consolation of afflicted souls; to regulate, by the Scripture, according to the analogy of faith, not only their sentiments but also their language; and to abstain from all those phrases which exceed the limits necessary to be observed in ascertaining the genuine sense of the Holy Scriptures, and may furnish insolent sophists with a just pretext for violently assailing, or even vilifying, the doctrine of the Reformed churches.

May Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who, seated at the Father’s right hand, gives gifts to men, sanctify us in the truth, bring to the truth those who err, shut the mouths of the calumniators of sound doctrine, and endue the faithful minister of His Word with the spirit of wisdom and discretion, that all their discourses may tend to the glory of God and the edification of those who hear them. Amen.
That this is our faith and decision we certify by subscribing our names.

Here follow the names, not only of president, assistant president, and secretaries of the synod, and of the professors of theology in the Dutch churches, but of all the members who were deputed to the synod, as the representatives of their respective churches, that is, of the delegates from Great Britain, the Electoral Palatinate, Hessia, Switzerland, Wetteraw, the Republic and Church of Geneva, the Republic and Church of Bremen, the Republic and Church of Emden, the Duchy of Gelderland and of Zutphen, South Holland, North Holland, Zeeland, the Province of Utrecht, Friesland, Transylvania, the State of Groningen and Omland, Drent, the French churches.
Source: The Confessions and the Church Order of the Protestant Reformed Churches, 179–80.
Read: The Formula of Subscription; Frederick’s Preface to the Heidelberg Catechism; Guido de Brès’ Preface to the Belgic Confession; The Conclusion of the Canons of Dordrecht; Lord’s Day 1
Introduction to the Belgic Confession, Canons of Dordtrecht, and Heidelberg Catechism

lessons.lesson: Introduction to the Belgic Confession, Canons of Dordtrecht, and Heidelberg Catechism

words.text: Q. 1. What is thy only comfort in life and death?

A. That I with body and soul, both in life and death, am not my own, but belong unto my faithful Savior Jesus Christ; who, with his precious blood, hath fully satisfied for all my sins, and delivered me from all the power of the devil; and so preserves me that without the will of my heavenly Father, not a hair can fall from my head; yea, that all things must be subservient to my salvation, and therefore, by his Holy Spirit, he also assures me of eternal life, and makes me sincerely willing and ready, henceforth, to live unto him.
Q. 2. How many things are necessary for thee to know, that thou, enjoying this comfort, mayest live and die happily?
A. Three: the first, how great my sins and miseries are; the second, how I may be delivered from all my sins and miseries; the third, how I shall express my gratitude to God for such deliverance.
Heidelberg Catechism Lord’s Day 1
Q. 3. Whence knowest thou thy misery?
A. Out of the law of God.
Q. 4. What doth the law of God require of us?

A. Christ teaches us that briefly, Matt. 22:37–40: “Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, with all thy soul, and with all thy mind, and with all thy strength. This is the first and the great commandment; and the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself. On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets.”
Q. 5. Canst thou keep all these things perfectly?
A. In no wise; for I am prone by nature to hate God and my neighbor.
Luke 10:27; Rom. 3:10, 20; Rom. 8:7; Titus 3:3; 1 John 1:8
Heidelberg Catechism Lord’s Day 2
Q. 6. Did God then create man so wicked and perverse?

A. By no means; but God created man good, and after his own image, in true righteousness and holiness, that he might rightly know God his Creator, heartily love him, and live with him in eternal happiness to glorify and praise him.
Q. 7. Whence then proceeds this depravity of human nature?
A. From the fall and disobedience of our first parents, Adam and Eve, in Paradise; hence our nature is become so corrupt, that we are all conceived and born in sin.
Q. 8. Are we then so corrupt that we are wholly incapable of doing any good, and inclined to all wickedness?
A. Indeed we are, except we are regenerated by the Spirit of God.
Heidelberg Catechism Lord’s Day 3
Q. 9. Doth not God then do injustice to man, by requiring from him in his law that which he cannot perform?
A. Not at all; for God made man capable of performing it; but man, by the instigation of the devil, and his own willful disobedience, deprived himself and all his posterity of those divine gifts.
Q. 10. Will God suffer such disobedience and rebellion to go unpunished?

A. By no means; but is terribly displeased with our original as well as actual sins; and will punish them in his just judgment temporally and eternally, as he hath declared, “Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them.”
Q. 11. Is not God then also merciful?

A. God is indeed merciful, but also just; therefore his justice requires that sin which is committed against the most high majesty of God be also punished with extreme, that is, with everlasting punishment of body and soul.
Heidelberg Catechism Lord’s Day 4
Q. 12. Since then, by the righteous judgment of God, we deserve temporal and eternal punishment, is there no way by which we may escape that punishment and be again received into favor?
A. God will have his justice satisfied; and therefore we must make this full satisfaction, either by ourselves or by another.
Q. 13. Can we ourselves then make this satisfaction?
A. By no means; but on the contrary we daily increase our debt.
Q. 14. Can there be found anywhere one who is a mere creature, able to satisfy for us?

A. None; for, first, God will not punish any other creature for the sin which man hath committed; and further, no mere creature can sustain the burden of God’s eternal wrath against sin, so as to deliver others from it.
Q. 15. What sort of a mediator and deliverer then must we seek for?
A. For one who is very man, and perfectly righteous; and yet more powerful than all creatures; that is, one who is also very God.
Heidelberg Catechism Lord’s Day 5
Q. 16. Why must he be very man, and also perfectly righteous?
