Lesson 20: The Necessity of the Mediator
1. What do the names of the mediator teach us?
a. The mediator’s names are revelations from God concerning the truth of our savior.
2. What are the principal names of the mediator?

a. The prophets said that the mediator would be called Immanuel; the angel of the Lord named him Jesus; scripture calls him Christ and Lord; and he referred to himself as the Son of God and the Son of man.
3. Does Jesus have other names in scripture?

a. Yes, Jesus is called the seed of the woman, the angel of Jehovah, the Word, the Lamb of God, the Lion of Judah’s tribe, a shoot out of the root of Jesse, a branch of David, the Alpha and the Omega, and Lord of lords and King of kings.
4. What is the meaning of the mediator’s name Jesus?
a. Jesus means that he is Jehovah God and that he saves his people from their sins.
5. What is the meaning of the Jesus’ title Christ?
a. The title Christ means that Jesus is ordained by the triune God and qualified with the Holy Spirit to be our prophet, priest, and king.
6. Why is Jesus called the Son of God?
a. Jesus is called the Son of God because he is the only-begotten Son of God.
7. Why is Jesus called Lord?
a. Jesus is called Lord because all authority is in his hands and because he redeemed and delivered us from all the power of the devil and made us his own property.

Lesson 21: The Names of the Mediator
1. Who is our savior?
a. Our savior is the only-begotten Son of God, and he is the true seed of David.
2. How many natures does Christ have?
a. Christ has two natures: a divine nature and a human nature.
3. What does it mean that Christ has a divine nature?

a. The divine nature means that Christ as the only-begotten Son of God is coequal and coeternal with the Father and the Spirit, so that Christ is God of God, Light of Light, true God of true God, and of the same essence as the Father.
4. What does it mean that Christ has a human nature?
a. The human nature means that the Son of God took to himself a nature from the virgin Mary, so that Jesus is a true man like his brethren in all things except sin.
5. Can we say more about the human nature that Christ took?
a. Yes, Christ’s human nature is a real, complete, weakened, sinless, and individual nature from the heart of the covenant line.
6. How does scripture indicate that Christ is a true man?
a. Scripture speaks of Christ as eating, drinking, sleeping, weeping, commending his spirit to the Father, and many other things that show he was like us in all things in body and soul.
7. How does scripture indicate that Christ is without his own sin?
a. Christ said, “Which of you convinceth me of sin,” and the writer to the Hebrews said that Christ was tempted as we are, “yet without sin.”

Lesson 22: The Person and Natures of Christ
1. What is the central truth concerning the two natures of Christ?
a. The central truth is that the two natures are united in the one person of the Son of God.
2. Why is that truth of the union of the two natures important?
a. That truth is important because in it we learn that everything that Christ did was the work of God.
3. What further importance is there to the truth of the union of the two natures?
a. That union is important because in Christ God and man became one so that in Christ’s person the covenant promise was fulfilled.
4. How are Christ’s two natures united?
a. Christ’s two natures are united without confusion, without change, without division, and without separation.
5. What does it mean that the natures of Christ are united without confusion?
a. Without confusion means that in the union, the natures remain distinct and do not become a single nature.
6. What does it mean that the natures of Christ are united without change?
a. Without change means that in the union each nature retains its own properties.
7. What does it mean that the natures of Christ are united without division and without separation?
a. Without division and separation means that the union is permanent and that there is constant communion between the divine and human natures in body and soul.

Lesson 23: The Union of the Two Natures of Christ
1. What office does Christ hold?
a. Christ holds the office of mediator of God’s covenant.
2. What is an office?
a. An office is the position in which one is the visible representative of the invisible God.
3. How did Christ receive his office?
a. God eternally appointed Christ to that office and anointed him with the Holy Spirit.
4. What is Christ’s work as mediator of the covenant?
a. Christ was appointed to reveal God and his eternal covenant of grace and to perfect that covenant in the glorification of God’s elect people in the new heaven and new earth.
5. How does Christ’s office relate to the kingdom of God?
a. Christ’s work to perfect God’s covenant means also the perfection of God’s kingdom when God will be all in all.
6. How does Christ’s office stand in relationship to Adam?
a. The relationship is that Christ is God’s eternal purpose and Adam was a temporary, earthly reflection of the coming glory of Christ.
7. What are the three aspects of Christ’s office as mediator of the covenant?
a. Christ is our chief prophet, our only high priest, and our eternal king.

Lesson 24: The Office of the Mediator
1. What does Christ do as our chief prophet?
a. As prophet Christ fully reveals to us the counsel of God for our salvation.
2. How does Christ carry out his office as prophet?

a. In the Old Testament Christ spoke through the patriarchs and prophets; in his coming he revealed God in his own person and works; and after his ascension Christ continues to teach through the church by his Word and Spirit.
3. What does Christ do as our only high priest?
a. As high priest Christ offered himself to God as the one, only sacrifice for sin that accomplished our salvation.
4. What else does Christ do as our high priest?
a. As high priest Christ makes continual intercession with the Father for us and blesses us with every spiritual blessing.
5. What did Christ do as our king?
a. As king Christ destroyed all the power of the devil, sin, death, and hell by his perfect work on the cross.
6. What does Christ continue to do are our king?
a. As king Christ rules and governs us by his Word and Spirit and defends and preserves us from all the powers of darkness unto eternal glory.
7. Does Christ’s church share in the glory of his anointing?
a. Yes, as Christians we are partakers of Christ’s anointing and are made prophets, priests, and kings to God in the office of all believer.

Lesson 25: The Three Aspects of Christ’s Office
1. What is the meaning of Christ’s state?
a. Christ’s state is his legal position before the tribunal of God, whether guilty or innocent.
2. What is Christ’s state of humiliation?
a. The state of humiliation is the legal position in which Christ stood before God as guilty because as the head of his people he was responsible for their sins.
3. What did Christ do in his state of humiliation?
a. Christ deeply humbled himself in our flesh before God in order to accomplish everlasting righteousness and salvation for us on the cross.
4. What was the first step of Christ’s state of humiliation?
a. Christ took our flesh from the virgin Mary in his incarnation.
5. How was Christ’s incarnation humiliation for Jesus Christ?
a. Though Christ remained true and eternal God, he came in the likeness of sinful flesh and humbled himself to the bitter and shameful death of the cross.
6. How else was Christ’s incarnation humiliation for him?
a. Christ was born of a woman and made under the law in order to satisfy God’s justice, redeem us from the curse of the law, and make atonement for the sins of God’s elect people.
7. How did Christ suffer all his life long?
a. Christ suffered under the curse of God his whole life, was despised and rejected of men, and endured the contradiction of sinners against himself.

Lesson 26: The State of Christ’s Humiliation (1): His Incarnation, Virgin Birth, and Lifelong Suffering
1. How else did Christ humble himself in his state of humiliation?
a. Jesus Christ humbled himself to the death of cross, died, was buried, and descended into hell in perfect obedience to the Father.
2. What is the significance of Christ’s death upon the cross?

a. The death of the cross was accursed of God, so we are assured that Jesus Christ took the curse of our sins on him, accomplished all righteousness, and fulfilled all God’s counsel for our salvation.
3. For whom did Christ die.
a. Christ died for his elect people and for them only.
4. Why did Christ have to die?
a. Christ had to die because with respect to God’s justice and truth, satisfaction for sin could not be made in any other way than by the death of the Son of God.
5. Why was Christ buried?
a. Christ was buried to show that he had entered fully into our misery and that Christ overcame the power of death and destroyed the corruption of the grave.
6. When did Christ descend into hell?
a. Christ descended into hell on the cross where he suffered all the pains and torments of hell in the place of his elect people.
7. What comfort is Christ’s incarnation and all his suffering for us?
a. Christ’s incarnation and suffering are comfort for us because he fully accomplished all our salvation, and there is nothing that we must do to be saved.

Lesson 27: The State of Christ’s Humiliation (2): His Cross, Burial, and Descent into Hell
Essentials of Reformed Doctrine
Book One
Holy Scripture to The State of Christ’s Humiliation
1. What is Christ’s state of exaltation?

a. The state of exaltation is the legal position in which Christ stood before God as innocent because as the head of his people Christ had satisfied for their sins and fully accomplished their righteousness.
2. What is the relationship between Christ’s humiliation and his exaltation?
a. Christ by his perfect obedience merited for himself and his people everlasting righteousness and highest glory.
3. What is the first aspect of Christ’s exaltation?
a. Christ rose from the dead on the third day and brought to light life and immortality.
4. What is the significance of the resurrection of Christ for our salvation?
a. Christ’s resurrection is the proof that we are justified at the cross, the power of our spiritual resurrection, and the pledge of our final resurrection.
5. What is Christ’s ascension?
a. Christ’s ascension is that wonder of grace in which Christ was bodily taken up from the earth and entered into the highest glory of heaven.
6. What does Christ do in heaven for us?
a. Christ prepares a place for his people, intercedes for them, and blesses them with all the blessings of salvation.
7. If Christ is in heaven, is he no longer with his church on earth?
a. In his ascension Christ became a quickening Spirit by whom he is always present with his church.

Lesson 1: The State of Christ’s Exaltation (1): His Resurrection and Ascension
1. What glory did God give to Christ in his ascension?
a. God set Christ at his own right hand and gave to Christ all authority in heaven and on earth.
2. What purpose does this authority of Christ serve?
a. Christ rules over all things in order that he might come again to perfect his eternal kingdom when God will be all in all.
3. What comfort is this power of Christ for us?
a. The comfort for us is that Christ appears in heaven as the head of his church, and by his power he defends and preserves us against all his and our enemies.
4. What does it mean that Christ will come again to judge the quick and the dead?
a. At his appointed time Christ will appear to express the judgment of God over all men, angels, and devils.
5. Is this coming of Christ for judgment a fearful thing?
a. It certainly is for all unbelievers and enemies of Christ, for he will cast them into everlasting condemnation.
6. Is Christ’s judgment a fearful thing for the elect?
a. Not at all, for we look for the one who loved us and gave himself for us and who will by his righteousness translate all his chosen ones into heavenly joys and glory.
7. What is the purpose of Christ’s judgment?
a. Christ will not judge to discover the eternal destines of men, but he will judge to demonstrate the righteousness of God in the salvation and damnation of men.

Lesson 2: The State of Christ’s Exaltation (2): Seated at God’s Right Hand and Coming as Judge
1. What is the purpose of the work of Christ as our mediator?
a. By Christ’s death he fulfilled all God’s covenant promises, and as the risen Lord he maintains and perfects God’s eternal covenant of grace.
2. What is God’s eternal covenant of grace?
a. The covenant is God’s gracious relationship of friendship and fellowship with Christ, the head of the covenant, and in him with his elect people, in which he is their God and they are his people.
3. What figures does scripture use for God’s covenant that show that the covenant is a relationship of friendship and fellowship?
a. Scripture reveals this when God calls Israel his son, refers to his church as his sons and daughters, speaks of Christ’s marriage to his people, and calls his covenant God’s tabernacle with men.
4. How do we understand the covenant in relationship to salvation?
a. The covenant is salvation itself, so that to be incorporated into God’s covenant is to be made a partaker of Christ, of his Spirit, of all his riches and gifts, and of every blessing of salvation.
5. How did God establish his covenant?
a. God established his covenant eternally in the counsel of election and by the wonder of grace when he incorporated his elect people into Christ.
6. Does God establish his covenant with all baptized children?
a. He does not, but God establishes his covenant with his elect in Christ in the line of continued generations.
7. Are there any conditions in God’s covenant?

a. There are no conditions in the covenant because God made the covenant by his promise, and he bestows all his promises by grace, so that there is nothing that man must do in the covenant for his salvation.

Lesson 3: The Covenant of Grace
1. What is the Reformed principle concerning the truth of salvation?
a. The Reformed confess that salvation is of the Lord, so that salvation is by grace and not by works.
2. What truth especially illustrates that salvation is of the Lord?
a. That salvation is of the Lord is illustrated by the truth of sovereign predestination with its two aspects of election and reprobation.
3. Define predestination.
a. Predestination is God’s eternal appointment without conditions of some in Christ to salvation by election and his appointment of others to damnation by reprobation.
4. What is the essence of the truth of gracious salvation?
a. The essence of gracious salvation is that the elect are in union with Christ.
5. What is union with Christ?
a. Union with Christ is the eternal connection that the elect have with Christ that manifests itself in their being engrafted into Christ by true faith through the operation of the Holy Spirit.
6. Is faith, then, to be considered as our union with Christ?
a. Yes, faith is the bond that unites us to Christ, whereby we become partakers of all his riches of salvation.
7. How do scripture and the creeds frequently speak of this union with Christ?
a. They refer to the elect as being made one with Christ or as being in Christ Jesus.

Lesson 4: Union with Christ
1. By whom does God make us partakers of Christ in his covenant?
a. God in Christ works through the Spirit of Christ, who dwells in Christ as the head and in his elect as the body of Christ.
2. Who is the Spirit of Christ?
a. Christ’s Spirit is the Holy Spirit, the third person of the Trinity, who is coequal and coeternal with the Father and the Son.
3. Why do we speak of the Spirit of Jesus Christ?
a. We speak thus because the Holy Spirit was given to Christ to be his Spirit to operate on Christ’s behalf in his church.
4. When did Christ receive the Spirit?
a. Christ received the Spirit at his ascension, and Christ poured the Spirit out on his church at Pentecost.
5. How does the Spirit of Christ accomplish our union with Christ?
a. The Spirit engrafts us into Christ, so that the Spirit personally is our union with Christ, and we become one spirit with Christ and with his entire body.
6. What does the Holy Spirit give to us in Christ?
a. The Holy Spirit gives to us Christ himself and all the riches of salvation that are in him.
7. How may we distinguish these riches of salvation in Christ?
a. We may distinguish regeneration, calling, faith, justification, sanctification, conversion, and glorification.

Lesson 5: The Work of the Holy Spirit
1. What is the first work of the Holy Spirit in the elect who are united to Christ?
a. The first work of the Spirit is the wonder of regeneration.
2. What is regeneration?
a. Regeneration is that wonder of grace whereby the Spirit renews the heart of the dead sinner by implanting in him the new life of Christ.
3. What is the fruit of regeneration?
a. The fruit of regeneration is that the sinner is a new creation, and he lives and can never die.
4. Who are the objects of this wonder of regeneration.
a. The elect children of God alone are the objects of this work.
5. Does scripture speak of regeneration in other ways?
a. Yes, scripture speaks of regeneration as being born again, being born from above, being a new creation, receiving a new heart, and as a resurrection from the dead.
6. What do all these terms indicate about regeneration?
a. These terms all indicate that regeneration is a wonderwork of God that is accomplished without any means.
7. What does is mean that regeneration takes place without means?
a. The meaning is that regeneration is worked by the Holy Spirit without the means of the preaching of the gospel.

Lesson 6: The Riches of Christ (1): Regeneration
1. What is the calling?
a. The calling is the wonder of grace whereby God addresses his people in the depths of their beings and calls them out of darkness and into God’s marvelous light?
2. What means does God use to accomplish the calling?
a. God calls his people by means of the pure preaching of the gospel.
3. What distinction must we make in the calling?
a. We must distinguish between the internal call and the external call.
4. What is the external call?
a. The external call is the proclamation by the church through her ordained ministry of the gospel of Jesus Christ and his finished work for God’s elect.
5. What is the internal call?
a. The internal call is the efficacious work of the Spirit whereby he works the truth of the gospel in the hearts of God’s elect.
6. What is the significance of the preaching of the gospel for the elect?
a. The preaching of the gospel is the means whereby God gathers his elect from all nations into the true church and works faith in their hearts.
7. What is the significance of the preaching of the gospel for the reprobate?
a. The preaching of the gospel to the reprobate is the means whereby God reveals the perversity of their hearts and hardens them in their unbelief and other sins.

Lesson 7: The Riches of Christ (2): Calling
1. What is faith?
a. Faith is the saving union between the elect sinner and Christ.
2. How does faith come to conscious expression in the hearts of the elect?
a. The conscious expression of faith is a certain spiritual knowledge and an assured confidence.
3. What is the believer’s certain spiritual knowledge of faith?
a. He knows that everything God has revealed in his word is true and that salvation in Christ is his possession.
4. What is the believer’s assured confidence of faith?
a. He is sure that the remission of sins, everlasting righteousness, and salvation are his by grace alone for the sake of Christ’s sacrifice alone.
5. How is faith worked in the hearts of the elect?
a. Faith is a free gift worked by the Holy Spirit in the hearts of the elect by means of the preaching of the gospel.
6. In whom does the Holy Spirit work this faith?
a. The Holy Spirit works faith in the hearts of God’s elect alone.
7. Are there imitations of true faith?
a. Yes, Christ describes these imitations in the parable of the sower as the stoney and thorny soils.

Lesson 8: The Riches of Christ (3): Faith
1. What is the chief benefit of saving faith for the elect child of God?
a. The chief benefit of faith is that as the ungodly sinner I am justified before God.
2. Why is justification chief of the riches of Christ?
a. Justification is chief because all of our salvation depends on our righteousness before God.
3. What is justification?
a. Justification is God’s gracious act to forgive the sins of his elect people and to impute to them the righteousness, holiness, and satisfaction of Jesus Christ.
4. When did God forgive his people?

a. God forgave his people eternally for Christ’s sake; and God manifested this in the cross, gave witness to this in the resurrection of Christ, and causes his people to know this by faith alone through the preaching of the gospel.
5. What is the meaning of justification by faith?
a. Justification by faith means that faith makes us members of Christ and thus partakers of his righteousness.
6. What is the meaning of justification by faith alone?
a. Justification by faith alone means that faith is the only instrument of justification and that justification is apart from any works.
7. What is the benefit of justification?
a. The justified believer has peace with God; is free from sin, the law, and death; and is an heir of eternal life as a child of God.

Lesson 9: The Riches of Christ (4): Justification
1. What is sanctification?

a. Sanctification is that wonder of grace whereby in union with Christ, who is our holiness, we are separated from the ungodly world and consecrated unto God in the love of the truth and in godliness.
2. What belongs to the wonderwork of sanctification?
a. Belonging to sanctification is God’s deliverance of the elect from the dominion and pollution of sin and transforming them according to the image of God in Christ Jesus.
3. How is sanctification related to justification?
a. Justification is the ground of sanctification, so the one is never present without the other.
4. How does God perform sanctification?
a. God works through the Spirit of Jesus Christ, who cleanses us from all sin and brings us near to God.
5. Is the believer’s sanctification perfect in this life?
a. The believer’s sanctification is perfect in his heart, for in the heart are the Spirit and perfect love for God, but in his flesh he remains totally depraved.
6. What is the consequence of this fact about sanctification for the believer’s life?
a. The consequence is a constant warfare between the Spirit and the believer’s flesh all his days.
7. What does sanctification mean for the antithesis?
a. Because the believer is consecrated to God, the believer is also separate from all that is common and profane, and he is called to hate and oppose God’s enemies.

Lesson 10: The Riches of Christ (5): Sanctification
1. What is the fruit of sanctification in the life of the believer?
a. The fruit of sanctification is God’s work of conversion.
2. What is conversion?
a. Conversion consists of two parts: the mortification of the old man and the quickening of the new man.
3. What is the mortification of the old man?
a. The mortification of the old man is the work of grace according to which the justified believer truly sorrows for his sins and more and more hates and flees his sins.
4. What is the quickening of the new man?

a. The quickening of the new man is the work of grace according to which the justified believer has a sincere joy in God as the God of his salvation and with love and delight lives according to the will of God in all good works.
5. What are good works?
a. Good works are the fruits of faith that are performed according to the law of God and out of gratitude to God for his deliverance of his people in Christ.
6. May the Christian rely on his works for his assurance?
a. Not at all, for then he would have no assurance, but his works are the fruits and demonstrations of his faith.
7. Which are the principal works of the converted believer?
a. Love for God, love for the neighbor, repentance for sins, and prayer.

Lesson 11: The Riches of Christ (6): Conversion
1. Can the Christian fall away from his faith?
a. This is impossible, for God’s is unchangeable, God’s election cannot be altered, God’s promise cannot fail, and God certainly preserves his people.
2. Can you prove that from scripture?

a. Yes. Romans 8:29–30 says, “For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate…whom he did predestinate, them he also called: and whom he called, them he also justified: and whom he justified, them he also glorified.”
3. What is the preservation of the elect?

a. Preservation is the wonder of God’s grace according to which God always renews the elect to faith, repentance, and good works; keeps them against all the power of sin and the devil until their deaths; and finally glorifies them in heaven.
4. What explains the preservation of the elect?

a. God is faithful to his promise; Christ never leaves the elect nor forsakes them; the Holy Spirit will not depart from them; and they can never— even by their great and many sins—lose the seed of regeneration or forfeit their state of justification.
5. What is the glorification of the Christian in this life?
a. The Christian’s glorification was accomplished in principle in the resurrection of Christ his head, and the Christian by his justification partakes of the glorious liberty of the sons of God.
6. What is the glorification of the Christian in the life to come?
a. At his death he is made perfect in his soul; in the final resurrection his body will be made like unto the glorious body of Jesus Christ.
7. Will not such a doctrine make men careless and profane?
a. It will not, but such assurance is the real source of the Christian’s perseverance and holy living.

Lesson 12: The Riches of Christ (7): Glorification
1. What is the church of Jesus Christ?
a. The church is the whole company of the predestinated chosen out of the whole human race and appointed to salvation in Christ.
2. What figures does scripture use to describe the elect church?
a. Scripture uses the figures of the bride of Christ, her husband; the body of Christ, her head; and the temple of Christ, the chief cornerstone.
3. How can the elect church be distinguished?
a. The elect church can be distinguished into the church militant on earth, the church triumphant in heaven, and the church latent yet to be born.
4. What are the perfections of the elect church?
a. The perfections of the elect church are one, holy, catholic, and apostolic.
5. What does it mean that the church is one?
a. One means that the church is united by the Spirit in perfect communion to Christ her head and with the other members of the body and that the church of both the old and new dispensations is one.
6. What does it mean that the church is holy?
a. Holy means that the church is consecrated to Christ her head by the Holy Spirit and that she is antithetically separated from all that is common and profane.
7. What does it mean that the church is catholic?
a. Catholic means that the church consists of elect from every tribe, tongue, and nation under heaven and that membership in the church is not limited by any earthly standard.
8. What does it mean that the church is apostolic?
a. Apostolic means that the church of all ages agrees in true faith and is founded upon the doctrine of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ being the chief cornerstone.

Lesson 13: The Elect Church of Christ
1. How does the elect body of Christ, the church, become manifest in the world?
a. The church is manifested in the gathering of believers and their seed in the instituted church.
2. How is the church gathered?
a. Christ gathers the church by his Spirit and Word through the preaching of the gospel.
3. How can the instituted church be distinguished?
a. The instituted church can be distinguished as visible and invisible.
4. What is the church visible?
a. The visible church is the body of Christ from the viewpoint of her ministry of the word, offices, membership, and confession.
5. What is the church invisible?
a. The invisible church is the body of Christ from the viewpoint of her communion with Christ by the Spirit and her enjoyment of all the blessings of salvation.
6. What are the marks that distinguish the true church from the false church?
a. The marks of the true church are the pure preaching of the gospel, the administration of the sacraments as Christ has commanded, and the exercise of Christian discipline against the impenitent.
7. What is the calling of the Christian in light of these marks?
a. The Christian is to forsake the false church and join himself to the true church wherever she manifests herself in the world.
8. What power is committed to the church?
a. Christ has committed to the church the power to teach, the power to discipline, and the power to show mercy.

Lesson 14: The Church Manifested in the Institute
1. How does Christ give the enjoyment of their salvation in Christ to the elect in the 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7. church?
a. Christ uses the means of grace.
Define the means of grace.
a. The means of grace are instruments ordained by God and used by the Holy Ghost to apply salvation to God’s elect people.
Which are the means of grace?
a. There are two means of grace: the preaching of the gospel and the sacraments instituted by Christ.
What is the preaching of the gospel?
a. The preaching of the gospel is the proclamation of Christ in the fullness and perfection of his person, natures, and works as the only way of salvation to all who believe on him.
What must accompany the preaching of the gospel?
a. There must also be the command to all men everywhere to repent and believe on Christ and the promise given that all who do believe and repent will surely be saved.
Is the preaching a means of grace to all who hear?
a. No, the preaching is a savor of life unto life to the elect and a savor of death unto death in those who perish.
How is the preaching a means of grace to the elect?

a. By means of the preaching, the Spirit of Christ works faith in the hearts of the elect, justifies them, brings to them the enjoyment of Christ and salvation in him, and causes them to grow daily in the grace and knowledge of the Lord Christ.

Lesson 15: The Means of Grace (1): The Preaching of the Gospel
1. What are sacraments?
a. Sacraments are holy, visible signs and seals of the covenant of grace instituted by God to accompany the preaching in order to strengthen the faith of God’s elect in the promise of the gospel.
2. How are the sacraments means of grace?
a. The sacraments are signs and seals of the righteousness of Christ Jesus.
3. How many sacraments did Christ institute?
a. Christ instituted two sacraments, holy baptism and the Lord’s supper.
4. What did Christ promise in the institution of these sacraments?
a. Christ promised to give to his elect spiritually what the sacraments signify outwardly.
5. What does holy baptism signify?
a. The sprinkling with water in baptism signifies the washing away of our sins through the blood of Christ and our incorporation into him and God’s covenant.
6. What does the Lord’s supper signify?

a. The broken bread and the poured-out wine of the Lord’s supper signify that Christ accomplished all our salvation. When we eat and drink this bread and wine, it signifies that we are more and more united to Christ’s body by his Spirit.
7. What do these sacraments seal?
a. The two sacraments seal that we are righteous before God by faith in Jesus Christ.
8. Are the sacraments grace to all who receive them?
a. They are not; the ungodly receive the sacraments to their condemnation; such as receive the sacraments by a true faith are strengthened by them.

Lesson 16: The Means of Grace (2): The Sacraments
1. Of what is baptism a seal?
a. Baptism seals to us that all our sins are washed away in the blood of Jesus Christ, so that we are righteous before God and heirs of eternal life.
2. Who must receive baptism?
a. Baptism must be administered to infant children of believers and to all who are converted in their adulthood and confess their faith in Christ.
3. Why must infants be baptized?
a. Infants must be baptized because they as well as adults are included in God’s covenant of grace, and Christ shed his blood and gives his Holy Spirit to infants as well as to adults.
4. How did God indicate this truth in the Old Testament?
a. God commanded Abraham to circumcise all the males in his household as a sign of the covenant.
5. Are all baptized children then elect and included in God’s covenant?
a. Certainly not, for the apostle wrote, “They are not all Israel that are of Israel.”
6. Which infants are comprehended in the covenant of grace?
a. The elect infants alone are members of God’s covenant.
7. What obligation follows from baptism?
a. In baptism God calls us to love him with all our hearts and to walk in new holy lives and never to despair of his mercy.

Lesson 17: Holy Baptism
1. What are the signs of the Lord’s supper?
a. There are two signs: the bread broken and the wine poured out, which signify the body of Christ broken and his blood shed to accomplish all our salvation.
2. How is Christ present in the Lord’s supper?
a. Christ is present by his Spirit and grace.
3. Do we eat Christ in the sacrament?
a. We surely do, for Christ said, “Except ye eat the flesh of the Son of man, and drink his blood, ye have no life in you.”
4. How do we eat Christ in the sacrament?
a. The manner of our partaking of Christ is by the Spirit through faith.
5. What does it mean to eat and to drink Christ in the Lord’s supper?

a. It means not only that we believe on Christ and have the pardon of our sins, but especially that we become more and more united to his sacred body by the Holy Ghost, so that we live and are governed by one Spirit.
6. To whom is the Lord’s supper administered?
a. The Lord’s supper is administered to those who by confession and walk reveal themselves to be true believers.
7. What is necessary before we come to the table?

a. Before coming to the table of the Lord’s supper, believers must rightly examine themselves whether they are sorry for their sins, they believe that their sins are forgiven for Christ’s sake, and they show their faith in true love toward their neighbors.

Lesson 18: The Lord’s Supper
1. What are the keys of the kingdom?
a. The keys of the kingdom are the preaching of the gospel and the exercise of Christian discipline.
2. What is the power of these keys?
a. The power is that by these keys the kingdom of heaven is opened to believers and shut to unbelievers and such as do not sincerely repent.
3. How is the preaching of the gospel a key to open the kingdom?
a. The preaching of the gospel is a key to open the kingdom when all who receive the promise of the gospel by a true faith have their sins truly forgiven them of God for the sake of Christ’s work.
4. How is the preaching of the gospel a key to shut the kingdom?

a. The preaching of the gospel is a key to shut the kingdom when God judges unbelievers according to the testimony of the gospel that such as do not believe stand exposed to the wrath of God and eternal condemnation so long as they are unconverted.
5. How is the kingdom shut by Christian discipline?

a. When the impenitent in a sinful life or false doctrine after being admonished do not repent, the church forbids them the use of the sacraments whereby they are excluded from the Christian church and by God from the kingdom of heaven.
6. How is the kingdom opened by Christian discipline?
a. When the penitent promise and show real amendment of life, they are received again as members of Christ and of his church.
7. Which is the chief key?
a. The key of preaching is chief, and Christian discipline serves to guard the pure preaching.

Lesson 19: The Keys of the Kingdom
1. What event will bring the end of all things?
a. The event that will bring the end of all things is the second coming of the Lord Jesus Christ on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.
2. What is the idea of the end of all things?
a. The end of all things is the goal that God eternally appointed in his counsel.
3. What is the goal of all things?

a. The goal of all things is the glory of God in the eternal kingdom of Jesus Christ, in which all his elect will live and reign with Jesus Christ world without end in the perfection of God’s eternal covenant.
4. How does scripture describe the time between Christ’s ascension and his second coming?
a. In Revelation 20:1–10 this time period is described as a millennium, or one thousand years.
5. What will characterize the millennium?
a. The world will increase in wickedness; the church will be persecuted; and at the end the kingdom of antichrist will rise.
6. Does not the church expect an earthly and glorious kingdom of Christ in this age?
a. She does not expect an earthly kingdom, for Christ said, “My kingdom is not of this world.”
7. What then is the church’s expectation for this age?
a. The church expects terrible opposition from Satan, persecution from the false church, false teachers to abound, and great apostasy, in light of which she prays, “Come, Lord Jesus. Come quickly.”

Lesson 20: The End of All Things and the Millennium
1. What is the intermediate state?
a. The intermediate state is the existence of the believer after physical death until his final resurrection.
2. Describe what happens to the believer in the intermediate state?
a. In his body the believer turns to dust in the grave, while in his soul he lives and reigns with Jesus Christ in heaven.
3. Can we also speak of the intermediate state of the unbeliever?
a. Yes, in his body the unbeliever rots in the grave, while in his soul he is cast in the eternal punishment of hell.
4. What is death?
a. Death has several aspects: spiritual death is total depravity; physical death is a rending of the soul from the body; and eternal death is hell.
5. What is death for the unbeliever?
a. Death for the unbeliever is the wages of sin.
6. If Christ died for us and paid our debt, so why must believers also die?
a. Death is not a punishment for sin for believers, but it is the dissolving of their earthly tabernacles, the dying off of their sinful flesh, and a passageway into everlasting glory.
7. What is the comfort of believers in their death?
a. At the moment of death, believers immediately will be with Christ in heaven.

Lesson 21: The Intermediate State
1. What are the precursory signs?
a. Precursory signs are public events in the realm of creation, the nations, and the church preceding the second coming of Christ, which indicate that he draws near and approaches quickly.
2. What is the relationship between the precursory signs and the coming of Christ?
a. The precursory signs are created by the coming of Christ, so that Christ comes with these signs.
3. For whom do the events serve as signs of Christ’s coming?
a. The signs are for the members of Christ’s church in order that the day of the Lord not come on them as a thief in the night.
4. What is the chief and controlling sign?
a. The chief and controlling sign is the worldwide preaching of the gospel.
5. Name some other signs.

a. In creation are natural disasters and earthquakes in divers places; among the nations are wars and rumors of wars, famines, and pestilence; in the church are false teachers and great apostasy; and finally the rise of antichrist, the great tribulation, Armageddon, and the sign of the Son of man in the heavens.
6. What is the sign of the Son of man in the heavens?
a. The sign of the Son of man is the final appearance of Christ in the heavens when every eye shall see him.
7. What purpose do these signs serve for the church?
a. The signs cause the members of the church to turn their eyes from the vanity of this world and to lift up their heads in hope of the coming of Christ.

Lesson 22: The Precursory Signs
1. What is the sign of apostasy?
a. The sign of apostasy is a forsaking of the truth by those who in their generations held to the truth.
2. Can God’s elect forsake the truth in this way?
a. Certainly not, for God preserves his elect over against all lies.
3. What explains apostasy?
a. The explanation of apostasy is reprobation: God did not give the reprobate the love of the truth, and he sends them a strong delusion that they believe a lie in order that they might be damned.
4. What purpose do reprobation and apostasy serve?
a. They serve the purpose of bringing about the rise of antichrist.
5. Who is antichrist?
a. Antichrist has three aspects: Antichrist is a religious power of opposition to the truth; a political power of opposition to the kingdom of Christ; and the personal antichrist, the man of sin.
6. What is the religious power of antichrist?
a. The religious power is the false church and at the end of the world the union of all false religions that will deceive the world to worship the antichrist.
7. What is the political power of antichrist?
a. The political power is the state throughout history as it is opposed to Christ’s kingdom and culminates at the end of the ages in the worldwide kingdom of antichrist.
8. Who is the personal antichrist?

a. The antichrist is that one man, the man of sin, the son of perdition, who will govern that final worldwide kingdom, persecute the church, and whom the Lord will destroy by the brightness of his coming.

Lesson 23: Apostasy and Antichrist
1. What is the second coming of Christ?
a. Christ’s second coming is his final, personal, and visible appearance from heaven to declare himself judge of the living and the dead.
2. Who will share in the glory of Christ’s coming?
a. Christ will come with his saints and with all his angels.
3. How will Christ’s coming be for the world?
a. Christ will come as a thief in the night.
4. Will Christ’s coming be so sudden for the elect church?
a. No, for this coming of Christ is preceded by clear signs that Christ taught the church to observe.
5. How do we understand Christ’s words “Behold, I come quickly”?
a. Christ comes quickly in light of the many events of God’s counsel that Christ must unfold before he comes.
6. What is the church’s view of Christ’s second coming?
a. His second coming is the object of the church’s hope, and she longs for it.
7. What is the church’s calling in light of the second coming of Christ?
a. She is to be sober, to watch, to pray, and to keep herself unspotted from the world.

Lesson 24: Christ’s Second Coming
1. What wonderwork will Christ perform first at his coming?
a. Christ will raise the dead.
2. What is the resurrection of the elect?
a. The resurrection of the elect is that wonder of grace whereby God in Christ will call them out of their graves and transform their bodies to be made like the glorious body of Jesus Christ.
3. Can we speak also of a resurrection of the reprobate?
a. Yes, it will be a resurrection to shame and everlasting contempt.
4. With what bodies will the wicked be raised?
a. The reprobate will be raised with bodies that are fitted for everlasting death in hell.
5. With what bodies will the elect be raised?
a. The elect will have their same bodies in which they lived on the earth, but their bodies will be fitted for everlasting life and glory in heaven.
6. What is the change that will happen to the bodies of the elect?
a. The mortal will put on immortality; the corruption will put on incorruption; the weak will be raised in power; and the natural will become spiritual.
7. What is the calling to Christians in light of the resurrection?
a. The calling is to be steadfast and immoveable in the truth and abound in the work of the Lord, knowing that our labors are not in vain.

Lesson 25: The Final Resurrection
1. Into whose judgment will the resurrected elect and reprobate be summoned?
a. They will be summoned before the tribunal of Jesus Christ and of the triune God.
2. How will the elect and reprobate appear in the judgment?
a. They will appear in their resurrected bodies, whether glorious or shameful.
3. What is the purpose of the final judgment?
a. The purpose of the final judgment is the revelation of the righteous judgment of God to his glory.
4. What will that revelation be?
a. That revelation will be the expression of God’s judgment of the moral, ethical value of men and their works.
5. What will be the standard of that righteous judgment?
a. The standard will be the holy gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ.
6. What is the eternal source of that righteous judgment?
a. The eternal source is the counsel of election and reprobation, in which God appointed men to their eternal ends.
7. How will works come up in this judgment?

a. Works will come up in the judgment in connection with the gospel, so that the works of the elect will appear in Jesus Christ as righteous, and the works of unbelievers will appear as wicked. The elect will receive their reward of the righteousness of Christ, and the wicked will receive the wages of sin.

Lesson 26: The Final Judgment
1. What will happen to the present form of the creation at the coming of Christ?
a. The creation will pass away with a great noise; the foundations of all things will be shaken; the elements will melt with a fervent heat; and the earth and all its works will be burned up.
2. What is the purpose of that total destruction of the present form of the creation?
a. God will make a new heaven and new earth, where righteousness and peace will dwell.
3. Why will the creation be glorified?
a. The creation will be glorified because the creation too was made subject to vanity in Adam in order that it also could participate in the glorious liberty of the children of God in Christ.
4. Can we speak more particularly about this new creation?

a. Yes, the new creation is the goal for which God made the creation. All the wicked will be cast into hell; the new creation will be the habitation of just men made perfect; in it there will be no possibility of sin and death; and it will continue world without end.
5. What explains the perfection, glory, and endurance of the new creation?
a. The new creation will have its unity and perfection in Jesus Christ, the new and everlasting head of all things; and creation will endure through his perfect righteousness.
6. What will be the blessedness of the new creation?
a. The blessedness of the new creation will be to dwell without sin in the glory of God’s everlasting covenant of grace.
7. Who will inhabit the new creation?

a. The inhabitants will be the elect church, gathered out of all nations, formed into one people, and symbolized in Revelation 21 as the new Jerusalem that descends out of heaven as a bride adorned for her husband.
